authors: Tian-Shun Chen, Hao Feng, Haozhe Wang, Kilar Zhang
abstract: Dedekind's problem counts monotone Boolean functions, equivalently downsets of a Boolean lattice. We recast this enumeration as a finite layer-ratio reconstruction problem for the Whitney numbers of the ranked ideal lattice. An exact adjacent-layer double count expresses each layer ratio through local averages of the number of addable elements and the number of removable elements. Reversible fixed-layer Markov chains estimate these averages and hence estimate the Dedekind number M(n). Backtests at M(8) and M(9) calibrate seed-level variability under the fixed protocol and measure the observed Monte Carlo budget scaling. The resulting estimate probes the Whitney-number sequence of the ideal lattice. Although these rows have previously been described empirically as unimodal, the high-precision n=9 estimate has a shallow two-shoulder feature around the central rank, contrary to that empirical description; n=11 and n=13 center-window estimates show a larger-contrast analogous pattern. The protocol estimate for M(10) is \[ \widehat M(10)=(8.9360\pm0.0010)\times 10^{78}, \] where the displayed uncertainty is the budget-based forecast scale from the cross-n scaling law under the production budget.

title: Certified spectral functions from lattice Monte Carlo data
authors: Sophie Mutzel, Antoine Tilloy
abstract: The Monte Carlo method, applied to lattice quantum field theory, gives access to Euclidean correlation functions with well-understood error bars. Recovering the observables one cares about, such as the spectral density, requires solving an ill-posed inverse problem, usually tackled with heuristics that lose rigorous control of the error. Instead of trying to find the ``best'' spectral density $ρ(ω)$, we ask how small or large linear functionals $\int_{\mathbb{R}^+} G(ω) ρ(ω) \mathrm{d} ω$ of it can be, given the Monte Carlo data and the reflection positivity of the lattice action. This is a convex but infinite-dimensional problem. We show how its dual can be rigorously relaxed into a hierarchy of finite semidefinite programs, solvable with standard solvers and enjoying strong convergence guarantees. The resulting bounds are rigorous even when the relaxation is not tight, and converge quickly to the regime where the error is entirely dominated by Monte Carlo statistics. The method also flags implausible Monte Carlo data, for instance underestimated error bars, through an infeasibility certificate. We demonstrate it on lattice $φ^4$ theory in two dimensions.

title: POTATR: A Lightweight Image-to-Graph Model for Page-Level Table Extraction
authors: Brandon Smock, Libin Liang, Max Sokolov, Amrit Ramesh, Valerie Faucon-Morin, Tayyibah Khanam, Maury Courtland
abstract: Large-scale document processing requires contextually aware table extraction (TE) that is both accurate and efficient. Yet current approaches require billions of parameters, hundreds of autoregressive steps, or costly API inference. Motivated by this, we introduce the Page-Object Table Transformer (POTATR), a lightweight 29M parameter image-to-graph model that extends the Table Transformer (TATR) for contextualized page-level TE. POTATR outperforms all models tested on the PubTables-v2 Single Pages benchmark -- including frontier MLLMs -- achieving $\textrm{GriTS}_\textrm{Con}$ of 0.964 while running over 130$\times$ faster at roughly 300$\times$ lower cost. Further, POTATR's output is spatially grounded: every recognized element has a bounding box, enabling visual verification and geometric text assignment. As a result, POTATR performs unified page-level TE while composing with other models, enabling extension to scanned documents via external OCR and to full-document TE via techniques like cross-page merging. Code and models will be released.

title: Quality-Diversity Search in Sound Generation: Investigating Innovation Engines for Audio Exploration
authors: Björn Þór Jónsson, Çağrı Erdem, Stefano Fasciani, Kyrre Glette
abstract: This study addresses the challenges composers and sound designers face in creating and refining tools to achieve their musical goals. Using evolutionary processes to promote diversity and foster serendipitous discoveries, we automate the search through uncharted sonic spaces for sound discovery, arguing that diversity-promoting algorithms can bridge the gap between the theoretical realisation and practical accessibility of sounds. We describe a system for generative sound synthesis combining Quality Diversity (QD) algorithms with a supervised discriminative model, inspired by the Innovation Engine algorithm, and explore different configurations and the interplay between the chosen synthesis approach and the discriminative model. We examine the interaction between Compositional Pattern Producing Networks (CPPNs) and Digital Signal Processing (DSP) graphs, introducing a novel approach that uses multiple specialised CPPNs for different frequency ranges; this yields simpler networks while maintaining performance comparable to single-CPPN setups. We also investigate evolutionary stepping stones by analysing goal switches between musical and non-musical contexts, revealing how lineages traverse unlikely paths to current elites. Expanding the behaviour space of a previous study to include various sound durations, we uncover specialisation within temporal niches. Results indicate that CPPN and DSP graphs coupled with a Multi-dimensional Archive of Phenotypic Elites (MAP-Elites) and a deep learning classifier can generate a substantial variety of synthetic sounds, diverse and innovative across temporal and contextual dimensions. We present the generated sound objects through an online explorer and as rendered sound files, and, in the context of music composition, an experimental application that showcases their creative potential across various durations and contexts.

title: AetheRock: An Arm-Worn Robot Teaching System for Force-Guided Vision-Tactile Learning
authors: Hong Li, Yue Xu, Yihan Tang, Yankang Dong, Chenyuan Liu, Chenyang Yu, Xuyang Li, Siyuan Huang, Yujun Shen, Nan Xue, Yong-Lu Li
abstract: Force and tactile sensing are indispensable in contact-rich manipulation. However, force-aware robot learning faces critical challenges due to the incompatible assembly of tactile and force sensors in handheld or wearable devices. To address these limitations, we first introduce AetheRock for gripper-force, vision, and tactile data collection, which is an arm-worn device featuring a modular and easily manufactured visuo-tactile sensor, GelSlim-MiniFab, at the fingertip, a resistive pressure sensor at the human finger contact region, a customized PCB module, and a wearable kit for comfortable and robust collection. Building on this, we propose ForceVT, a representation learning framework that uses force and vision to guide fidelity-agnostic tactile learning, enabling robust inference in any tactile situation. Real-world experiments show that AetheRock achieves qualified data efficiency and that ForceVT effectively alleviates inefficiencies when visuo-tactile sensors exhibit manufacturing and utilization inconsistencies. Overall, our work mitigates the limitations of gripper-force vision-tactile robot learning through innovative hardware design and algorithms.

title: SIGA: Self-Evolving Coding-Agent Adapters for Scientific Simulation
authors: Matthew Ho, Brian Liu, Jixuan Chen, Audrey Wang, Lianhui Qin
abstract: Advanced scientific simulators expose specialized input languages that turn simulation goals into executable configurations, but learning them can cost domain scientists hours to days. We study simulator setup as a problem of agent-tool interface grounding: what minimal simulator-specific adaptations are needed for an off-the-shelf coding agent to operate real scientific software? Our intuition is that coding agents already know how to navigate files, edit code, run commands, and repair outputs, but they lack the simulator's executable contract: its vocabulary, structural constraints, validation rules, and termination conditions. We introduce SIGA, a Simulator-Interface Grounding Adapter that supplies this contract through retrieval, procedural memory, in-trajectory validation, and validation-enforced termination. We primarily evaluate SIGA on GEOS, an open-source multiphysics simulator used in subsurface science. SIGA produces a complete GEOS deck in about five minutes with TreeSim above 0.90, matching an extended-budget human expert who took about three hours, a roughly 36x wall-clock speedup. On a harder held-out set, grounding raises TreeSim from 0.720 to 0.789, a roughly 10% relative gain over the bare agent, and can reduce the across-seed standard deviation by 16x. Self-evolution further improves SIGA by rewriting adapter contents from prior trajectories, yielding the highest held-out GEOS mean and matching or outperforming the strongest hand-designed configuration. Transfers to OpenFOAM and LAMMPS show that the dominant mechanism shifts by interface: validation matters most when structural completeness is the bottleneck, while memory and retrieval matter most when domain correctness is the bottleneck. These results suggest that lightweight, self-improvable grounding layers can turn general coding agents into practical operators of scientific software.

title: SemDINO: A DINOv3-Driven Network for Cross-Temporal Semantic Alignment in Change Detection
authors: Xinyu Tong, Meihua Zhou, Jinxiao Sun, Yingjie Tang, Lei Wang
abstract: Semantic change detection (SCD) aims to simultaneously locate land-cover changes and identify semantic categories before and after transition. However, existing methods suffer from insufficient cross-temporal alignment, weak multi-scale representation, and poor robustness to pseudo-changes caused by illumination, season, and registration noise. To address these issues, we propose a novel end-to-end semantic change detection network named SemDINO, which integrates a dual-branch encoder, multi-scale temporal interaction, semantic purification, change enhancement, and decoupled multi-task prediction into a unified framework. Specifically, we construct a dual-branch encoder that combines a CNN backbone and frozen DINOv3 features via gated pyramid fusion, enabling rich multi-scale semantic representation. Then, a multi-scale temporal bidirectional transformer interaction (M-TBTT) module is proposed to achieve global cross-temporal feature alignment and information interaction. To further enhance genuine changes and suppress pseudo-variations, we introduce semantic purification (SCP), bidirectional change enhancement (BiChangeEnhance), and multi-scale change enhancement (MCE) modules collaboratively. Finally, a multi-branch CD prediction head is designed to jointly output binary change mask, bi-temporal semantic maps, and edge constraint. Extensive experiments on public remote sensing CD datasets demonstrate that SemDINO achieves superior performance and generalization ability against state-of-the-art methods, especially in complex scenarios with interference factors.

title: Difference-Aware Retrieval Policies for Imitation Learning
authors: Quinn Pfeifer, Ethan Pronovost, Paarth Shah, Khimya Khetarpal, Siddhartha Srinivasa, Abhishek Gupta
abstract: Parametric imitation learning via behavior cloning can suffer from poor generalization to out-of-distribution states due to compounding errors during deployment. We show that reusing the training data during inference via a semi-parametric retrieval-based imitation learning approach can alleviate this challenge. We present Difference-Aware Retrieval Policies for Imitation Learning (DARP), a semi-parametric retrieval-based imitation learning approach that addresses this limitation by reparameterizing the imitation learning problem in terms of local neighborhood structure rather than direct state-to-action mappings. Instead of learning a global policy, DARP trains a model to predict actions based on $k$-nearest neighbors from expert demonstrations, their corresponding actions, and the relative distance vectors between neighbor states and query states. DARP requires no additional assumptions beyond those made for standard behavior cloning -- it does not require additional data collection, online expert feedback, or task-specific knowledge. We demonstrate consistent performance improvements of 15-46% over standard behavior cloning across diverse domains, including continuous control and robotic manipulation, and across different representations, including high-dimensional visual features. Code and demos are available at https://weirdlabuw.github.io/darp-site/.

title: Partial Pressure Contributions of Hadron Families to the QCD Equation of State
authors: Jonathan Gonzales, Alejandro Florez, Johannes Jahan, Angel R. Nava Acuna, Naman Mehndiratta, Claudia Ratti
abstract: Lattice simulations provide the thermodynamics of quantum chromodynamics (QCD) as a function of the temperature, at zero-to-moderate values of the baryonic chemical potential. However, the contribution of single hadronic species cannot be directly isolated from lattice calculations. In this work, we find linear combinations of up to fourth order susceptibilities which isolate the contribution of hadrons to the QCD pressure according to their baryon number $B$, electric charge $Q$ and strangeness $S$ content. These combinations are valid, provided that the thermodynamics of a strongly-interacting gas in the low-temperature regime can be modeled as a gas of non-interacting hadrons and their resonances. Finally, we test the validity of these linear combinations in the Hadron Resonance Gas (HRG) model and compare them to available lattice QCD results, using continuum-estimated susceptibilities.

title: Sambe Approach to Floquet-Lindblad Open Quantum Systems
authors: Andriani Keliri, Marco Schirò
abstract: We study driven and open quantum systems described by a time-periodic Lindblad master equation. In closed systems, the stroboscopic dynamics can always be described by an effective time-independent Floquet Hamiltonian; this idea is the basis of Floquet engineering. However, in the presence of dissipation, the existence of an effective time-independent Floquet Lindbladian is not guaranteed due to the non-unitary nature of the evolution. Using Floquet theory, we construct a well-defined time-independent Floquet Lindbladian in an extended Sambe-Liouville space, transforming the initial time-dependent problem to a static and non-Hermitian eigenvalue problem. For harmonic driving, we introduce a matrix continued fraction method to nonperturbatively resum multiphoton processes and construct an effective Floquet Lindbladian acting only on the physical Liouville space. Compared to other high-frequency expansions, this method has the advantage of providing the whole infinite series expansion at once. Using a resolvent formalism, we show how to obtain a spectral Floquet representation of correlation functions of an open quantum system. As an application, we consider a dissipating two-level system in a linearly polarized field and calculate its resonance fluorescence spectrum. Furthermore, we consider a parametrically driven quantum dot with pump and loss for which we calculate its spectral function and current-voltage characteristics.

title: NEXUS: Abundance, Environments, and Spectral Diversity of Little Red Dots from the NIRSpec MSA Sample
authors: Zhiwei Pan, Ming-Yang Zhuang, Yue Shen, Feige Wang, Jenny E. Greene, Adam J. Burgasser, Junyao Li, Zachary Stone, Padmavathi Venkatraman
abstract: We present a comprehensive study of Little Red Dots (LRDs) at 2.3 < z < 7.4 using NIRCam photometry and NIRSpec MSA/PRISM spectra from the ongoing NEXUS program. Photometric selection combining several commonly adopted methods yields a high completeness of about 85% for LRD selection over this redshift range and for a flux limit of F444W < 26. The overall purity is about 60%, with contamination from emission-line galaxies and normal active galactic nuclei (AGNs), as well as dwarf stars. Most (>90%) of the spectroscopically confirmed LRDs have robust broad-line detection. Our spectroscopic sample of 36 LRDs displays the full range of spectral diversity of LRDs. It includes objects with extreme Balmer breaks similar to the LRD "Cliff", as well as objects with moderately reddened rest-optical continua that can be fit with low-temperature blackbody components in the recent BH* model framework. The broad H$α$ emission is correlated with the continuum emission at 5100 Angstrom, suggesting common origins for these emission components; the narrow [O III] emission, however, is poorly correlated with the optical continuum. We do not find evidence of redshift evolution in these spectral properties. The space density of LRDs declines toward z about 2, opposite to the trend for normal AGNs, although low-luminosity LRDs at z about 2-4 may be more abundant than currently probed by ground-based searches. The clustering of LRDs suggests that they live in dark matter halos of several times $10^{11}\ h^{-1}$ solar masses, albeit with large uncertainties. Overall, these results are consistent with recent observations of LRDs and with the emerging picture of accreting SMBHs enshrouded in dense gas envelopes as the origin of LRDs.

title: Elastoinertial effects govern dynamic response of soft hair beds
authors: Jonas Smucker, Naiesa Freeman, Eric Caballero, Philip J. Morrison, José Alvarado
abstract: Fluid-immersed hair beds are ubiquitous in biology-from the endothelial glycocalyx and primary cilia to intestinal microvilli-where they serve as mechanosensors that transduce dynamic flow signals into biochemical regulatory responses. Despite the inherently dynamic nature of physiological flows, the dynamic mechanical properties of fluid-immersed hair beds under time-varying conditions remain poorly characterized. Here we investigate the transient rheological response of elastic hair beds to large-amplitude oscillatory shear flows at low to intermediate Reynolds number. While the hairs and fluid themselves obey linear constitutive laws, their coupled interaction produces a dynamic nonlinear response that depends sensitively on driving frequency and amplitude. We identify a crossover from a stress-lagging regime to a stress-leading regime, which is governed by an interplay between fluid viscosity, fluid inertia, and hair elasticity. A simplified rigid-beam model qualitatively captures the crossover behavior. Characterizing the dynamic flow response of soft hair beds has direct biological implications, since the lag time sensitively determines the stability of mechanosensory signaling in the feedback loops underlying essential biological processes such as vasodilation, ciliary remodeling, and tubular reabsorption. Our results establish a framework for understanding how the physical properties of biological hair beds optimize dynamic information transmission during mechanotransduction.

title: Degenerate Diffusions on Continuum Percolation and Hamilton-Jacobi-Bellman Equations
authors: Rodrigo Bazaes, Alexander Mielke, Chiranjib Mukherjee
abstract: We study degenerate controlled diffusions and Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equations posed on genuine continuum percolation clusters. The diffusion is constrained to evolve inside the infinite cluster and degenerates according to the distance to the irregular random boundary. Under suitable regularity and degeneracy assumptions on the diffusion matrix, we prove that the corresponding controlled diffusion never reaches the boundary and therefore admits a unique global strong solution. Using this result, we establish a stochastic control representation formula for viscosity solutions of degenerate Hamilton--Jacobi--Bellman equations posed on the random cluster, without imposing boundary conditions. We further verify that the structural assumptions introduced in this work, including quantitative integrability properties of the distance-to-the-boundary function and the associated degeneracy, hold for concrete continuum percolation models such as the Boolean model. In particular, although the diffusion never reaches the boundary, the boundary geometry remains a fundamental ingredient of the theory through the admissible degeneracy regime. The results establish a quenched framework for stochastic control and degenerate partial differential equations on genuine continuum percolation geometries, and identify a link between the analytic structure of the diffusion and the stochastic geometry of the underlying cluster. They also provide the foundational framework for the homogenization theory developed in the companion article [BMM26].

title: Codifferential Calculi on Quantum Homogeneous Spaces
authors: Julius Benner
abstract: We develop the theory of first- and higher-order codifferential calculi over coalgebras $C$ over fields $k$ with characteristic $\mathrm{char}(k)\neq 2$. For a given first-order codifferential calculus, we introduce its maximal prolongation by means of an explicit construction that associates to it a differential graded coalgebra, satisfying a universal property. For module coalgebras over a Hopf algebra $U$, we introduce the notion of an equivariant codifferential calculus. If $C$ is of the form $U\otimes_H k$ for a Hopf algebra $U$ and a right coideal subalgebra $H$ such that $U$ is faithfully flat as a left- and right $H$-module, we show that equivariant first-order codifferential calculi correspond to certain right coideals $T\subseteq \ker(\varepsilon\colon C\rightarrow k)$ called quantum tangent spaces. If $H$ is a sub bialgebra and the right $C$-coaction on $T$ is trivial, then the maximal prolongation is described in terms of a quadratic coalgebra. We further relate codifferential calculi to differential calculi and Cartan pairs over the dual algebra $C^\ast$, or more generally subalgebras thereof. We explicitly compute codifferential calculi on the coalgebra pre duals of the Podleś sphere and the quantized projective spaces. As an application, we give a new proof that the antiholomorphic Heckenberger--Kolb calculi on quantized projective spaces have classical dimension.

title: Exact Four-Parameter Rotating NS--NS Vacuum in Double Field Theory
authors: Hun Jang, Minkyoo Kim, Jeong-Hyuck Park
abstract: We construct an exact rotating vacuum of the NS--NS sector, equivalently a Double Field Theory vacuum. The construction applies compact $\mathbf{SO}(2)$ S-duality to a rotating Einstein--scalar seed. The solution has four independent parameters $\{\mathfrak{m},j,\mathfrak{q},ζ\}$. To our knowledge, this is the first explicit rotating solution of the pure NS--NS vacuum equations with all three NS--NS fields $\{g,B,φ\}$ determined analytically, independent dilaton and $H$-flux charges, and no Maxwell sector. The static limit is obtained by taking the rotation parameter $j\to 0$. In this limit the geometry is not the spherical Burgess--Myers--Quevedo solution. Instead, it is an axial Zipoy--Voorhees branch carrying $H$-flux, so an oblate deformation remains after rotation is switched off. This geometric memory is absent in pure general relativity and in Einstein--Maxwell--dilaton--axion. The two static branches nevertheless share the same $\ell=0$ parametrized post-Newtonian data $\{MG,β_{\mathrm{PPN}},γ_{\mathrm{PPN}},h\}$. They give two inequivalent NS--NS geometries at identical monopole charges, with the degeneracy lifted at $\ell=2$. At the Kerr horizon locus the outer shell is generically singular in curvature. Above the threshold $|\mathfrak{q}|>\sqrt{\mathfrak{m}^{2}-j^{2}}$, polar geodesics are repelled outward and the rotation axis becomes regular in curvature at the shell. On that axis the inverse metric $g^{μν}$ stays finite while the lower-index Riemannian metric components diverge, whereas off the axis the inverse metric itself diverges. This axis-local degeneracy may offer a setting for non-Riemannian geometry in Double Field Theory, where $g_{μν}$ is not fundamental and the $\mathbf{O}(D,D)$ variables $\{d,\mathcal{H}_{AB}\}$ remain well defined.

title: An Intelligent Assessment of Machine Vision Courses Based on HA-GNN
authors: Henan Hu, Haonan Chen, Tiantian Liang
publish: Proceedings of the 2026 International Conference on Generative Artificial Intelligence and Education
abstract: None

title: CityPulse 360 – AI-Powered Real-Time Urban Happiness and Sentiment Analysis
authors: Sai Charan Naidu P, J Naveen Krishna, A Akash, J Vijayarani
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: The rapidly growing urbanization in India has introduced a demand of intelligent tools that will be used to monitor the well-being of the city in real time. Traditional urban performance indicators utilize stagnant or lagging data and can be unable to reflect the present public feeling. CityPulse 360 brings in an AI-based system merging social media mood, environmental factors, price-of-living information, and infrastructure indicators and produce a real-time mood of happiness in Indian cities. The system uses Natural Language Processing (NLP) and Machine Learning (ML) to process live streams in the format of weather and air-quality data provided by OpenWeatherMap API, sentiment-based data provided by Twitter, Reddit, and YouTube, and structured data in the form of safety, healthcare, affordability. These multimodal inputs are aggregated into composite scores and represented with the help of interactive Power BI dashboard. In this paper, the architecture of the system, data acquisition, preprocessing, feature engineering, and dashboard design will be presented to monitor the urban happiness. The framework provides assistance to policy makers, researchers and citizens by providing an ever-present and multi-dimensional picture of urban well-being.

title: Deepfake Video Forensics Using ResNeXt–LSTM Visual Modeling and DSP-Enhanced Audio Spectrogram Analysis
authors: Tanish Lal O, Sijo Devassy V J, P. Deepa
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: The technology for creating deepfakes has advanced rapidly, and this allows for realistic modifications to both audio and video. Many current forensic systems only use frame-based visual artefacts, while modern deepfake pipelines use temporal smoothing, high-quality generative models, and synthetic voice, which makes unimodal detectors less dependable. This research introduces a multimodal deepfake detection framework that integrates a ResNeXt50 video feature extractor, an Long Short Term Memory (LSTM)-based temporal analysis module, and a Digital Signal Processing (DSP)-enhanced audio spectrogram classifier. Visual characteristics find differences in faces, temporal modelling finds differences between frames, and the audio stream finds problems in synthetic speech, like odd harmonic patterns and smoothed spectrum energy. A fusion module takes predictions from all branches and merges them to give a single authenticity score. Experiments show that using both DSP-based audio cues and temporal modelling makes things far more stable than only using video. The system is set up using a Django web interface that is built for automatic real-time deepfake verification.

title: A Dual-Level Data-Driven Framework for Reducing Financial Fraud in Banking
authors: Nusrat Jahan, Sheikh Razia Sultana, Anika Sayeema, Sabiha Firdaus, Jannatul Maua, Hashibul Ahsan Shoaib
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Pervasive Computing and Social Networking (ICPCSN)
abstract: Financial fraud detection remains a major challenge in modern banking due to evolving adversarial manipulation, data imbalance, and the narrow focus of conventional detection strategies. This paper introduces a dual-level data-driven framework that combines account-level behavioral risk modeling with transaction-level anomaly detection to enable more accurate and context-aware fraud identification. The proposed system achieves an ROC-AUC of 0.995 and AUPRC of 0.907 under the final combined inference model, surpassing traditional classifiers such as Logistic Regression (AUPRC 0.716) and even deep MLP models (AUPRC 0.843). Under stringent operating requirements of 90% precision, the proposed model maintains a recall of 0.611 compared to 0.519 for deep MLP and 0.381 for Logistic Regression. Additionally, the fairness-aware account model reduces demographic prediction disparity from 0.178 to 0.081. These results, validated on both the Credit Card Fraud dataset and the BAF account dataset, demonstrate that multi-scale risk reasoning substantially improves operational fraud detection performance and provides practical benefit for real-world banking applications and fraud monitoring workflows.

title: Performance Comparison of Radiative Transfer Simulation Tools Based on Land Surface Radiance in Beihang Green Garden
authors: Yunda Yang, Huaiwen Yang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: To evaluate the performance of radiation transfer simulation tools in ground object radiance modeling, this study conducted research at Beijing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics' Lvyuan campus, selecting typical ground objects including vegetation, water bodies, soil, and buildings. Radiance data were extracted from Gaofen-1 satellite imagery and ASTGM TM V003 elevation data, with simulation scenarios constructed using three projection methods: fisheye camera, parallel projection camera, and perspective camera. Three mainstream radiation transfer simulation tools—LESS, DART, and ERADIATE—were employed for radiance modeling. A quantitative evaluation system was established, including absolute radiance error, relative error, root mean square error (RMSE), and coefficient of determination, combined with qualitative dimensions such as morphological fidelity and scene adaptability to systematically compare simulation accuracy under various solar geometric conditions. Results demonstrated that the LESS model consistently outperformed others across all observation conditions, maintaining a stable coefficient of determination above 0.99 and achieving the lowest RMSE across all experimental groups, showcasing superior numerical stability and morphological fidelity. Leveraging ray tracing algorithms, LESS demonstrated significant advantages in complex 3D scene simulations (vegetation-water-body-building mixtures), particularly excelling in fine-grained vegetation canopy structure representation, making it an ideal tool for scientific quantitative remote sensing inversion. The DART model exhibited balanced performance with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$</tex> generally above 0.95 and RMSE slightly higher than LESS but still within acceptable ranges. DART demonstrates robust performance in multiscale simulations (from individual blades to landscape scales) and multi-angle radiative transfer analysis, particularly exhibiting strong adaptability when handling heterogeneous surfaces, making it suitable for engineering simulation validation and parameter inversion applications. The ERADIATE model shows significant uncertainty in complex projection conditions, with RMSE values notably higher than the other two models. While its high-precision radiative transfer algorithm performs well in idealized simple scenarios, simulation accuracy and stability are relatively limited in highly heterogeneous urban environments like Beihang Green Park due to constraints in scene modeling flexibility and computational resource availability. However, ERADIATE demonstrates lower coefficient of variation under weak signal conditions (e.g., cloudy days or water bodies), indicating its advantages under low-energy input scenarios. This study provides quantitative evidence for selecting and optimizing radiation transfer simulation tools in complex surface environments, while establishing methodological foundations for precision evaluation and parameter calibration of simulation tools in quantitative remote sensing applications.

title: Dynamic Data-Driven Framework for Forecasting Employment Trends in the AI Era
authors: Mayank Singh Negi, Vansh Raheja, Ketan Kumar, Meena Chaudhary, Gunjan Chandwani, Narender Gautam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI) is reshaping global labor markets by automating repetitive tasks while creating new opportunities in data-driven and technology-intensive sectors. This paper presents a Dynamic Data-Driven Employment Forecasting Model (DDEFM) that predicts employment trends using a hybrid machine learning framework integrating time-series forecasting, natural language processing, and sectoral analysis. The model combines real-world labor datasets, AI adoption metrics, and investment indicators to evaluate employment volatility across sectors. The research contributes a technical methodology to forecast job creation and decline across industries such as healthcare, IT, education, and green energy. Experimental results demonstrate the model’s ability to reduce prediction error by 12–18% compared to baseline statistical models. This study provides a scalable framework for policymakers and industries to plan workforce development in the AI era.

title: Harnessing Large Language Models for Next-Generation Artificial Intelligence Applications
authors: Navin Garg, Sasikanth Yangalasetty, Tharakesavulu Vangalapat, Mina Kumari, Mahesh Basavaraj, Manish Gupta
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) usage is the innovation capable of introducing the new generation of the artificial intelligence system with adaptive thinking, multimodal understanding, and grounded knowledge synthesis. Transformer architecture can be developed; less supervised scalable cognitive automation can be enabled by combining developed augmented retrieval-based generation and parameter-efficient tuning. This paper presents a technically valid and conceptually coherent hybrid-grounded LLM model that has cross-modal embedding alignment, external-knowledge recall, symbolical equivalent validation, and, the hallucination-controlled decoding. The methodology guarantees the semantic integrity and logic-sensitive decision-support in the different fields. The experimental evaluation of a group of complex reasoning demonstrates a considerable improvement as the number of perplexities was decreased to 7.1 instead of 16.8, the level of overall accuracy was increased by 63.4 to 83.8 percent, and the level of F1-score improved by 65.1 to 85.4. Generative metrics also show a significant improvement as BLEU scores 36.9, ROUGE-L scores 56.4 and exact match scores also goes to 63.2 and expectation calibration error also goes down to 3.6 and hallucination error also drops to 5.1. These findings indicate that grounded LLM design is an experimental catalyst of the future AI ecosystems with central interests on reliability, knowledge validity and high-fidelity reason.

title: An AI-Driven Optimization Framework for Sustainable E-Waste Management and Resource Utilization
authors: Priyanka Chauhan, Deepak Kumar Adhana, Mayank Saxena
publish: 2026 2nd International Conference on Big Data &amp; Machine Learning (ICBDML)
abstract: In the given paper, an AI-based optimization model of sustainable e-waste management and resource use is presented. The research falls in the fields of circular economy engineering, intelligent decision analytics, and intelligent urban sustainability systems. The framework creates a spatio-temporal digital twin of e-waste flows, which incorporates graph neural prediction of waste generation, deep reinforcement learning prediction of adaptive collection and routing, and multi-objective evolutionary optimization prediction of processing and material recovery. Lifecycle sustainability assessment is incorporated to pursue, all at the same time, cost, environmental impact, and circularity goals. Experimental assessment on the city scale shows that there are very high increments in performances relative to traditional methods that depend on rules to use in collection, logistics systems relying on optimization and, hybrid AI logistics systems. The efficiency of the proposed framework is 93% material recovery efficiency, 23.1% reduction on greenhouse gases, 25.6% operational costs efficiency and 27.4% reduction on waste which is not controlled. Further measurements ensure better routing effectiveness, increased service completion, greater prediction performance, increased processing throughput, greater circularity gain, and greater stability of policy compliance. These findings prove the potential of the framework to convert e-waste ecosystems into smart, resilient, and sustainability-oriented resource infrastructures of the contemporary smart cities.

title: Research on Construction and Virtual Commissioning of Intelligent Warehousing System Based on PQFactory Platform
authors: Runsheng Hao, Guohao Feng
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Digital twin virtual simulation technology plays a significant role in the virtual commissioning of intelligent production lines. Based on the PQFactory platform, this paper conducts virtual commissioning research on the intelligent warehousing system design. A digital model of the warehousing system is designed, a digital twin scenario is built on the PQFactory platform, and relevant parameters are configured according to the process flow. The robot program and PLC control program are compiled, and the KingIOServer gateway is used to realize communication between the physical PLC and the PQFactory platform, completing the integrated digital twin commissioning of the entire system. The virtual simulation verifies the accuracy and reliability of the intelligent warehousing system design, which helps improve the design and commissioning efficiency and reduce the development cycle and cost.

title: New approach to improve low-noise amplifier performances for 2.45 GHz wireless applications
authors: Moustapha Séré, Jamal Zbitou
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This article describes a new approach to improving the performance of low-noise amplifiers (LNA) operating at 2.45 GHz. It focuses on the use of novel circuit topologies, advanced techniques to reduce the noise figure (NF), and simultaneous gain enhancement. Such improvement is made possible by advancements in CMOS technologies, such as gallium arsenide (GaAs), and the careful selection of impedance matching network components. The GaAs-based ATF-21170 transistor is used as the active element in the proposed LNA. This LNA operates in class $\mathbf{A B}$ and is designed using the Advanced Design System (ADS) software. Simulations yield a gain of 15.918 dB and a noise figure of $\mathbf{1. 1 4 3 ~ d B}$. The input (S11) and output (S22) reflection coefficients are -20.136 dB and -15.205 dB, respectively. The implementation layout measures $\mathbf{4 0} \times \mathbf{8 3. 5} \mathbf{~ m m}^{\mathbf{2}}$. The final results demonstrate enhanced performance for receivers used in wireless communications, such as radar systems, Wi-Fi, and satellite communications…

title: FDSCBAM-YOLO: Integrating Frequency-Domain Attention with Depthwise Separable Convolution for Data-Scarce SAR Vehicle Detection
authors: Dan Gao, Shuo Yu, Caoyi Mei, Xiaofang Wu, Zhijin Wen
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: The intelligent advancement of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) Automatic Target Recognition (ATR) is frequently hindered by the scarcity of high-quality annotated data. To address this fundamental challenge, we propose FDSCBAM-YOLO, a lightweight detection network incorporating a novel Frequency-domain Depthwise Separable Convolutional Attention Module (FDSCBAM). By synergistically integrating depthwise separable convolutions with both frequency-domain and spatial attention mechanisms, FDSCBAM effectively suppresses inherent coherent speckle noise and accentuates target scattering features in the frequency domain without compromising computational efficiency. Furthermore, we introduce a frequency-domain enhanced loss function specifically tailored to improve small target detection. Extensive experiments conducted under data-scarce conditions demonstrate that the proposed method achieves superior detection accuracy while maintaining low model complexity.

title: PSO-Based Optimization of a CMOS Switched-Capacitance Current-Starved Ring VCO in 0.18-μm Technology
authors: Elhafidi Sanae, Aymen Hal-Houssine, Maaroufi Ismail, El Beqal Asmae, Benhala Bachir
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This paper presents the design and optimization of a switched-capacitance current-starved ring voltage-controlled oscillator (VCO). The proposed architecture relies on a current-starved topology combined with switched MOS capacitance tuning. The circuit is implemented in TSMC 0.18 μm CMOS technology. It operates at a center oscillation frequency of 1.5 GHz under a 1.8 V supply voltage. The main performance metrics considered are phase noise and power consumption. Design trade-offs inherent to multi-stage ring VCOs are thoroughly investigated. Particle Swarm Optimization (PSO) is adopted and applied as a metaheuristic optimization strategy to identify optimal transistor sizing and tuning parameters. The optimization process targets low phase noise and reduced power dissipation. The complete VCO is designed and simulated using the Cadence design environment. Simulation results confirm the effectiveness of the proposed methodology. The optimized VCO achieves a phase noise of –80 dBc/Hz at a 1 MHz offset. The total power consumption is limited to 15.8 mW. These results demonstrate the suitability of PSO for highperformance CMOS Current-Starved ring VCO design.

title: Dual-Path Motion Encoding for Robust Violence Detection
authors: Abderrahim Ouza, Mohamed El Ghmary, Ali Choukri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: In this paper we tackle the problem of violent event detection in realistic crowd scenes, which combine small scale individual actions with large scale group movement. We propose a dual-path motion encoding framework that jointly models fine-grained human micro-motions and global crowd dynamics. The local branch captures spatiotemporal human-centric features using 3D convolutions, while the global branch models scene-level temporal evolution through a bidirectional recurrent encoder. A stable training strategy combining warm-up scheduling and adaptive learning rate reduction ensures reliable convergence. Experimental results on the Real-Life Violence Situations dataset demonstrate that the proposed approach outperforms CNN, LSTM, and 3D CNN baselines in terms of accuracy and F1score. Visualization analysis confirms that the two branches learn complementary motion patterns. The method is suitable for realtime surveillance applications.

title: IAC-TecMed Database for Infrared Thermography-Based Diabetic Foot Monitoring
authors: Badiaa Ait Ahmed, Pilar Montañés-Rodríguez, Otman Aghzout, Juan Ruiz-Alzola
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This publication presents a publicly available subset of a database created to assess diabetic foot anomalies using infrared thermography, aimed at enhancing early detection of complications in diabetic patients. The initial study involved 22 healthy subjects (9 females and 13 males) to develop a standard morphological model for evaluating temperature asymmetry in the plantar aspects of both feet. The database comprises thermographic and RGB-D images captured at four time points (T0, T5, T10, T15) under controlled conditions (temperature: $\mathbf{2 5}^{\boldsymbol{\circ}} \mathbf{C}$, constant luminosity, and humidity), resulting in a longitudinal dataset that reflects natural thermal variations. This methodology is clinically relevant as it identifies thermal anomalies that may prevent complications in diabetic foot. Images were subjected to segmentation masks, manually delineated by three experienced researchers, and validated via the STAPLE algorithm to establish a ground truth. Statistical analyses utilized Wilcoxon’s matched-pairs test, with adjustments for multiple comparisons via Hochberg and false discovery rate methods, maintaining a significance level of 5%. These analyses revealed significant temperature variation areas, reinforcing the validity of the results. From the cohort, 19 participants (7 females and 12 males) consented to share their data for research. This subdataset (IAC-TecMed database, hereafter IAC-TMBD) is a valuable resource for exploring thermal patterns and their implications in foot disorder monitoring. This subset retains the integrity and scope of the original data, providing a resource for further investigation into temperature patterns and their implications for early detection and monitoring of foot disorders.

title: Language Supervised Multi-Camera Multi-Object Tracking
authors: Kaige Mao, Xiaopeng Hong, Xiaopeng Fan, Wangmeng Zuo
abstract: Recent multi-camera multi-object tracking (MC-MOT) algorithms are primarily trained using per-detection identity annotations, which are complicated to obtain. In contrast, labeling a language description per-object is a more natural and human-friendly way. In this paper, we explore MCMOT in a language-supervised manner (LS-MCMOT) and propose a novel approach LaVST, which performs language-to-vision weakly-supervised learning based on reliable pseudo-labels generated via tracklet-level cross-modality matching. In addition, we design an ID-aware projection self-correction mechanism to correct inaccurate image-to-ground projection in a self-supervised manner. The models trained with our approach exhibit promising performance in LS-MCMOT. Surprisingly, they perform favorably against state-of-the-art identity-supervised methods, especially in cross-dataset evaluation (with an average gain by 20.0% in IDF1), underscoring the potential of language annotations in MCMOT. Codes and language annotations will be available here.

title: Intuitive Tele-Operated Robotic Catheter System with Wrist-based Controller
authors: Satoshi Miura, Motoi Hamashima, Victor Parque, Tomoyuki Miyashita
publish: IEEE Transactions on Medical Robotics and Bionics
abstract: Catheter ablation realizes radical surgery for arrhythmia. However, it is too challenging to manipulate the catheter accurately because the catheter’s motion is quite different from human hands due to the flexible and non-haptic structure. This study presents a novel tele-operated robotic catheter system for using human wrist embodiment. We developed the wrist-based controller, and the catheter advancement robot adapting to most catheters. The catheter’s tip moves according to the motion and twist of the operator’s hand at each direction due to the robot system. We conducted two experiments as follows: First, the motion of the catheter tip was measured by three-dimensional magnetic positioning sensor. Second, five participants conducted the experiment to insert the catheter into the vessel and heart model each six times randomly by both manual hand and tele-operated system, respectively. As results, the master’s input matches the catheter’s motion with high precision in each direction, respectively (R2>0.99). Furthermore, the contact force decreased significantly (p=0.02), the achievement time decreased 45.7%, and the oxy-Hb in parietal area activated 54.8% higher in tele-operated system than manual by fNIRS. It was concluded that the tele-operated system could perform precise, quick, and intuitive operation.

title: Rotor Electromagnetic Structure Optimization for Improving the Efficiency of Permanent-Magnet Synchronous Motors in New Energy Vehicles
authors: Hucheng Chen, Peiyuan Ma, Yunchong Wang, Bensheng Xu, Hui Jing, Guo Ma, Weizhong Mo
publish: IEEE Open Journal of Vehicular Technology
abstract: Rotor electromagnetic structure optimization (RESO) has emerged as a key approach for improving the efficiency of permanent-magnet synchronous motors (PMSMs), resulting in a substantial volume of published studies. This paper systematically reviews the major RESO strategies targeting efficiency improvement. The rotor electromagnetic-structure characteristics and efficiency evaluation indicators are identified. Then, according to the different optimization mechanisms of the rotor structure, RESO techniques are classified into several classic categories. The most popular optimization methods are summarized in terms of their mechanisms, characteristics, and complexities. Finally, current research limitations and future directions are highlighted. This review indicates that RESO is an important pathway for improving efficiency, but its effectiveness depends on motor topology, operating conditions, performance requirements, manufacturing feasibility, and cost constraints. It is expected that this review will provide useful guidelines for designers and researchers seeking to enhance PMSM efficiency and offer insightful prospects, indicating where extra efforts are needed.

title: Information Control in Networked Multi-User Discrete-Event Systems Using State Estimates
authors: Fei Wang, Feng Lin, Jun Chen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper investigates the problem of information control in networked multi-user systems, where agents such as robots, sensors, and software entities interact via a communication network to achieve individual or shared goals. Information control involves deciding which state estimates to share or broadcast, balancing cooperation among friends and privacy from adversaries. Since each user has only partial knowledge of the system, efficient protocols for sharing relevant data to balance privacy, security, and transparency is needed. This study models multi-user systems as discrete-event systems where agents need to distinguish certain state pairs in order to perform their tasks. We systematically study and solve critical problems to address the key aspects of information control: determining the necessity of shared information, minimizing communication for security, and maximizing public information release when required. A framework that addresses private communications, public broadcasting, and adversarial dynamics, offering strategies to meet both security and transparency requirements is introduced. Solutions and algorithms are proposed to solve these problems.

title: Design and Evaluation of an Educational Virtual Agent Based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation for the Entrepreneurship Culture Course in Higher Education_supp1-3700935.pdf
authors: Isabel Guerrero Garcia
abstract: The digital transformation of higher education has driven the incorporation of intelligent systems aimed at academic support and competency development, particularly in courses related to entrepreneurial training. In this context, this article aims to design and evaluate a prototype of an AI-based educational virtual agent built upon a Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) architecture, intended to support the learning process in the Entrepreneurial Culture course. The prototype was designed and developed using an Agile methodology based on SCRUM, enabling an iterative and incremental construction process centered on the pedagogical and technological requirements of the institutional context. The virtual agent was implemented in a web environment and integrates semantic retrieval mechanisms, natural language generation, and contextual knowledge management to provide personalized guidance in business plan development and academic inquiry resolution. To evaluate the prototype’s quality, black-box functional testing and the System Usability Scale (SUS) were applied in accordance with the criteria established in the ISO/IEC 25010 standard. This analysis enabled assessment of the system’s performance in terms of usability, functionality, efficiency, and reliability from the end-user perspective. The results indicate an average SUS score of 72.96, reflecting an acceptable level of usability according to international software quality standards for educational systems.

title: PCPSI: Efficient Unbalanced Private Set Intersection Using Homomorphic Encryption with Plaintext–Ciphertext Multiplication
authors: Dongju Lee, Sungyeon Lee, Youyeon Joo, Kevin Nam, Yunheung Paek
abstract: Private Set Intersection (PSI) allows two parties to compute the intersection of their datasets without revealing anything beyond the result. Many real-world applications arise in unbalanced settings, where a small client set is compared against a much larger server database. Homomorphic encryption (HE)–based PSI protocols provide strong privacy and a non-interactive workflow, but existing designs rely on compressing comparison results into a single ciphertext, requiring costly ciphertext–ciphertext multiplications. As the server dataset grows, this leads to deeper circuits, larger parameters, and substantial overhead. We present PCPSI, a new HE-based PSI protocol that replaces product-based compression with a depth-1 plaintext–ciphertext comparison strategy, eliminating ciphertext–ciphertext multiplications altogether. This shallow circuit scales efficiently without bootstrapping and enables a two-dimensional batching technique that reduces communication cost and increases parallelism. To further improve accuracy, PCPSI isolates computations per hash function and adaptively selects the number of hash functions based on dataset characteristics. Empirical evaluation under the semi-honest model shows that PCPSI outperforms prior HE-based PSI protocols across both large and small client sets. For large client sets, PCPSI achieves substantial speedups over strong HE-based PSI baselines, reaching up to 8.9× improvement under real (actual) cloud instance evaluation environments.

title: Adaptive Model-Free Predictive Current Control for SPMSM Drives Using Algebraic Estimator: Enhanced High-Frequency Disturbance Rejection
authors: Yang Shen, Zhenxiao Yin, Xueyan Wang, Fobao Zhou, Yujia Zhang, Hang Zhao
abstract: Deadbeat predictive current control (DPCC) is widely used in permanent magnet synchronous motor current control for fast dynamic response and strong current tracking, but its predictive model accuracy relies on accurate motor parameters. Model-free predictive current control (MFPCC) based on algebraic estimators (AE) exhibits excellent parameter robustness and disturbance rejection capability, serving as a data-driven method with parameter robustness that relies solely on input-output data. However, disturbances caused by proportional factor mismatch and high-frequency disturbances still limit its performance. To fully exploit the rich historical data from the AE and further enhance the disturbance rejection performance, an AE-based adaptive model-free predictive current control (AMF-AE) for SPMSM drives is proposed in this paper, which is inherently constructed with two tiny neural networks (NNs) to address both parameter mismatch and high-frequency disturbances. First, a tiny neural network for adaptive proportional factor is proposed to suppress disturbances caused by proportional factor mismatch. Second, another tiny neural network for the suppression of high-frequency disturbances is constructed, which is independent from specific motor or inverter parameters. Experimental results on a 1 kW surface-mounted permanent magnet synchronous motor (SPMSM) test bench verify the effectiveness of the proposed strategy. When there is a parameter mismatch with the proportional factor set to twice its nominal optimal value <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\alpha _{o}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, the proposed method achieves a 26.26% reduction in current total harmonic distortion (THD) compared with the conventional AE-based MFPCC under rated load conditions.

title: Explainable AI-Driven Intrusion Detection System Using Hybrid Deep Learning Models for Secure Network Infrastructure
authors: B. Vijay, Chandra J, Nagendra N, Prabhakaran M V, Rajiv S, Parul
publish: 2026 International Conference on Intelligent Computing and Automation for Sustainable Solutions (ICASS)
abstract: The network security threats have not been fixed hence the need to adopt more sophisticated intrusion detectors (IDS) that in addition to being highly accurate, should offer decision-making processes that are understandable. Conventional IDS systems are not typically transparent and cannot readily comprehend how attacks are processed by the system and the reaction by the security analysts. To make the integrated deep learning architecture, including Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) networks, and explainable AI models, such as SHAP (SHapley Additive explanations) and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations), easier to understand, this paper proposes to use an integrated deep learning architecture. The system was evaluated using NSL-KDD and CICIDS2017 datasets through comprehensive preprocessing, feature selection, and cross-validation techniques. The hybrid model proposed has a higher accuracy of detecting 98.7 and precision of 97.3 and a recall rate of 96.8 and an F1-score of 97.05, which is 12-15 superior to the traditional machine learning models, however, the model possesses a high explainability (score 89) confidence rate. The integration of explainable AI with hybrid deep learning models significantly enhances both detection performance and interpretability, providing security professionals with actionable insights for proactive threat mitigation.

title: Hybrid Consistency Policy: Decoupling Multi-Modal Diversity and Real-Time Efficiency in Robotic Manipulation
authors: Qianyou Zhao, Yuliang Shen, Xuanran Zhai, Duidi Wu, Jin Qi, Ce Hao, Jie Hu, Qiaojun Yu
abstract: In visuomotor policy learning, diffusion-based imitation learning has become widely adopted for its ability to capture diverse behaviors. However, approaches built on ordinary and stochastic denoising processes struggle to jointly achieve fast sampling and strong multi-modality. To address these challenges, we propose the Hybrid Consistency Policy (HCP). HCP runs a short stochastic prefix up to an adaptive switch time, and then applies a one-step consistency jump to produce the final action. To align this one-jump generation, HCP performs time-varying consistency distillation that combines a trajectory-consistency objective to keep neighboring predictions coherent and a denoising-matching objective to improve local fidelity. In both simulation and on a real robot, HCP with 25 SDE steps plus one jump approaches the 80-step DDPM teacher in accuracy and mode coverage while significantly reducing latency. These results show that multi-modality does not require slow inference, and a switch time decouples mode retention from speed. It yields a practical accuracy–efficiency trade-off for robot policies. Project website: <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://sites.google.com/view/hybrid-cp</uri>.

title: A Review of Vision-Based Vehicle Detection for UAV-Based Traffic Monitoring: Experimental Insights and Future Directions
authors: Jianlin Ye, Christos Kyrkou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: In Intelligent Transportation System (ITS), uncrewed aerial vehicle (UAV)-based surveillance offers an innovative solution to traffic surveillance with wide coverage and real-time data collection capabilities. In comparison to fixed ground-based infrastructure, UAVs are able to respond to dynamic traffic but present challenges such as vehicle detection at varying altitudes, compensation for motion-induced image variations and efficient processing of high-resolution images. Deep learning has been largely beneficial on improving the detection accuracy; however, for practical deployment, a critical assessment of the accuracy, latency, and harmonization with current transportation systems needs to be carefully considered. This survey reviews recent advancements in the UAV-based traffic monitoring, with a primary focus being deep neural network models for traffic analytics in various urban settings. Three main challenges identified in the literature are ensuring compatibility with traffic control systems, achieving real-time processing to optimize traffic flow, and maintaining robust detection in different environmental conditions. Existing solutions often lack comprehensive frameworks for utilizing UAV captured data to respond to incidents and manage traffic effectively. Future research should focus on optimal detection models, edge processing, and adaptive control integration to improve the responsiveness of urban traffic management.

title: Real-Time Detection of Subsurface Defects in CFRP Using Line-laser Scanning Thermography and CSDNet
authors: Jixiang Luo, Zhijie Zhang, Wuliang Yin, Guangyu Zhou, Haoze Chen, Gaokun Wang, Chaofan He, Qianfang Xie, Yonghua Xun
abstract: Carbon fiber reinforced polymers (CFRP) are widely used in aerospace and other fields but are susceptible to defects during manufacturing and service. Infrared thermography-based non-destructive testing enables non-contact, large-area inspection; however, most existing approaches rely on offline processing, limiting their applicability to online scenarios. To address this limitation, a real-time subsurface defect detection method for CFRP based on line-laser scanning thermography is proposed. First, real-time differential preprocessing is applied to suppress the thermal trailing effect. Then, a lightweight detection network, CSDNet (CFRP Subsurface Defect Detection Network), is developed, consisting of three core components: the C2f-RepViT-EMA module, the Lightweight Multi-branch Modulated Feature Pyramid Network (LM²FPN), and the Lightweight Quality-aware Detail Head (LQDH). These components reduce noise interference in infrared images, enhance multi-scale feature representation, and suppress low-quality detection boxes, respectively. Experiments on the T300 CFRP dataset demonstrate that the proposed method achieves a mAP50 of 0.984 with 1.4 million parameters, 5.0G FLOPs, and a deployment inference speed of 30 FPS. Compared with various lightweight detection models, the proposed method achieves a better trade-off between accuracy and efficiency, enabling synchronized scanning and detection and providing effective support for real-time online inspection of CFRP subsurface defects.

title: Robust Beamforming Design for STAR-RIS Assisted MISO Secure Communication System
authors: Bin Qiu, Chunni Deng, Zhongshan Zhang, Jianqing Li, Zhen Chen
abstract: As a novel reconfigurable intelligent surface (RIS) architecture, the simultaneous transmitting and reflecting RIS (STAR-RIS) provides full-space coverage and has strong potential to enhance communication security in future 6G networks through optimized beamforming. Motivated by this, we investigate the physical-layer security issues in STAR-RIS-assisted multi-user multiple input and single output (MISO) communication systems. Considering the difficulty in obtaining the channel state information (CSI) of eavesdropping users, we propose a robust beamforming optimization scheme with artificial noise (AN) under imperfect CSI. Specifically, we aim to maximize the system weighted sum secrecy rate by jointly optimizing the base station (BS) beamforming, AN vector and STAR-RIS transmission-reflection coefficients, subject to system transmit power and user information rate constraints. Due to the non-convex constraints and strong coupling of variables, the problem is difficult to solve directly. To address this, we propose an alternating optimization algorithm using successive convex approximation (SCA) and penalized concave convex process (PCCP), and utilize S-procedure to handle the uncertainty of eavesdropping CSI. Simulation results show that the proposed algorithm exhibits excellent convergence and robustness. Moreover, it outperforms other benchmark schemes in terms of secrecy performance.

title: Untethering EIT: An Integrated Wireless Tactile Interface for Human&#x2013;Machine Interaction_supp3-3688715.mp4
authors: Shili Liang
abstract: Large-area tactile sensing is a core requirement for intelligent robots. However, traditional high-density sensor arrays face challenges due to complex wiring and high integration difficulty. These issues limit their industrial application. This article proposes a stable and flexible tactile sensing system based on electrical impedance tomography (EIT). At the hardware level, we developed a highly integrated and miniaturized data acquisition (DAQ) system. The developed DAQ system weighs 34 g, complementing a conformable flexible sensor with a thickness of 700 <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\mu$</tex-math> </inline-formula>m. This design improves portability and achieves real-time dynamic mapping at 11.1 FPS. At the algorithmic level, we adopted a U-Net architecture to solve the nonlinear reconstruction challenges of EIT. Within a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$58\times 58\,\,\text{mm}^{2}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> sensing area, the model achieves a two-point spatial resolution limit of 7 mm and a mean multipoint localization error of 1.33 mm under multitouch scenarios. Furthermore, we utilized a CNN-BiLSTM model to decode complex spatiotemporal signals, achieving 96% accuracy for dynamic handwriting recognition and 85% in cross-user tests with ten participants, thereby demonstrating strong generalization capability. Finally, we deployed the system on an industrial robotic arm and a bionic hand. The system demonstrates high-fidelity contact sensing. It can also precisely identify small objects of 5 mm. The proposed EIT system provides a lightweight and scalable solution for industrial tactile perception and intelligent interaction.

title: Performance Evaluation of Floating Solar Power Projects in India Using Multi-Criteria Decisionmaking Techniques
authors: Vasupalli Manoj, Guntu Nooka Raju, Akasapu Kanaka Varshini, Balajee Maram, Gadi Bheema Sankararao, Gudiya Kamal
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: In India, floating solar photovoltaics (FSPV) are becoming more popular as a result of a scarcity of available land, improved energy efficiency, and other features, including decreased water evaporation losses. However, due to many different technical, financial, and ecological characteristics that go into evaluating these projects, these evaluations are complicated and difficult to make decisions on. Through this article, we provide a systematic evaluation of the performance of floating solar power projects in India by utilizing MultiCriteria Decision-Making (MCDM) techniques. An integrated framework that combines the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), Technique for Order Preference by Similarity to Ideal Solution (TOPSIS), and VIKOR will be used to accomplish this goal. AHP will be applied to sdevelop the relative weightings for the eight evaluation criteria while TOPSIS will be used to rank the ten floating solar project alternatives. The implementation of these methods will occur in MATLAB. The results indicate that regardless of the MCDM method applied, there was consistency among the rankings produced by each technique with respect to the floating solar power projects evaluated in terms of their overall performance; thus, the proposed methodology offers an effective decision-making tool for policymakers, planners, and investors interested in developing sustainable solar energy projects.

title: LoRa-based IoT System for Remote Phytosanitary Monitoring in Agriculture
authors: José Acerbis, Javier González, Joel Gak, Matías Miguez, Álvaro Pardo
abstract: This work presents a proof-of-concept system designed for remote phytosanitary monitoring in rural agricultural environments using LoRa technology. The aim is to detect if the plant has been infected by a pest. In this work, the more traditional approach of transmitting the whole image to a central server for analysis was implemented and compared to including local image processing using edge computing and micro AI techniques at node level. A prototype based on an ESP32-CAM and Heltec WiFi LoRa 32 (V3) was implemented, leveraging both compression and fragmentation strategies to overcome LoRa’s inherent limitations in bandwidth and data rate. The proposed system demonstrates significant reductions in energy consumption and transmission time while maintaining detection reliability when using on the edge computing, highlighting the potential of integrated edge intelligence for sustainable agriculture.

title: Weather Pattern Analysis for Corn Yield Explainability
authors: Md. Samiul Basir, Dennis Buckmaster, James V. Krogmeier
abstract: Weather is a principal driver of agricultural yield and exhibits both spatial and temporal heterogeneity with quasiperiodic behavior. This study integrates statistical summaries and topological features of weather time series to explain county-level corn yields in Indiana state of the US. Using persistent homology, we demonstrate that the geometric structure of weather data—capturing connected components and loops—varies across counties and years, and is highly sensitive to the temporal framing (e.g., full year vs. cropping season). Including daily extremes (maximum and minimum temperature) significantly enhances the topological signal compared to mean-based representations. Combining pattern descriptors–mean, standard deviation, entropy, low-frequency power, maximum jump–with topological invariants, i.e., connected components (H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf> as distinct weather events) and loops (H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> as cyclical transitions in weather dynamics) counts, mean H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> persistence, and persistence gap, a machine learning model (CatBoost regression) achieved R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.59 and MAPE = 10.31% explaining 59% of the yield variability. Sensitivity analysis identified topological features—particularly the counts of H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">0</inf>, H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf>, and mean H<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">1</inf> persistence—as significant contributors to yield explanation, underscoring the value of topological structure in modeling agro-meteorological interactions.

title: Skin Lesion Classification for Melanoma Detection Using Pretrained CNN Architectures
authors: Ch Amarnatha Sarma, P. Sree Lakshmi, S. Dinakar Jose, L. R. Sujithra, M. K. Prathiba, M. V. Subramanyam
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The prognosis for melanoma patients greatly depends on how quickly the disease is detected. Automated diagnosis from dermoscopic images however continues to be a computer vision challenge due to the complex multi-class intra and inter-class variations. In this work, we present a framework for the classification of dermoscopic skin lesion images for melanoma using different convolutional network (CNN) architectures. Our method combines lesion-specific preprocessing and feature enhancement, and multiple pretrained CNNs backs for transfer learning. Our approach is empirically tested on the ISIC 2018 dermoscopic image datasets, and we propose an ensemble-based fine-tuning strategy to classifications that are superior to existing state-of-the-art methods. Our model has an accuracy of 96.84 %, a sensitivity of 97.92 %, a specificity of 95.73 %, and an AUC of 0.984. Furthermore, our model is simple to implement and demonstrates non-oscillatory convergence, low number of missed detections, and generalized to novel/non-previously used datasets. Our experiments proved that a combination of pretrained CNN and our task-specific optimizations developed the most reliable melanoma detection. Our approach is rapid, simple, and has the potential to be integrated into real-world automated skin cancer detection systems.

title: Adaptive Resource Management and Security in Fog-Based IoT Healthcare Systems
authors: Deepak Kumar Tiwari, Smita Dash, Yogendra Kumar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: The applications of Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) like remote patient monitoring and emergency response require ultra-low latency and high reliability and a strong level of data security, which cannot be ensured in federated cloud architectures. Fog computing is an operation that solves these shortcomings by moving computation much nearer to medical sensors and gateways, but current solutions mostly consider resource management and security to be independent design issues and do not provide an integrated adaptive control. The paper suggests a security conscious adaptive fog architecture which optimizes Quality of Service (QoS), resource utilization, and data protection at the same time by applying a closed-loop control architecture comprised of priority-aware task scheduling, dynamic load balancing, adaptive authentication and encryption, intrusion detection, and federated learning on a per-mog level. The system is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem targeting end-to-end latency, energy consumption, service-level agreement compliance, and security risk, and is evaluated under realistic healthcare workloads and cyberattack scenarios against state-of-the-art scheduling, security, and privacy-preserving baselines. The results demonstrate that joint adaptation significantly reduces latency and SLA violations while enhancing resilience to attacks with moderate computational and communication overhead compared to static and single-objective approaches. By unifying adaptive resource orchestration, security enforcement, and privacy-preserving analytics within a single fog-based IoMT architecture, this study provides a resilient and scalable foundation for next-generation real-time healthcare systems.

title: A Hierarchical Virtual Machine Scheduling Algorithm for Multi-Zone Heterogeneous Environments
authors: Xiao-ming Hu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: Large-scale cloud deployments increasingly span multiple geographic zones whose hardware capabilities and pricing structures diverge substantially, rendering uniform scheduling policies inadequate. Existing VM placement mechanisms address either cost or load balance in isolation, leaving a gap for joint multi-objective optimization. We propose MZHSA, a two-stage hierarchical scheduler that separates zone-level admission from host-level placement. At the upper stage, candidate zones are ranked by a weighted composite of normalized price, current utilization, and residual headroom; the lower stage resolves host selection by jointly minimizing post-placement fragmentation and occupancy imbalance. Simulation across 1000-request workload traces shows that MZHSA cuts average provisioning cost by 21.5% over round-robin baselines while simultaneously reducing load-variance by 39% and sustaining sub-millisecond scheduling latency at scale.

title: Navigating the Future: AI in Shipping, Its Challenges and Legal Considerations
authors: Reetika Bansal, Trisha Gosain, Tanuja, Pratibha Verma, Gurpreet Saini, Trupti Maruti Muntode
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: In this changing world, the shipping industry has been a critical component in trade and commerce. It has faced a lot of challenges, such as security, safety, system integration, and shipping costs. The introduction of Artificial Intelligence (AI) has played a major role in combating these challenges. This digital revolutionization offers various benefits such as reduction in port time, better coordination and lower environmental impact. However, these advancements also have significant impacts on the shipping industry, including cybersecurity risks such as navigation and phishing attacks, high implementation costs, and a shortage of professionals. If the system is integrated with AI, who will be responsible in case any malfunction occurs, and what kind of training will the system undergo to conquer the situation? To get the answer to various questions as mentioned, the paper discusses challenges of AI in the shipping industry and what solutions does the industry provide to contend with the mentioned challenges. Additionally, the paper talks about the legal framework, especially the Maritime Law, emphasizing its role in the maritime sector. With the current regulations posed by the International Maritime Organization (IMO), the study highlights case studies and statistical data to provide a more comprehensive view of the proposed challenges.

title: Real-Time Agricultural Environment Monitoring with ESP32 and Cloud Security
authors: Shaik Anas, Kolluri Sahithi, Nakka Siva Rami Reddy, Pellakuru Venkatesh, V. Niveatha Saro
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The increasing demand for sustainability and efficiency in agriculture has prompted the use of Internet of Things for monitoring in real time of farms. Work revealed here is a real-time agricultural environment monitoring system which is developed in ESP32 microcontroller with various environmental sensors to measure parameter like temperature, humidity, soil moisture, and light. System will allow cloud-based monitoring by wireless transmission of sensed data, which will help the farmer in scheduling irrigation time, managing resources, protecting crop safety, etc. Apart from monitoring environmental conditions in the field, the system will emphasize securely transmitting and storing the data in the cloud.
