title: Target-centered Micro-motion Tracking and Measurement with Commodity WiFi
authors: Zijun Han, Zixuan Huang, Shurong Ge, Zhaoming Lu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Wireless signals-based radio tracking and measurement has emerged as a significant avenue for innovation in human-computer interaction. Among various wireless sensing modalities such as acoustic signals, visible light, Radio Frequency (RF), etc., the WiFi-based technique holds significant promise owing to its pervasiveness and cost-effectiveness. However, constrained by the limitations ofWiFi’s low frequency, small antenna array and bandwidth, current state-of-the-art systems can only achieve an accuracy on the order of decimeters. Many sophisticated micro-motion applications, such as aerial handwriting tracking, necessitate a significantly higher level of accuracy than what current state-of-the-arts can provide. In this paper, we present MicroFi, which targets to measure the micro-motion of WiFi radios in view of the Channel State Information (CSI). To be specific, we devise an innovative inside-out incremental motion tracking model to convert the tracking task from a transceiver view to a target-centered view. This model operates independently of any prior knowledge regarding anchor locations and demonstrates robustness against occlusions. Additionally, we propose a two-way communication protocol for CSI bidirectional collection, and eliminate clock asynchronization-induced random frequency offsets by channel reciprocity. We employ a 3D Multiple Signal Classification algorithm to jointly estimate the arrival angle, time of flight and Doppler velocity of the tracking target, and then derive the motion traces by multi-anchor aggregation. We have prototyped MicroFi utilizing commercially available WiFi radios, and comprehensive experiments confirm that MicroFi is capable of achieving an accuracy of 1.8 cm across a variety of user scenarios and environmental conditions, positioning it as a promising solution for numerous motion measurement applications.

title: Efficient Attention-Mamba Trajectory Prediction for Intelligent Vehicles Based on Self-Supervised Learning
authors: Hui Li, Qinghua Lin, Xiao Li, Xiugong Qin, Xiangzhen Kong, Jun Yan
abstract: Existing trajectory prediction models often fail to balance computational efficiency with the ability to capture complex interactions, typically hindered by the quadratic complexity of attention mechanisms and the limitations of supervised learning on imperfect data. To address these challenges, we propose EAT-SL, an Efficient Attention-Mamba Trajectory Prediction framework based on Self-Supervised Learning. First, we design a novel masking strategy comprising Interaction-Aware Masking and Lane Restructure Masking for the pre-training phase. This strategy encourages interaction-sensitive representations among vehicles, as well as between vehicles and map elements, via self-supervised reconstruction. Secondly, we propose the Inherit Mamba Net and Hamba Scene Encoder, which synergize the linear complexity of State Space Models with attention mechanisms to efficiently capture both global semantics and local dependencies. Finally, to mitigate the accumulated errors inherent in long-horizon trajectory prediction, we design a Recursive Memory Decoder to capture spatiotemporal interactions and maintain trajectory continuity during the fine-tuning phase. Experimental results on the Argoverse 2 dataset demonstrate that the proposed EAT-SL achieves a superior balance between accuracy and efficiency. Compared to the state-of-the-art baseline QCNet, EAT-SL reduces MinADE1 by 2.4% and MinFDE1 by 4.9%, while requiring only 42% of the parameters and maintaining a high inference throughput of 4.67 GFLOPs.

title: Active Neutral Point Clamped Configured Topologies with Equal and Unequal Step Voltages: Topological Derivation and Performance Assessment
authors: Md Reyaz Hussan, Marif Daula Siddique, Md Samiullah, Md Shafiullah, Ali T. Al-Awami, Mehdi Seyedmahmoudian, Saad Mekhilef, Alex Stojcevski
abstract: Over several decades, multilevel inverters have continued to play a vital role in power conversion because of their numerous advantages and one such advantage has been the improved power quality. Although it is commonly assumed that if number of levels are higher, the THD will reduce, however, in most of the cases, the step voltage is not considered in this statement. In this paper, this convention has been checked through nine different multilevel inverter topologies with different number of levels. First, a four-level topology is discussed, followed by the five-level, six-level, and seven-level topologies with a voltage gain of 1.5. With a slight modification to the 4 L topology, the voltage gain is increased to 2.5. With this voltage gain, 5 different topologies have been developed for six-level, eight-level, two topologies with nine-levels, and 11-levels. All the developed topologies shows different step voltages. A power quality assessment of output voltage and current has been carried out for different filter sizes as well as carrier frequencies for all nine topologies. The power loss analysis using two different modulation techniques has been carried out for all nine topologies. A detailed comparative study of all nine topologies has been carried out regarding component count, power loss, and harmonics. Simulation and experimental results have been provided for different topologies at the same operating conditions for a better decree of the topologies.

title: Multilevel Converter based PV-system with Low-voltage Global Maximum Power Point Tracking during Partial Shading
authors: Subhojit Das, Suman Maiti, Naba Kumar Peyada
abstract: Conventional photovoltaic (PV) converters typically employ a two-stage architecture comprising a DC-DC converter for maximum power point tracking (MPPT) followed by a central inverter for grid or load-side regulation. This arrangement encounters challenges in locating the Global Maximum Power Point (GMPP) at reduced voltages under partial shading conditions. This paper proposes a single-stage power conversion topology featuring a multilevel voltage output, which provides three key benefits: (i) reduced overall losses and enhanced performance ratio, (ii) elimination of AC-side passive filters, and (iii) capability to operate at low-voltage maximum power point. The proposed multilevel converter integrates PV panels directly with each submodule. Emphasis is given on overmodulation in submodules caused by reduction of the DC-bus voltage during partial shading. An auxiliary submodule, supplied via a tertiary winding of the power transformer, is introduced to mitigate low-voltage GMPP-related issues. Various control strategies, like AC-side control to match the grid requirements, active and reactive power regulation of individual submodules, overmodulation control, and PFC control to address GMPP operation have been developed for satisfactory operation during partial shading scenario. The losses of the converter are computed in PLECS incorporating different non-idealities of the switching devices and circuit elements to increase accuracy of calculation. The proposed converter is compared with the contemporary topologies encompassing component count and efficiency to highlight the effectiveness of this configuration. The operation of the converter including control methodologies are validated using MATLAB/Simulink simulations. A laboratory prototype of this converter is built using a DSP-based digital control platform to experimentally validate the closed-loop operation. Both simulation and experimental results show the effectiveness of the use of auxiliary submodule to effectively mitigate low-voltage GMPP-related issue during partial shading without allowing the submodules to enter into overmodulation zone.

title: Vibration of Soft, Twisted Beams for Underactuated Quadrupedal Locomotion_supp1-3686693.mp4
authors: Daniel Aukes
abstract: Underactuated compliant robotic systems offer a promising approach to mitigating actuation and control challenges by harnessing predesigned, embodied dynamic behaviors. This article presents Flix-Walker, a novel, untethered, centimeter-scale quadrupedal robot inspired by compliant underactuated mechanisms. Flix-Walker employs flexible, helix-shaped beams as legs, which are actuated by vibrations from just two motors to achieve three distinct mobility modes. We analyze the actuation parameters required to generate various locomotion modes through both simulation and prototype experiments. The effects of system and environmental variations on locomotion performance are examined, and we propose a generic metric for selecting control parameters that produce robust and functional motions. Experiments validate the effectiveness and robustness of these actuation parameters within a closed-loop control framework, demonstrating reliable trajectory-tracking and self-navigation capabilities.

title: Single-Stage Instance Segmentation Survey: A “1+2+3” Technical Framework
authors: Tao Zhou, Defang Chang, Huiling Lu, Wenwen Chai, Ke Song, Feiyu Shen
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: Single-stage instance segmentation is a prominent research direction in computer vision. However, in existing single-stage instance segmentation methods, there is a lack of summary on the 6 key questions: “How to select the learning paradigm?”, “How to extract features?”, “How to fuse features?”, “How to classify objects?”, “How to generate object masks?”, and “How to localize objects?”. These questions restrict the development and application to some degree. To address these questions, this paper proposes a “1+2+3” technical framework for summarizing single-stage instance segmentation. “1” represents one learning paradigm. Four mainstream learning paradigms and selection strategies are systematically summarized. “2” represents two local structures. The three characteristics of feature extraction are summarized, and the two feature fusion strategies are summarized. “3” represents three aspects of instance prediction. The object classification, object mask generation methods and object localization are discussed. Furthermore, the paper discusses new technologies such as instance segmentation based on promptable foundation models and instance segmentation based on vision-language models, as well as their typical applications in medical, video, and remote sensing image segmentation. The “1+2+3” technology framework proposed in the paper provides a problem-oriented technical map for instance segmentation, promoting the further development of single-stage instance segmentation.

title: Performance Analysis on Hybrid NOMA under Varying Channel Conditions
authors: Chenzhan Xu, Yanshi Sun, Ximing Xie, Fang Fang
abstract: By combining pure non-orthogonal multiple access (NOMA) and conventional orthogonal multiple access (OMA) techniques, hybrid NOMA (H-NOMA) can achieve significantly high energy efficiency. However, existing work on H-NOMA assumes that the channel gains of a user in different channel resource blocks remain constant, which might be unrealistic in many practical systems. To address this limitation, this letter provides a new analytical characterization of the H-NOMA framework under statistically independent channel realizations. Rigorous analysis reveals that under independent fading, the probability that H-NOMA achieves a higher instantaneous data rate than conventional OMA while consuming less transmit energy approaches one in the high SNR regime, which indicates the superiority of H-NOMA in energy efficiency. Numerical results are provided to validate the developed analysis and also demonstrate the superior performance of H-NOMA.

title: Finite-Time LQR Control With Redundant Inputs for Continuous-Time Systems
authors: Jianzhou Liu, Ze Zhang, Hongzheng Quan, Tianliang Zhang
abstract: This article investigates the effects of redundant control inputs on the finite-time linear quadratic regulator (LQR). Despite the widespread application of finite-time LQR control, characterizing the impact of redundancy remains challenging due to its time-varying nature. To address this gap, we first derive novel lower and upper bounds for the solution of the Riccati matrix differential equation by leveraging key matrix and eigenvalue inequalities. Building on these bounds, we establish a sufficient condition that guarantees a simultaneous reduction in both the optimal quadratic performance index and the controller gain. Furthermore, a deterministic algorithm is presented to facilitate the practical design of the weighting matrices. The effectiveness and engineering applicability of the proposed approach are demonstrated through numerical simulations and a case study on a fluid catalytic cracking reactor.

title: BIFFNet: Bi-Branch Interaction and Frequency-Domain Feature-Enhanced Network for Remote Sensing Change Detection
authors: Chenyang Wang, Jing Wang, Runzhou Li, Lu Wang, Dong Ren, Li Liu, Hao Chen
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: In recent years, remote sensing image change detection has attracted increasing attention and achieved remarkable performance gains. However, it remains challenging to effectively disentangle background and object features in multi-temporal imagery, which makes the results susceptible to pseudo-changes under variations in illumination, season, and sensor characteristics. Meanwhile, most existing methods rely on simple concatenation or subtraction for feature fusion, making it difficult to simultaneously ensure semantic consistency and detail fidelity, and consequently leading to missed detections of small objects and imprecise boundary delineation. To address these issues, this paper proposes a Bi-branch Interaction and Frequency-domain Feature-enhanced Network (BIFFNet) for change detection. Specifically, we construct a Temporal Feature Interaction and difference-enhancement Module (TFIM), which jointly models difference-enhanced features and original feature fidelity to mitigate the information loss and domain shift caused by naive differencing. Furthermore, we design a Frequency-domain Decomposition and Multi-branch Modeling module (FDMM), which explicitly decomposes features into high- and low-frequency components: the high-frequency branch emphasizes edge and texture details, while the low-frequency branch models global illumination and background structures, thereby improving boundary accuracy and robustness in complex scenes. Experimental results on multiple remote sensing change detection datasets validate the effectiveness of the proposed network in suppressing pseudo-changes and enhancing boundary delineation. The code will be made available on <uri xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">https://github.com/WCYGORETZKA/BIFFNet</uri>.

title: A Location-Sensitive Gaussian Attention Transformer Method for Hyperspectral Target Detection
authors: Ying Pang, Qiang Qu, Mingxing Wang
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Hyperspectral target detection (HTD) remains a challenging task due to high spectral dimensionality, complex background interference, and the difficulty of jointly modeling local spectral–spatial structures and long-range contextual dependencies. Existing Transformer-based methods have demonstrated strong global modeling capability, but their effectiveness is often limited by the lack of explicit and continuous spatial priors. To address this limitation, we propose a novel Location-sensitive Gaussian Attention (LGA) framework for hyperspectral target detection. The core of the proposed method is a Gaussian-based attention mechanism that embeds a continuous, data-driven spatial prior directly into Transformer self-attention, fundamentally differing from conventional discrete or handcrafted positional encoding strategies. By integrating Gaussian-weighted spatial priors with global self-attention, the proposed approach adaptively emphasizes target-relevant regions while suppressing cluttered backgrounds, leading to more accurate target localization. In addition, spectral–spatial feature extraction is incorporated to preserve local spectral continuity and spatial coherence. Extensive experiments conducted on seven widely used hyperspectral benchmark datasets demonstrate that the proposed LGA method consistently outperforms state-of-the-art classical and deep learning–based detectors in terms of detection accuracy and robustness. The results confirm that the proposed Gaussian-enhanced attention mechanism provides an effective and generalizable solution for hyperspectral target detection in complex remote sensing scenes.

title: Systematic Evaluation of Interpolation Strategies for Soil Moisture Retrieval Using Sentinel-1/2 Data: A Case Study in Shandian River Basin
authors: Dongqiang Yang, Changhua Liu, Haobo Cai
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Microwave remote sensing is widely used for soil moisture (SM) monitoring due to its sensitivity to dielectric properties. However, microwave backscattering is affected by surface roughness, vegetation structure, and soil texture, which limits high-resolution SM retrieval accuracy and necessitates multi source data integration. Optical remote sensing provides complementary vegetation information but suffers from data gaps caused by cloud cover. Although various gap-filling methods for vegetation indices have been compared, their impacts on multi source SM retrieval remain underexplored. This study integrates Sentinel-1 and Sentinel-2 data through random forest (RF) regression in northern China's Shandian River Basin, comparing univariate and multivariate interpolation methods for SM retrieval. Comprehensive evaluation includes spatial cross validation, leave-one-station-out (LOSO) validation, Station Based Spatial Error Assessment and feature importance assessment. Results demonstrate that interpolation methods significantly improve SM retrieval accuracy. The Savitzky-Golay (SG) filter achieves highest accuracy (R = 0.849), while Multivariate Singular Spectrum Analysis (MSSA) demonstrates superior spatial generalization. This study provides quantitative guidance for interpolation method selection in agro-pastoral transitional zones.

title: An enhanced Lightweight Key Synchronization scheme for Secure IoT Communication in smart cities
authors: A. AbhdulKalam, M.Arul Pugazhendhi, Ananth Kumar Tamilarasan, Saikat Gochhait, Zafrul Allam
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: As the Internet of Things (IoT) continues to evolve rapidly, establishing secure communication between devices becomes essential, not just to guard sensitive data, but to uphold user privacy and prevent any form of unauthorized access. With IoT touching everything from homes to industries, the need for strong yet efficient protection has never been more urgent. Home automation to industrial automation requires cryptographic algorithms to be robust and lightweight enough to run effectively on low-end devices. Traditional encryption techniques typically incur significant computational overhead, making them unsuitable for low-power Internet of Things devices designed for these constrained environments. In this paper, an innovative lightweight real-time key synchronization and encryption method is proposed. The envisaged lightweight security framework is demonstrated through an exemplary smart door lock application scenario, which can be considered a typical case representing secure communication across constrained IoT devices. The proposed system employs elliptic curve Diffie-Hellman (ECDH) to generate temporary shared keys, thus ensuring forward secrecy and eavesdropping immunity. ChaCha20-Poly1305, a high-speed, low-memory, and low-latency authenticated encryption algorithm, is employed to secure data transfer after setting up the keys. A socket-based client-server protocol is implemented in Python to allow real-time key exchange and secure transmission of command.

title: Spatio-Temporal Analysis of Traffic Accidents in Bahrain using Geographic Information System
authors: Uneb Gazder
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: The objective of this paper is to design and develop a Geodatabase and analyze spatial and temporal fatal traffic accidents in the Kingdom of Bahrain utilizing Geographic Information Systems (GIS) as a tool. The data was collected from the General Traffic Department of the Ministry of Interior from the years 2012 to 2018. The analysis involved spatio-temporal analysis and mapping of the fatal traffic accidents. The analysis identified both persistent high-risk locations as well as the temporal shift in the hotspot locations towards the northern part of the country, demonstrating the advantage of spatio-temporal analysis over the traditional static methods. A primary example of recurrent high-risk location was node 286 at Dry Dock Highway and Hidd Avenue, and Shaikh Jaber A. AlSubah Highway. These results provide evidence-based insights for targeted interventions and guide the future direction of research towards the understanding of underlying causal factors behind these spatial dynamics.

title: Design of a Low-Noise Wideband Preamplifier Based on Multiple Discrete Current Sources
authors: Kun Guan, Xulei Han, Qidi Sun, Meitao Zhai, Limin Zhang, Xiaofeng Yi
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: In borehole transient electromagnetic (BTEM) detection, the received signals are extremely weak and broadband, making them highly susceptible to intrinsic circuit noise and low-frequency interference. To address these challenges, a low-noise wideband preamplifier based on a multi-discrete current source scheme is proposed. The preamplifier employs a four-stage cascaded architecture, including a junction field-effect transistor (JFET) differential input stage with independent current source biasing to suppress device parameter dispersion, a second-order high-pass filter for low-frequency interference rejection, a low-noise operational amplifier stage for signal amplification, and a second-order low-pass filter for bandwidth limitation and high-frequency noise suppression. Theoretical analysis and parameter optimization are conducted for each stage. Simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed design achieves an input-referred noise voltage below 2.00 nV/√Hz over a bandwidth of 150 Hz to 65 kHz, with a passband gain of approximately 60 dB. The proposed preamplifier satisfies the requirements of borehole TEM systems and offers an effective approach to enhancing detection depth and resolution.

title: A Robust Incremental Deadbeat Predictive Current Control for SPMSM With Double-Weighted Compensation
authors: Halzat Yitniyaz, Guomao Li, Jihong Zhu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: This paper addresses the steady-state error and limited robustness of deadbeat current predictive control (DPCC) for permanent magnet synchronous motors under parameter mismatches. Incremental DPCC (IDPCC) is analyzed, showing effective suppression of resistance and flux mismatch-induced deviations but sensitivity to inductance mismatch, leading to oscillations. A dual-weighted robust incremental predictive control (RIDPCC) is proposed, enhancing stability and parameter robustness while maintaining fast dynamic response. Simulations demonstrate that RIDPCC achieves superior current tracking and effectively suppresses oscillations under various parameter mismatches, outperforming conventional DPCC and IDPCC.

title: From Isolation to Integration: Road Infrastructure Development and Economic Growth of Eastern and North-Eastern States of India
authors: Sumit Oberoi, Pooja Kansra, Gagan Kukreja, Sugandh Arora
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: Road infrastructure is a major component of economic development particularly in the less served areas where the lack of connectivity and governmental presence are found. This paper focuses on the relationship of improvement of road infrastructure with economic development in Eastern and North-Eastern India. This study using the panel data from year 2001 to 2022 investigates the bidirectional and unidirectional relationship between road infrastructure and the GDP in the Granger causality relationship. The data indicates notable differences across various regions. In the East, there is a reciprocal relationship between road infrastructure and economic activity, while in the North-East, it is economic growth that propels infrastructure development. This underscores the need for increased financial resources and customised strategies. The findings suggest that improvements to national road infrastructure yield long-term advantages, despite any short-term effects. Analyses suggest that initiatives like the Bharatmala Pariyojna contribute to diminishing regional disparities and promote sustainable development. It promotes tailored policies for specific regions, continuous investments, and fair distribution of resources to improve connectivity and foster economic growth across the diverse areas of India. The findings will assist decision makers, legislators or interested parties in using infrastructure for equitable development.

title: Trajectory Optimization of AUV for Oil Spill Monitoring Based on ConvLSTM Environmental Prediction*
authors: Hanhui Fu, Jicheng Ding, Yi Duan, Chuanqi Li
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: Autonomous Underwater Vehicles (AUVs) are core equipment for marine environmental monitoring. Efficient and safe navigation in complex dynamic marine environments is key for oil spill emergency response. Traditional AUV trajectory optimization based on static environmental models cannot adapt to spatiotemporal variability of marine multi-physical fields, causing path failures due to eddies and density fronts. We propose a 3D trajectory optimization method integrating ConvLSTM-based spatiotemporal prediction. A ConvLSTM-3D network predicts key physical fields (density, flow) for early perception of severe conditions. Predicted fields are quantified into communication, safety, and timeliness cost functions, then integrated into an improved Genetic Algorithm for optimal path planning. Simulation results for oil spill monitoring show the proposed algorithm significantly improves path safety and mission efficiency, reducing comprehensive path cost by 32.6% compared to static methods.

title: Large Language Model Driven Clinical Knowledge Graph Enhancing Medical Decision Accuracy
authors: Abhishek Gokula
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The growing complexity of clinical decisionmaking in oncology, especially in lung cancer multidisciplinary team consultations, can be explained by the simultaneous presence of heterogeneous data silos and ontological burden hindering timely and accurate diagnoses and therapeutics. Current methodologies that rely solely on large language models are riddled with hallucinations and fail to explain interpretability, while static knowledge graphs lack the flexibility to include changing evidence. In response, the present work proposes LLM- ClinKG, a hybrid framework, which dynamically augments clinical knowledge graphs through entity-relation extraction and multimodal fusion performed by large language models followed by the symbolic-inductive reasoning for link prediction and counter factual analysis. Evaluation on multimodal lung cancer cohort results show that the system achieves F1 score of 92% in relation extraction (14 % increment improvement compared to baseline methods), 0.89 area under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (ROC) curve for relapse risk prediction and 45 % consultation time reduction with <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{8 5 \%}$</tex> data utilisation. LLM-ClinKG thus promotes the field of trustworthy artificial intelligence by providing interpretable evidence-based help in improving diagnostic accuracy and efficiency of work flows in high-stakes oncology.

title: Innovation in Educational Media: Development of Augmented Reality-Based Multimedia Flashcards for Introducing Yogyakarta's Local Culture
authors: Rizal Justian Setiawan, Khakam Ma’ruf, Siti Hardila, Fatimatuzzahro’, Akhmad Mukhibun, Darmono
publish: 2025 3rd International Conference on Sustaining Heritage: The Future of Conservation: Embracing Technology and Innovation for Sustainable Heritage (ICSH)
abstract: Globalization poses a significant threat to the preservation of Yogyakarta’s cultural heritage, particularly among digital-native youth who tend to favor global culture over traditional practices. Conventional learning methods, such as lectures, textbooks, and static visual media, have proven insufficient in engaging students effectively. This study develops an Augmented Reality (AR) based multimedia flashcard to address this gap, employing the ADDIE model (Analysis, Design, Development, Implementation, Evaluation) with a sample of 30 eighth-grade students from three junior high schools in Yogyakarta. The AR flashcards integrate 3D cultural objects, audio narration, quizzes, and immersive mobile interactions to enhance the learning experience. Results from pre-test and post-test assessments indicate a significant improvement in cultural understanding, with an average increase of 22.9 points. The greatest gains were observed in knowledge of the Keraton architecture, with an increase of 24.5 points, and comprehension of classical dance, with an increase of 25.2 points. User satisfaction, measured using the System Usability Scale (SUS), achieved an average score of 86.2, which is categorized as excellent usability. These findings confirm the effectiveness of AR flashcards in promoting cultural education and heritage preservation through technology, providing practical implications for educational practice and theoretical insights for strategies in digital cultural preservation.

authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)

title: A Hybrid Monkey Spider Optimization Approach for Economic Load Dispatch with Renewable Energy
authors: Pitchala Vijaya Kumar, Aruna Jeyanthy P, Shilaja C
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Conventional methods struggle with a nonlinearity, a non-convexity, and a complexity in the renewable energy sources. Hence, in this paper, we propose a novel hybrid optimization method for solving the ELD problems in the power systems that use both the wind and solar energy as well as other renewable sources. This strategy named Hybrid Monkey Spider Optimization (HMSO) tends to combine the the Monkey Algorithm (MA) and Spider Monkey Optimization (SMO). The hybridization makes it easier to refine things locally and search for things globally. We test the suggested HMSO algorithm no IEEE 30-bus and 118-bus systems. The results show that the proposed HMSO lowers the generation costs, speed up convergence, and a better deal with the renewable energy sources.

title: A Data Driven Approach to Analyze Crime Data of Los Angeles
authors: Abdallah Al Naboulsi, Mohamed Abdallah Nour, Saadat Mehmood Alhashmi
publish: 2026 17th Student Research Conference on Applied Computing (SRC)
abstract: Crime analysis and prediction research is essential in order to improve safety in the digital era. This study utilizes Los Angeles crime data to investigate how victim age, gender, and ethnicity vary across certain types of violent crime. This paper adopts a comparative quantitative approach to violent crime types, and it does so by applying Binary Logistic Regression Models. The analysis models victim characteristics as the outcomes and crime types as the predictors. First, the results show that victim ages differ meaningfully across crime types. Second, the findings reveal variation in gender across offenses. Third, the analysis showed clear variation in ethnicities across different offenses. Finally, these results highlight the significance of treating violent crime types as their own separate categories since treating the offenses as one uniform category may hide meaningful patterns in the demographic. This paper should meaningfully contribute by coordinating police resources and efforts with certain victimization patterns and strengthening victim support services for victim groups that are more likely to be implicated in particular offense types.

title: Blind Separation Algorithm for Convolutive Frequency-Hopping Signals Based on Dynamic Step-size IVA
authors: Le Sun, Juncheng Liu, Yu Guo, Yabo Wang, Songhu Ge, Lin Li
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th World Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (WCCCT)
abstract: To address the challenges of man-made interference and multipath propagation faced by ultra-short wave frequency-hopping communication systems in complex electromagnetic environments, this paper presents a Dynamic Step-size Independent Vector Analysis algorithm (DS-IVA), based on the dynamic regulation of the learning step-size using the time-frequency matrix of source signals. By incorporating the concept of the second-order Newton method into the optimization process, the algorithm adaptively adjusts the iterative step-size, thereby significantly accelerating the convergence speed while ensuring algorithm stability. Simulation results demonstrate that the separation performance of the presented algorithm for convolutive frequency-hopping signals is significantly superior to that of the traditional Blind Source Separation (BSS) algorithm. Compared with the traditional IVA algorithm, the presented method improves the output average Signal-to-Interference-plus-Noise Ratio (SINR) by 1.5 dB and the average correlation coefficient by 7.1%, while reducing the average computation time by 94.74%.

title: A Programmable CRISPR-Phage Strategy to Disable Antibiotic Resistance and Biofilm Formation in Pseudomonas aeruginosa
authors: Mariam Khalid, Marah Altabbal
publish: 2026 17th Student Research Conference on Applied Computing (SRC)
abstract: Antibiotic resistance has become an impending global crisis in modern medicine, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa is recognized by the World Health Organization (WHO) as a critical priority pathogen. In its published list of antibiotic-resistant bacteria requiring urgent development of new antibiotics, P. aeruginosa ranks among the highest due to its prevalence, the difficulty of treating it in clinical settings, and its prominent role in nosocomial infections, notably ventilator-associated pneumonia, bloodstream infections, and chronic lung disease in cystic fibrosis patients. This bacterium uses overlapping layers of defense that include β-lactamase production, efflux pumps, changes in porins, and quorum-sensing systems such as lasI/lasR that regulate biofilm formation, which can make the bacteria up to a thousand times more tolerant of antibiotics than planktonic cells. Current therapeutic options from β-lactam/inhibitor combinations and phage cocktails to quorum-sensing inhibitors are gradually losing efficacy, as each addresses only one aspect of resistance without fully eliminating resistance determinants or preventing biofilm maturation. Engineered bacteriophages with dual functions, bacterial lysis and CRISPR-based disruption of targets such as ampC and lasI, could therefore provide a comprehensive approach to counter both antibiotic resistance and biofilm development, paving the way for more effective treatments against multidrug-resistant Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Accordingly, the objective of this study is to evaluate whether bacteriophage-mediated delivery of a dual CRISPR–Cas9 system targeting ampC and lasI can simultaneously enhance β-lactam antibiotic susceptibility and reduce biofilm formation in Pseudomonas aeruginosa.

title: Lightweight and Explainable Phishing URL Detection Using Feature Selection and XGBoost
authors: Nandan Srinivasarao Vaddempudi, Dinesh Sajja, Chilukuri Phanidar, Sagar Basavaraju
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: More than 70% of the initial attacks are phishing sites that attack millions of users using fake links. Conventional detection systems, e.g., rule-based and blacklist-based systems, do not keep up with quickly changing phishing patterns, whereas deep learning models, though correct, are plagued by high computational ability and low interpretability. This paper presents the lightweight and interpretable phishing URL detection framework, which combines Recursive Feature Elimination (RFE) to select the features, and Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique (SMOTE) to deal with the imbalance between classes, as well as SHapley Additive explanations (SHAP) to understand the models. The lexical features that are proposed in the XGBoostbased framework include URL length, Digit count, Dot count, Has HTTPS among others to achieve real time detection without any reference to the content of a webpage or to external API. The model was trained using the PhiUSIIL Phishing URL Dataset (235,795 URLs) and when the model was optimized to four features, it provided 85.4% accuracy, which is close to the full-feature model (85.6%) but reduced the computation time by nearly a fourfold. Following the usage of SMOTE, the recall rose to 78% and F1-score rose to 0.558, which proved the best phishing detection using unbalanced data. The balance between accuracy, interpretability, and efficiency is high in the proposed framework.

title: Detection of Arbitrary Polarization States Based on Spoof Surface Plasmon Polaritons
authors: Yi Ren, Jun Xia, Yuxiang Wang, Dianjie Wang, Jingjing Zhang
publish: IEEE Antennas and Wireless Propagation Letters
abstract: Polarization detection is crucial for advanced electromagnetic applications, yet conventional Stokes-based methods are often bulky and complex. This letter proposes a universal, compact approach for detecting arbitrary polarization states on the Poincaré sphere using a spoof surface plasmon polariton (SSPP) platform. Leveraging the spin-momentum locking property of SSPPs, the design incorporates only two spiral shaped scatterers coupled to a single SSPP waveguide. Incident polarization is decomposed into left- and right-handed circular components, which are separated directionally and collected at two output ports. The signal ratio between these ports yields the full polarization state via a derived analytical relation, eliminating the need for multi-element cascades or Stokes parameter retrieval. Simulated and measured deviations are below 0.9 ° and 8.5 °, respectively. The proposed platform offers a fabrication-friendly, planar architecture suited for on-chip integration, providing a robust miniaturized solution for polarization sensing, communication, and imaging.

title: Structure-Aware Diffusion Policy for Bimanual Cooperative Manipulation_supp1-3701562.mp4
authors: Shiqi Li
abstract: Bimanual cooperative manipulation involves critical closed-chain contact phases in which task success depends on maintaining inter-arm geometric consistency while mitigating antagonistic interaction. We present a structure-aware diffusion policy that conditions action generation on an explicit cooperative representation, rather than inferring coordination implicitly from raw multimodal inputs. The representation combines (i) Cooperative Dual Task-Space (CDTS) descriptors computed from forward kinematics, which decompose bimanual geometry into absolute motion and relative configuration, and (ii) a gravity-compensated force-difference cue computed from left–right end-effector forces to capture interaction inconsistency. To integrate perception with these structured priors, we design a hierarchical fusion module in which bidirectional vision–force cross-attention first aligns cross-modal evidence, followed by CDTS-guided cross-attention that uses structural tokens as queries to extract coordination-relevant features. Real-robot experiments on dual-arm cooperative carrying across objects of varying sizes and appearances demonstrate improved closed-chain stability and more robust performance across the evaluated object variations, increasing the overall success rate from 58% to 89% compared with a vision-dominant diffusion baseline.

title: Unfolded Coprime Padded L-Shaped Array Design From Fourth-Order Difference Co-Array Concept
authors: Hua Chen, Yifan Zhang, Wei Liu, Qing Wang, Ye Tian, Ming Jin, Hing Cheung So
publish: IEEE Transactions on Aerospace and Electronic Systems
abstract: Sparse array design leveraging the fourth-order difference co-array (FODCA) can significantly enhance the total count of uniform degrees-of-freedom (uDOF). The unfolded coprime L -shaped array (UCLsA), owing to its high sparsity and reduced mutual coupling effect, has become an attractive structure for high-resolution two-dimensional (2D) direction-of-arrival (DOA) estimation. However, the FODCA of UCLsA exhibits numerous holes in its central region, resulting in constrained uDOF. To mitigate the aforementioned limitation, this paper introduces an unfolded coprime padded L -shaped array (UCPLsA), which is constructed by augmenting the UCLsA with two additional subarrays to achieve a substantially larger number of uDOF. First, a systematic analysis of the FODCA of UCLsA is conducted, and the exact representation of the central-hole locations is derived. Then, the position distribution of the newly generated fourth-order virtual elements in UCPLsA is analyzed, followed by the computation of its key weight functions. When compared to current 2D sparse array designs, the proposed UCPLsA provides two major augmented proficiencies, offering a larger number of uDOF and effectively mitigating the mutual coupling effect. Numerical experiments are presented to corroborate the superior performance of UCPLsA in relation to alternative 2D sparse arrays.

title: PIVOT: A Framework for High-Fidelity Facade PV Layouts via Generative Seeding and Differentiable Optimization
authors: Dongxu Zhuang, Ruyu Liu, Xiufeng Liu, Per Sieverts Nielsen, Tangao Hu, Jianhua Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: The automation of Building-Integrated Photovoltaic (BIPV) design is essential for the digital transformation of urban renewable energy, yet current methods struggle to bridge the gap between semantic facade assessment and engineering-grade installation plans. Existing AI-based approaches typically yield raw segmentation masks that lack the geometric precision and physical validity required for real-world deployment. This paper presents PIVOT, a novel end-to-end framework for the automated synthesis of high-fidelity, collision-free PV layouts from single 2D facade images. We formulate the layout generation as a two-stage neuro-symbolic and differentiable optimization process. First, an LLM-based programmatic seeding stage uses structured facade information and elementary spatial-relation cues to generate initial layout hypotheses. Second, a differentiable layout optimization engine refines these proposals by treating panel placement as a continuous constrained optimization problem, effectively resolving spatial overlaps while maximizing geometric regularity. Validated on a diverse dataset of 80 building facades, experimental results demonstrate that PIVOT significantly outperforms heuristic (MaxRects) and evolutionary (MOGA) baselines. Our framework reduces the layout collision rate from 9.7% to 0.7% and improves geometric regularity by over 10%, while requiring only approximately 80 seconds of processing time per building. This work advances BIPV design automation by converting semantically parsed facade information into geometrically constrained, constructibility-oriented PV layouts for downstream performance evaluation.

title: Koopman Neural Operator for Multi-Task Modeling in Optical Fiber Communications with NTK Analysis
authors: Yiming Meng, Xiao Luo, Xiaotian Jiang, Wenbin Chen, Shengnan Li, Min Zhang, Danshi Wang
abstract: Optical fiber communication networks serve as a critical backbone of modern information infrastructure and have undergone rapid development in recent decades. To optimize transmission dimension resources, extensive research has focused on accurately modeling key linear and nonlinear effects, including chromatic dispersion, Kerr nonlinearity, stimulated Raman scattering, laser transition processes, and light field evolution, all governed by partial differential equations (PDEs). Traditional numerical solvers and black-box data-driven neural networks are commonly used to solve these PDEs but suffer from high computational complexity and strong data dependency, respectively. This paper explores the Koopman neural operator (KNO) for multi-task modeling in optical communications. The framework is validated across four representative tasks: 16QAM waveform propagation, quality of transmission (QoT) estimation, optical amplification modeling, and light field evolution. In all benchmarks, KNO outperforms Fourier neural operators (FNO) and convolutional neural operators (CNO), reducing mean squared error by up to one order of magnitude in certain tasks, requiring approximately three times fewer parameters. Branch ablation studies on spectral components reveal that KNO's dual-branch architecture adaptively balances low-frequency (LF) global dynamics and high-frequency (HF) local details. Furthermore, generalization tests under unseen physical conditions, such as launch powers and cross-device generalization, demonstrate KNO's robustness in handling conditions not observed during training. Neural tangent kernel (NTK) analysis highlights KNO's superior spectral properties, such as higher effective rank, lower condition number, and improved kernel ridge regression performance, which underpin its enhanced trainability, modal response, and generalization. This work establishes KNO as a scalable surrogate for PDE-related problems in optical fiber communication systems.

title: Finite-Time Distributed Time-Varying Optimization of High-Order Agents With Unknown Gradient Derivative
authors: Wenbo Zhu, Shu Liang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Control of Network Systems
abstract: In this paper, the finite-time distributed time-varying optimization problems for high-order agents are investigated, where the gradient derivative of time-varying cost functions is unknown. First, a finite-time distributed optimal trajectory estimator which do not rely on the Hessian information and the partial derivative of gradient with respect to time is constructed. Then, based on the generated optimal trajectory estimation signal, a distributed time-varying optimization algorithm for high-order agents with globally practically finite-time convergence is designed, where the bound of optimization error can be arbitrarily small by regulating controller parameters. Moreover, the rigorous convergence analysis is provided as well. In final, a simulation example is conducted to validate the proposed theoretical results.

title: Polarization-Stable Conformal FSS for ISM-Band (5.8 GHz) EMI Shielding in Conformal IoMT Devices
authors: Md Kutub Uddin, Touhidul Alam, Mohamad A. Alawad, Abdulmajeed M. Alenezi, Mohamed S. Soliman, Mohammad Tariqul Islam
abstract: The growth of the Internet of Medical Things (IoMT) has intensified electromagnetic-interference (EMI) concerns in the 5.8 GHz ISM band. Frequency-selective surfaces (FSS) offer a practical alternative to rigid enclosures for wearable and conformal medical devices, providing lightweight, integrable shielding. This article presents a polarization-insensitive, conformal FSS designed for broadband EMI shielding in 5.8 GHz ISM-band for IoMT applications. The proposed structure employs a complementary patch configuration printed on opposite sides of a thin dielectric substrate to achieve a broadband band-stop response. The top-layer resonator defines the lower stopband with S21 < −10 dB and a compact unit-cell size of 0.17λ₀, where λ₀ corresponds to the resonance frequency. The FSS achieves a <−10 dB stop band from 4.0 GHz to 7.7 GHz, centered at 5.8 GHz with the bandwidth of 63.793% that fully covered the ISM spectrum. Owing to its fourfold symmetric geometry, the design demonstrates polarization independence and maintains angular stability up to 75°. To validate the real time performance, A fabricated 6×6 array prototype is experimentally evaluated under both planar and conformal conditions, showing strong agreement with simulation results. The proposed lightweight and flexible FSS provides an effective solution for broadband EMI suppression, enabling reliable electromagnetic compatibility (EMC) and signal integrity in compact and wearable biomedical IoT systems.

title: Beyond Artifacts: Enhancing Morphing Attack Detection with Beauty Latent Features
authors: Rasmus Stilling, Juan E. Tapia, Christoph Busch
abstract: This work investigates the relationship between facial attractiveness and morphed face images to improve morphing attack detection in biometric systems. We propose a Differential Morphing Attack Detection (D-MAD) method that integrates beauty latent features derived from Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) trained on facial attractiveness with those from CNN models trained on facial recognition datasets. By incorporating beauty features alongside facial recognition features, our approach aims to boost the accuracy of morph detection. Experiments conducted on synthetic and non-synthetic datasets of morphed images demonstrate the potential of utilizing beauty latent features to enhance D-MAD performance, particularly when dealing with synthetic images. Our results suggest that incorporating beauty information can significantly improve the detection capabilities of D-MAD methods, especially when synthetic images are involved in either training or testing.

title: Welding Robot Selection in Automotive Seat Frame Manufacturing: Pythagorean Fuzzy Analytic Hierarchy Process
authors: Nitidetch Koohathongsumrit, Wasana Chankham
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Control, Automation and Robotics (ICCAR)
abstract: The selection of an appropriate welding robot influences production quality and operational efficiency in automotive seat frame manufacturing. This study applies the Pythagorean fuzzy analytic hierarchy process (PF-AHP) to the welding robot selection problem. The method starts with pairwise comparisons of six criteria: horizontal reach, vertical reach, price suitability, precision performance, number of poles, and load handling capability. Decision-maker evaluations expressed through linguistic terms are converted into Pythagorean fuzzy numbers, which support the formation of the difference, interval multiplicative, determinacy, and pre-normalization weight matrices to calculate the criteria weights. The same computational procedure applies to six welding robot alternatives under each criterion. The final ranking results from the aggregation of the criteria weights and the weights of the alternatives. In the case study, implementation of the selected welding robot based on the PF-AHP results led to a substantial improvement in production quality, with welding-related defects reduced by up to 75%. The results provide the most appropriate welding robot that best aligns with the operational priorities of the case study, with its performance standing out across the criteria that hold the greatest influence. The proposed approach provides decision makers in industry with a structured basis for selecting welding equipment, and it also creates opportunities for further work that may involve changing production environments, the inclusion of additional evaluation factors, or the application of other multi-criteria decision methodologies.

title: Study on the Detection and Tracking of Broken Pavement Using YOLO Based on Retinex Image Processing
authors: Che-Ping Hung, Hong-Yu Lin, Kuo-Yang Tu, Wei-Xiang Huang
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Control, Automation and Robotics (ICCAR)
abstract: In this paper, the detection and tracking of broken pavement based on Retinex image processing are designed and implemented. In traditional methods, there are some problems in the detection systems of broken pavement, such as the detection influenced by environmental lighting changes, and unstable identification when the system gets closed to broken target. In aspect of detection, Multi Scale Retinex (MSR) with color restoration is engaged to enhance training images. This method improves the problem of training model influenced easily by lighting and shadow changes. In addition, YOLO and Deep SORT are combined to boost the identification stability because of approaching broken targets.In this paper, the experiment scheme is divided into three parts. In the first part, images of broken pavement taken at different speeds are used to train a YOLO model, and the most suitable model for target identification is selected. In the second part, Retinex image processing is applied to enhance the training data set’s image which is obscure due to severe light and shadow’s changes and re-training the model. In addition, the video of lower motorcycle speed used to demonstrate the identification influenced by speed is also included. In the third part, the detected broken pavement is tracked through Deep SORT to promote the identification stability and to result in different Track ID

title: Enhancing Cross-Border Financial Transactions with AI-Driven Blockchain Solutions - A Deep Neural Network Approach
authors: Manjula Pattnaik, Kathiresan Jayabalan, Rambabu Nalagandla, Deiveegan Arumugam, I Mohana Krishna, N.Padmavathi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Combining blockchain technology with artificial intelligence (AI) and more especially Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) produces a possibly revolutionary outcome meant to solve inefficiencies in global financial transactions. Conventional systems typically have drawbacks about foreign payments including costly fees, long processing times, and fraud risk. Solutions that boost the speed, openness, and security of transactions will help to fit the growing globalization of financial systems. This paper investigates how artificial intelligence systems driven by DNNs might be combined with blockchain systems to change world financial transactions. Blockchain technology guarantees that transaction records are transparent, unchangeable, and safe even while DNN models improve fraud detection, risk assessment, and dynamic optimization of transaction paths. The proposed system uses smart contracts to automatically follow global financial regulations. It also real-time optimizes money exchange rates using DNN-based predictive analytics. Our method consists in deep neural network algorithms trained on massive databases of transaction records and blockchain ledger systems. Among the main challenges the answer tackles are fraud prevention, scalability, and latency. Artificial intelligence improves operational efficiency as well as decision-making capacity; distributed ledger technology (DLT) promises decentralized and tamper-proof records. The results show that processing times and transaction costs have dropped, our capacity to prevent fraud has developed as well as our security has improved. Taken together, artificial intelligence and blockchain technology generate a scalable and effective architecture fit for use in many financial ecosystems.

title: Optimization of Material Allocation in a Dedicated Storage System Using a Linear Programming Model
authors: Ahmed Raecky Baihaqy, Nurhadi Siswanto, Tazkia Mahda Ummil Husna, Mutyara Shafa Tsany
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Warehouse is one of the most vital components in overall supply chain activities. Warehouse operation costs are contributed from four main activities: receiving, holding, picking, and shipping. Recognizing that order picking accounts for up to 50% of warehouse operational costs, the objective of this research is to minimize material handling and transportation expenses. The proposed solution employs a linear programming (LP) model, adapted for a system operating under a dedicated storage policy. The model's key parameters integrate the distance between storage locations, and the input/output (I/O) point with the measured frequency of material flow. This optimization approach, aided by LINGO software, resulted in a 69% reduction in total cost compared to the existing system, indicating a substantial technical improvement in warehouse material allocation efficiency.

title: Intelligent Human Detection for Efficient Disaster Response
authors: Ravindra Ghugare, Sayali Datir, Madiha Diwan, Sanika Kadam
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Search and rescue operations are essential for saving lives in the aftermath of natural and man-made disasters. This project presents the Intelligent Rescue Navigator, an innovative system aimed at improving the detection of individuals trapped in disaster-affected areas. By modernizing traditional rescue methods into real-time operations, this system harnesses advanced technologies, including artificial intelligence (AI), machine learning (ML), and unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs). The Intelligent Rescue Navigator utilizes AI algorithms alongside high-resolution cameras and thermal sensors mounted on UAVs to locate individuals in challenging environments. Our methodology incorporates a diverse array of datasets to train deep learning models capable of recognizing human shapes and movements, even when individuals are concealed by debris or in low-light conditions. The UAVs are designed for autonomous navigation, enabling efficient coverage of both extensive and intricate areas. Furthermore, the system includes a comprehensive communication interface that relays real-time information and GPS coordinates of detected individuals to ground rescue teams, thereby improving response times and resource allocation efficiency.

title: Federated Learning and Differential Privacy Preserve for Precise Disease Prediction
authors: Koushik Sundar, Ravikumar S, Babu R, Eugene Berna I, Vijay K
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: A new phase in biological and clinical research has come with machine learning and pattern-recognition-based systems, which have shown complex interaction networks between clinical parameters in demanding human diseases. Throughout this task it has been made clear that AI algorithms are now responsible for critical predictions in relation to disease susceptibility given some values on different factors such as gene expressions. But the requirement for training at a scale needs to be reconciled with medical organizations’ concerns about data privacy. It is inspired by federated learning, a scheme designed to protect data confidentiality in which neural networks are trained locally at various locations and the knowledge is pooled together to produce a global model. We incorporate the federated learning framework with differential privacy in our novel diabetes prediction scenario. The process includes shaping the data to acquire an artificial federated learning environment and applying the training algorithm on server and client sides. The TensorFlow-Privacy library also incorporates differential privacy, a technique for protecting sensitive data by selectively adding noise This study employs traditional performance metrics to assess the power of prediction from our model as a balancing act between accuracy and privacy. These comparisons show FL's advantage in maintaining privacy, sharing data, scalability, system, and communication efficiency, and regulatory compliance in comparison with traditional ML methods. Federated learning shows the potential to revolutionize disease prediction by enabling the medical community (doctors, academics, and data scientists) to concentrate on privacy-preserving predictive models for clinical decision-support systems despite interpretability issues and communications delays. The extent to which AI research now includes discussion of federated learning’s potential to transform the moral privacy-protecting engaging AI ecosystems becomes more evident.

title: Design and Optimization of Rocker Arm of Diesel Engines by Using Finite Element Method
authors: Choy Hau Yan, Shua Jun Jian, Lee Yoon Ket
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: A rocker arm is a reciprocating lever used as an internal combustion engine tool to transfer pushrod motion to the valve stem. This study investigates the failure of rocker arms in internal combustion engines and proposes seven modification ideas using Finite Element Analysis (FEA) simulation. The rocker arm model is designed using SOLIDWORKS and the material used is S-Glass Fiber. Static structural and modal analysis are performed on the modified designs to determine the best modification idea. The study explores material selection for rocker arms using four different materials - S-Glass Fiber, Aluminum Alloy 6061, Carbon Steel, and High-Density Polyethylene (HDPE). The results suggest that the best modification for static structural analysis is the model M6. S-Glass Fiber is recommended as the preferred material for rocker arms due to its high stiffness and low total deformation with higher natural frequency.

title: AI-Powered Smart Defect Detection System for Printed Circuit Boards
authors: Vijay K, Sharukesh M, Shrinithi S, Sivabalan T
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: In electronics manufacturing companies PCB defect detection is needed to see the quality of products whether it is good. basically in which this process is done by people and machine in which this takes so much time and also it is expensive. So overcome this problem ,we have implemented Smart Defect Detection System that can detect the PCB issue by using AI. In which we have used two models YOLOv11, MobileNet. YOLOv11 model is used for detecting the surface area problem like missing components, misalignment parts, solder bridges. MobileNet Model is basically used for checking the internal circuit patterns, in which small and hidden defects can be detected. These two models are combined with OpenCV and python to make a low cost and efficient inspection tool. In which it includes Graphical User Interface(GUI) so that non technical user can also able to upload the PCB images and can see the defected issues in the screen. This helps mainly for small electronic manufacturing companies where we can reduce their cost. In increases accuracy, reducing human errors and in which it makes the inspection process faster and reliable. the experiment result shows it is a good balance between performance, cost, flexibility, making it suitable for real time application.

title: AI-Powered Circular Economy: Towards More Sustainable and Efficient Industrial Operations
authors: Ankit Garg, Sarjue Pandita, Parth Balyan, DR.R. Srikala, Dr. Ravi Prakash, Dr. Neha Verma
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: This research examines the use of circular economy strategies—namely closed-loop systems and economic decoupling—within major industrial sectors such as refining, renewable energy, and electric vehicles. It uses a mixed-methods methodological approach, combining qualitative case studies with quantitative modeling through the Brazilian Land-Use Model for Energy Scenarios (BLUES) and the ARIMA forecast method, to evaluate the effect of circular practices on resource productivity and environmental sustainability. Additionally, artificial intelligence models based on neural networks were used to optimize process efficiency, distinguishing them from purely statistical predictive approaches. The outlook is encouraging: waste generation will fall by 20 to 25%, and recycling performance may increase from 50% to 83% within the next decade. The validity of these forecasts was verified, with measurement of errors such as MAPE and RMSE showing that the deviation between predictions and occurrence was below 5%. These findings illustrate the ways in which circular economy methods can assist us in consuming fewer new materials, reducing carbon emissions, and illustrate the need for technology and laws to continue to evolve. Companies can gain valuable insights that assist them in growing sustainably today and ensure that resources are responsibly utilized for generations to come by integrating these approaches.

title: Unveiling Factors Influencing University Students’ Intention to Adopt AI-Based Teaching Assistance
authors: Idris Gautama So, Razib Chandra Chanda, Miao Ling Zhang, Anderes Gui, Syaiful Bachri, Priscilia Calista Kowirya
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Artificial intelligence is reshaping higher education with its personalized teaching help fit to each student’s way of thinking. However, bringing AI into universities is still fairly limited, especially in developing nations. This study set out to discover the key factors that influence whether university students in these regions are willing to use AI-powered teaching assistance. This study conducted a quantitative survey, gathering data from 402 university students through targeted sampling. The information was analyzed using a statistical method of PLS-SEM. The Key Findings are: System Quality Matters, But Indirectly: We found that the quality of the AI system itself has a strong positive effect on both student satisfaction and the perception of service quality. However, surprisingly, it doesn't directly influence the student's overall attitude toward the AI. Service Quality is Vital: The quality of the service provided by the AI (how helpful and reliable it is) positively impacts both how satisfied students are and their overall attitude. Student satisfaction is a major predictor of both their attitude and their ultimate intention to use the AI tools. Attitude Predicts Action: A student's positive attitude strongly forecasts their behavioral intention— meaning if they like it, they plan to use it. The study suggests that effectively using of AI has significant potential for developing countries. It can be a crucial tool for solving educational challenges and dramatically improving student learning outcomes in higher education.

title: The Capacity of the Weighted Read Channel
authors: Omer Yerushalmi, Tuvi Etzion, Eitan Yaakobi
abstract: Nanopore sequencing is emerging as a powerful tool for storing digital information in DNA molecules. Its advantages over traditional sequencing methods such as support for long reads, scalability, and relatively low cost, making it an attractive and growing area of research. A fundamental aspect of this technology is the nature of its output: instead of reading individual bases in isolation, the sequencer observes overlapping segments of the DNA sequence due to inter-symbol interference (ISI). Similar readout mechanisms also arise in emerging memory technologies such as domain wall (racetrack) memory, where transverse-read techniques can sense the number of stored bits within a region without shifting any domains. Motivated by this analogy, we study theoretical models that capture the fundamental structure of such overlapping read processes. In this work, we study a simplified model of the nanopore sequencing process, known as the (ℓ, δ)-read channel. This channel operates by applying a sliding window of length ℓ across a DNA sequence and shifting it by δ positions after each read. The output of each window is a numerical value, which, in our model, corresponds to the sum (or Hamming weight) of the bases within that window. The collection of these outputs forms what we refer to as the read vector. Due to overlapping windows, multiple input sequences can map to the same read vector, leading to ambiguity. The main object of interest in this work is the capacity of the (ℓ, δ)-read channel, defined as the number of distinct output sequences that can be generate by this channel. Previous studies have determined the capacity for specific combinations of ℓ and δ, such as when ℓ ≤ δ, when ℓ is a multiple of δ, and for certain small values of ℓ and δ. In this study, we extend the current knowledge by addressing more general cases. Our first main result is a closed-form expression for the capacity when δ < ℓ < 2δ, given by: 1/δ log2 ( 1/2 (ℓ + 1 + √(ℓ + 1)2 − 4(ℓ − δ)(ℓ − δ + 1) )). This result is derived by analyzing a deterministic finite-state representation of the read channel and applying tools from graph theory, particularly the Perron–Frobenius Theorem. Next, we consider the case where ℓ ≥ 2δ and δ does not divide ℓ. For this setting, we construct a constrained code that yields an upper bound on the capacity, improving upon known bounds and further refining our understanding of this more complex regime. Finally, we extend the model to the two-dimensional setting. Motivated by the physical layout of some sequencing platforms and potential future applications, we propose and analyze a two-dimensional version of the weighted read channel and we present initial results on its capacity.

title: Co-deterioration Study of Nanoparticle-Modified Oil-Paper Insulation Systems Under Electric and Thermal Fields Based on Molecular Dynamics
authors: Haoxi Cong, Linghe Kong, Xuan Zhang, Junzhang Li, Xuefeng Hu, Qingmin Li, Yangchun Cheng, Li Xie, Hua Zheng, Xuelei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Natural ester insulating oils possess advantages such as high flash point and biodegradability. However, their low oxidative stability and high kinematic viscosity limit their application in transformers. During actual operation, transformers are subjected to the coupled effects of electric and thermal fields, which accelerate the degradation of the oil-paper insulation system. In this study, molecular dynamics simulations are employed to construct oil-paper insulation models modified with three types of nanoparticles, namely Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>, Fe<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">4</sub>, and TiO<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>. Systematic analyses are conducted on the elastic modulus and mean square displacement of insulating paper under various electric and thermal field conditions, as well as the fractional free volume and cohesive energy density of oil-paper insulation systems, and the shear viscosity of natural esters. The results indicate that all three types of nanoparticles can enhance intermolecular interactions and suppress deterioration processes under coupled electric and thermal fields. Among them, the Al<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sub>O<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">3</sub>-modified model exhibits the best overall performance. This study elucidates the microscopic mechanisms underlying nanoparticle-modified oil-paper insulation systems and provides theoretical support for improving the safe operation of transformer insulation systems.

title: Deadline-Driven Deterministic Wireless Communication for Time-Sensitive Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Mikael Gidlund, Tao Zheng
publish: IEEE Transactions on Industrial Cyber-Physical Systems
abstract: Time-triggered industrial cyber–physical control requires decode-by communication: information arriving after a fixed deadline is control-invalid and must be discarded. Conventional feedback-based wireless reliability mechanisms (e.g., HARQ) introduce random completion times, which complicates worst-case analysis and certification in time-sensitive industrial networks. This paper presents a deadline-driven coded reliability framework that enables a 5G/6G wireless hop to operate as a deterministic decode-by service with a bounded latency contract. In each control cycle, the payload is encoded into a fixed number <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n$</tex-math></inline-formula> of transmissions scheduled within a reserved window that ends at a strict cut-off <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$t^*$</tex-math></inline-formula>. To provision the minimal radio-time reservation for a target violation probability <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\epsilon$</tex-math></inline-formula>, we formulate an exact dynamic program under a Gilbert–Elliott loss model that maps channel evolution to cycle-level failure events. Based on this analysis, the proposed Minimal-Overhead Deterministic Scheduler (MODS) computes the smallest feasible transmission budget <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$n^*$</tex-math></inline-formula> satisfying the latency–reliability contract. Trace-driven evaluation using industrial 5G/6G measurements shows that the framework achieves a <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{-5}$</tex-math></inline-formula> violation probability while reducing deterministic airtime by up to 45% compared to replication-based baselines. By enforcing a fixed completion boundary, the approach eliminates stochastic latency tails by construction and enables certifiable wireless integration for TSN/DetNet systems. The results further show that decode-by performance is governed by burst-correlated losses rather than average error rates, and that efficient provisioning requires dimensioning transmission budgets against the burst-loss envelope. The proposed framework exposes an explicit latency–reliability trade-off through <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$(n^*,t^*)$</tex-math></inline-formula>, enabling predictable resource allocation and scalable admission of time-sensitive flows. These properties provide a practical path toward integrating wireless links into deterministic industrial networks without introducing timing uncertainty.

title: The Design and Fabrication of a Compact Band-Stop Filter Based on Bulk Acoustic Resonator
authors: Yuannian Li, Weipeng Xuan, Hong Jiang, Rui Ding, Long Chen, Peng Zheng, Wenjun Li, Lingling Sun, Hao Jin, Jikui Luo, Shurong Dong
publish: IEEE Microwave and Wireless Technology Letters
abstract: In this work, a compact bulk acoustic wave (BAW) resonator–based band-stop filter operating at 2 GHz was designed, fabricated, and characterized. The Mason model of the bulk acoustic resonator is used to design the <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\pi $</tex-math> </inline-formula>-topology configuration filter. To optimize filter performance and reduce area mismatch between series and shunt resonators, the series resonator is equivalently divided into two parallel resonators, effectively minimizing the area difference among the resonators that compose of the filter. The fabricated ladder-type filter achieves a center frequency of 2057 MHz with a 3 dB stopband width of 34 MHz and up to 20 dB rejection with only a 9 MHz sideband. The filter shows a passband insertion loss (IL) below 1.2 dB, with a minimum IL of 0.7 dB and band-stop rejection of more than 15 dB. Power-handling tests show that the filter can withstand up to 24 dBm of continuous sine-wave input power. The experimental and simulation results demonstrate that the proposed BAW-based stopband filter achieves low IL, high rejection, and excellent power endurance, providing a promising candidate for integration into next-generation RF front-end modules and compact high-frequency communication systems.
