title: Low-Power Wide-Area Networks (LPWAN) for IoT Applications: Engineering the Innovative Practices
authors: Narender Chinthamu, Krishna Kant Dixit, Mudit Mittal, Arun Pratap Srivastava, Akhilesh Kumar Khan, Rajeev Sobti
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: This paper explores the integration of Low-Power Wide-Area Network (LPWAN) technologies within the Internet of Things (IoT) ecosystem. As IoT applications proliferate, the need for efficient, low-power communication solutions that can operate over extensive areas becomes critical. LPWAN technologies, characterized by their long range and low power consumption, are well-suited to meet these demands. This study examines various LPWAN technologies, including both cellular and non-cellular systems, and assesses their performance, design specifications, and suitability for different IoT applications. Through a comprehensive review and real-world use case analysis, this research aims to provide insights into the optimization of LPWAN technologies to enhance IoT connectivity. The paper also discusses potential challenges and future research directions in the deployment of LPWAN solutions, contributing to the broader understanding and development of IoT infrastructure.

title: Architecture of an IoT–AI Digital Ecosystem for Air Quality Monitoring and Environmental Assessment
authors: Ali Abbasov, Elchin Aliyev, Ramin Rzayev, Asgar Almasov
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Control, Automation and Robotics (ICCAR)
abstract: This paper presents an Internet of Things (IoT)– Artificial Intelligence (AI)-based digital ecosystem for continuous air quality monitoring and environmental assessment in urban and ecologically sensitive regions of Azerbaijan. The system integrates distributed sensor networks, cloud storage, and intelligent analytics within a unified framework. Real-time and historical data are processed using machine learning models and fuzzy inference to estimate pollution levels, detect anomalies, and generate short-term forecasts. The platform incorporates automated data validation, adaptive model updating, and spatial– temporal analysis of pollutant dynamics. The approach was evaluated using multi-year datasets from urban monitoring stations and benchmarked against conventional statistical methods. Results demonstrate improved prediction accuracy and greater robustness under varying meteorological and emission conditions. The proposed architecture enables timely identification of high-risk pollution zones and supports evidence-based environmental management. Its modular and scalable design allows adaptation to regions with similar climatic and urban characteristics.

title: Incorporating Edge Computing in Wireless Sensor Networks for Efficient Data Processing
authors: Himanshu Rai Goyal, Yogendra Kumar, Zaid Alsalami, A. Kakoli Rao, Amit Srivastava, P. William
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: This paper explores the integration of Edge Computing with Wireless Sensor Networks (WSNs) to address the challenges posed by traditional centralized data processing architectures. WSNs, pivotal in applications like environmental monitoring, healthcare, and smart cities, face issues of latency, bandwidth inefficiency, and scalability due to the vast data they generate. Edge Computing offers a paradigm shift by processing data near its source, thus enhancing responsiveness and operational efficiency. We propose a novel framework that employs localized computing at the edge to optimize network performance. Through simulations and real-world experiments, we demonstrate how this integration reduces latency, conserves bandwidth, improves security, and enhances scalability. Our findings indicate significant improvements in network management and efficiency, confirming the benefits of Edge Computing in transforming WSNs into more robust, efficient, and scalable systems.

title: Detecting Ovarian Cancer Early using a State-of-the-Art Machine Learning Approach
authors: A M S V Sushma, A Aswini Priyanka, S. Koteswari, Ummiti Sreenivasulu, A N G Lakshmi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Ovarian cancer poses a great deal of diagnostic and treatment challenges; it appears with non-specific symptoms, is diagnosed late, and also resistant to treatment. Existing techniques of diagnosis, such as ultrasound, CT, and CA-125 biomarker, have limited sensitivity, as well as specificity, in the detection of early-stage disease. Standard imaging and invasive biopsy processes have a tendency of providing inconclusive outcomes, and treatment methods have challenges of resistance to chemotherapy and low efficacy of targeted therapy. The paper will fill these critically missing gaps by suggesting improved diagnostic models that would use improved imaging, such as MRI and PET scans, and liquid biopsy techniques to detect them, which are non-invasive and more accurate. We introduce a machine learning methodology based on the Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) and the Logistic Regression to automatically detect and classify ovarian cancer. The proposed model was tested on a dataset of 2,500 anonymized MRI scans with an 80 20 training testing split with 100 percent classification accuracy. We also address the issue of clinical deployment, such as the integration of the Electronic Health Record (EHR) system and regulatory compliance requirements. This paper shows how AI-powered diagnostic device can be used to support faster diagnoses, better patient outcomes, and revolutionize clinical practice in ovarian cancer.

title: Cyber Physical System Based Distributed Real Time Draining System for Smart Cities
authors: Avishek Golder, Simarpreet Kaur, Komal Sharma, Gaurav Kumar Bharti
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: In this era of technology, almost everything is now automated. Cyber physical systems play an important role in making our lives smarter and contributes the development of a smart city. A smart drainage system significantly contributes in building a smart city. The residents are currently facing issues due to the accumulation of excess water in the drain and water logging caused by the inadequate flow of drain water. To date, none of the existing research has addressed the simultaneous monitoring, cleaning, and decontamination of underwater drains within manholes. In this paper, our aim to solve this issue by using cyber physical systems. Our proposed system will automate the process of drainage cleaning and solve the problem of manual drain cleaning process. It will decrease the problems faced by manual drainage cleaning such as disease, death of workers, time consuming process etc.

title: When Inertia Drives Transparency: The Role of Business Environmental Uncertainty in Inert and Alert Firms’ Sustainability Disclosure
authors: Made Irma Lestari, Pandu Dwi Luhur Pambudi, Emny Harna Yossy, Netnapha Chamnisampan, Steven Walker Simangunsong, Arinda Cinta Lestari
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: The turbulence of business environmental uncertainty in Indonesia provides a timely context for discussing corporate sustainability. This study aims to examine the impact of business environmental uncertainty on sustainability disclosure across two groups of firms: alert and inert. Using the full population data of 12 industry sectors in Indonesia, consisting of 363 listed companies and a total of 1,089 firm-year observations (570 alert and 519 inert), we tested differences using the Chow Test and dummy-interaction regression. Multiple linear regression was applied to identify variations in effects. Results show that business environmental uncertainty significantly influences sustainability reporting in both groups. While both alert and inert firms demonstrate positive significant effects, the impact is stronger in inert firms. This finding aligns with legitimacy and signaling theory, suggesting that environmental uncertainty compels inert firms, being less adaptive to rely more heavily on sustainability disclosure as a defensive strategy. Conversely, alert firms, being more responsive, do not depend as strongly on disclosure for legitimacy. This study contributes novelty by distinguishing firms based on adaptive capacity, thereby enriching legitimacy, signaling, and organizational adaptation theory.

title: Green Goals Toning Fintech: A Literature Review
authors: Rachna, Manjit Kour
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: To enhance the financial sectors, it is essential to integrate policies, frameworks, practices, and strategies that will address both economic demands and social needs. The Environmental, Social, and Governance (ESG) pillars of sustainable finance fulfill businesses' responsibilities towards the environment and social welfare. Technological advancements are also being used to achieve the firm's sustainable objectives. Fintech is enhancing the functionality of financial products and services, but evolving needs have increased the call for a more sustainable approach, leading to the emergence of Green Fintech. Green Fintech merges financial technology with environmental and social requirements to promote sustainability. It is an intelligent advancement achieved by digitization and prioritizing sustainability as the main motivators. It creates eco-friendly technologies to enhance corporate conduct and optimize financial processes, along with green strategies to support brand sustainability.In order to understand the conceptual meaning of Green Fintech and other terms associated with it, the theories of a number of researchers have been reviewed in this paper. This paper also considers the Difficulties and Future Possibilities of Fintech practicing Green, which can be considered by experts, researchers, and the general public for further study.

title: Enhancing Reliability in Luxury Marketing Using AI, Big Data, and Financial Analytics: Infocom Technologies and Computational Strategies in the Digital Age
authors: Navleen Kaur, Smriti Sethi, Mehpreet Kaur, Naman Kalra, M Kashish
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: To strike a balance between exclusivity and accessibility, the digital transformation of luxury marketing has brought computational tactics that make use of big data, financial analytics, machine learning, and artificial intelligence (AI). This study investigates how financial modelling, neural networks, and AI-driven customisation improve luxury brand experiences while preserving status. The study illustrates important technological strategies through case studies of Gucci and Louis Vuitton, such as influencer analytics, AI-driven demand prediction for restricted online releases, financial forecasting for trends in the luxury industry, and virtual reality-enhanced shopping experiences. The results shed light on how luxury firms can use financial strategies and computational methods to successfully manage digital transition without sacrificing their basic principles. Furthermore, 203 luxury consumers' survey answers provide insights on how consumers view digital personalization, influencer partnerships, accessibility, and exclusivity in the premium sector. To assess the connections between digital strategy and customer behaviour, statistical studies are used, such as regression models, correlation analysis, and chi-square testing.

title: A Bibliometric Analysis of Artificial Intelligence enabled Predictive Analytics in Customer Retention and Credit Risk Management
authors: Richa Sharma, Aanchal Tyagi, Shaleen Suneja, Preshni Shrivastava, Nidhi Agarwal
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Customer retention has become an important aspect in today's competitive world as it helps avoid losses and increase long-term value which are the main goals of organizations. The organizations are eager to learn about the creditworthiness and retention of their customers. Artificial intelligence driven predictive analytics can help organizations in this regard. The study is aimed at conducting a bibliometric analysis of the research on artificial intelligence (AI)-enabled predictive analytics in customer retention and credit risk management domain. For the purpose of this study, 211 publications have been retrieved from Scopus database through a structured search of titles, abstracts, and keywords in September 2025. Further, VOSviewer software was used to map bibliographic coupling, Keyword trends and co-authoring networks in the dataset. The results reveal consistent growth in publications since 1997, followed by an increase in 2016 and recent years. The results revealed that the key themes are Fraud detection, machine learning, data mining and consumer behavior modelling. India and the US have emerged as the top two countries in terms of output and influence, despite the fact that institutional contributions are still dispersed. The results suggest for more coordinated institutional efforts with greater attention to ethical and regulatory concerns.

title: MU-MIA: Machine Unlearning for Membership Inference Attacks
authors: Hongming Yang, Guopu Zhu, Long Sun, Wei Jiang, Xinpeng Zhang, Tao Xiang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Information Forensics and Security
abstract: The widespread deployment of deep learning models across various applications has raised significant concerns regarding data privacy. Membership inference attacks (MIAs), a major privacy threat, aim to determine whether a specific sample is used during model training, thereby posing significant risks to sensitive information. Most existing MIA methods rely on the model’s final state output, overlooking the process by which the model memorizes training samples. To better exploit model memorization for MIAs, we propose a novel attack method called machine unlearning-based membership inference attack (MU-MIA). The proposed method introduces machine unlearning to incrementally reduce the model’s memorization of specific samples, generating a forgetting trajectory for each sample. The forgetting trajectory is composed of temporal variations in different metrics of the sample during machine unlearning. To distinguish member from non-member samples, we design a BiLSTM-based binary classifier with attention, which captures discriminative temporal patterns within each forgetting trajectory. Moreover, the machine unlearning phase of our attack is conducted under a zero-shot setting, which eliminates the need for any real data during the unlearning process, thereby improving the practicality and generalizability of the attack. We evaluate the proposed MIA method across different datasets and model architectures, and the comparative experimental results show that our method outperforms existing baseline attack methods.

title: Adaptive Power Electronics: Neuromorphic Control of Converters for Renewable Microgrids
authors: Anurag Shrivastava, Hassan M. Al-Jawahry, Sorabh Lakhanpal, Kanchan Yadav, B. Sireesha, Shashi Prakash Dwivedi
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Adaptive power-electronics control is essential for robust, efficient integration of distributed renewable resources into future microgrids. This paper proposes a neuromorphic control framework for power converters that leverages event-driven spiking neural networks (SNNs) and lightweight on-chip learning to deliver low-latency, energy-efficient, and fault-tolerant regulation of voltage and current in heterogeneous renewable microgrids. The proposed approach (i) maps converter dynamics to compact SNN architectures that exploit temporal encoding of measurements, (ii) employs local, online adaptation rules inspired by biological plasticity for rapid set-point tracking and disturbance rejection, and (iii) integrates with conventional hierarchical microgrid controls (droop and secondary regulation) to ensure stability and power sharing. Analytical stability arguments are developed for the neuromorphic-controller-in-the-loop using Lyapunov-inspired energy metrics and are complemented by a suite of hardware-aware performance bounds that quantify latency, spike-rate energy, and robustness to parameter drift. Simulation and hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) emulations against representative photovoltaic and battery-sourced microgrids demonstrate that the neuromorphic controller attains comparable regulation accuracy to model-predictive and adaptive-neural controllers while reducing active control-compute energy and response latency under abrupt load changes and communications degradations. The results indicate that neuromorphic control offers a promising pathway to embed adaptive intelligence directly within converters, enabling resilient, distributed renewable microgrids with reduced communications dependence and improved edge energy efficiency.

title: Fast and Accurate Beam Direction Calibration Method for Phased Array Antennas Using PCA
authors: Zengdi Bao, Ziwei Peng, Yinbo Zhao, Liang Zhang, Zaiping Nie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation and Measurement
abstract: Various methods have been proposed in the literature to calibrate the amplitudes and phases of phased array channels. However, physical factors—such as mutual coupling between antenna elements, quantization errors in phase shifters, and residual channel calibration inaccuracies—often interact to cause the actual beam direction to deviate from the designed angles. These deviations typically become more pronounced at larger scanning angles. Consequently, accurate beam direction calibration is critical for phased arrays employed in high-precision angle-of-arrival estimation. In this work, we propose a fast and accurate offline calibration method based on principal component analysis (PCA) for phased arrays whose channels have been calibrated. By extracting beam direction error features at a subset of angles and fitting their principal components, our method accurately predicts beam direction errors across all required angles. Experimental results show that, when measuring at only 1/22 of the angles, the three standard deviations (3σ) of the differences between predicted and measured beam directions remain below 0.25º for 12×12-element arrays within a ±25º range in both azimuth and elevation planes. Compared with typical interpolation methods, our approach substantially improves the efficiency of beam direction calibration and the underlying mechanisms are thoroughly analyzed.

title: Generalized Semi-analytical Modelling for Eddy Current Testing Based on Reflection and Transmission Coefficients at Heterogeneous Interfaces
authors: Zhengshuai Liu, Yong Li, Wenlong Gao, Bingjie Su, Zhenmao Chen
publish: 2025 IEEE Far East NDT New Technology &amp; Application Forum (FENDT)
abstract: In an effort to clarify the influence of the ferrite core and defect on the propagation of the electromagnetic field in eddy current testing, the reflection and transmission characteristics at their boundaries are thoroughly investigated based on the extended truncated region eigenfunction expansion (ETREE) method. Building upon this, generalized expressions of reflection and transmission coefficients are formulated at two types of heterogeneous interfaces, namely homogeneous-homogeneous interfaces and homogeneous-heterogeneous interfaces, which are subsequently applied to the theoretical modeling of eddy current testing involving a ferrite-core probe and a multi-layer conductive medium with a defect. The accuracy of the theoretical model as well as the reflection and transmission coefficients at both homogeneous-homogeneous interfaces and homogeneous-heterogeneous interfaces is validated through experimental results.

title: Design and Performance Evaluation of a Compact UWB Antenna for Breast Tumor Detection
authors: V. N. Koteswara Rao Devana, M. Hemantha Rao, K. Moulya Sri, K. Nagarjuna, D. Tarun Deva Harish, Ch. V. Ravi Sankar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: This paper describes the design and numerically evaluating a small Ultra-Wideband (UWB) radiator for utilization in detecting breast tumors. The proposed antenna is made on a FR4 substrate and works over 3.33 GHz to 13.23 GHz. This covers the entire UWB spectrum set aside for imaging applications. CST Studio Suite 2025 with a time-domain solver is used to design the antenna and verify its performance to make sure to get an adequate broadband characteristic. The simulated results show that the reflection coefficient (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{1 1}}$</tex>) stays below <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{- 1 0 ~ d B}$</tex> across the entire operating band, which means that the impedance matching is very good. The antenna has stable radiation properties and a pattern that stays uniform over the UWB range. Also, the radiation efficiency ranges from 60 % to 82.3 %, and the highest gain achieved is 5.22 dBi. To ensure safe operation near biological tissues, a Specific Absorption Rate (SAR) analysis is done, and the results show that the values are within internationally accepted safety limits. This shows that the radiator is suitable for biomedical microwave imaging applications.

title: Research on Leak Source Localization in Complex Pipelines Based on Acoustic Emission Technology
authors: Gao Zhigang, Lu Ronghao, Zhong Weicheng, Yuan Xumin, Zhang Lanzhu, Li Ke
publish: 2025 IEEE Far East NDT New Technology &amp; Application Forum (FENDT)
abstract: Pipelines are widely employed in the petroleum, natural gas, and chemical industries due to their high efficiency and low cost. However, leakage frequently occurs at components such as valves and flanges in complex pipeline structures due to sealing failures or impact fractures, posing significant safety risks. This study conducts experimental research on leak source localization in complex pipelines using acoustic emission (AE) technology. Lead break tests are performed to analyze the propagation velocity and attenuation characteristics of AE signals. A multi-level analysis combining time-domain, frequency-domain, and wavelet transform methods reveals that the amplitude and frequency band distribution of leakage signals attenuate progressively during propagation. To enhance signal quality, a parameter optimization method based on vector weighted averaging is proposed and validated within the framework of variational mode decomposition (VMD). Effective signal modes are selected using sample entropy and correlation coefficients, followed by signal reconstruction through a wavelet thresholding denoising algorithm, resulting in a significant improvement in localization accuracy. Furthermore, the impact of signal attenuation on localization errors is investigated, and an optimized sensor placement scheme tailored for complex pipelines is proposed and experimentally verified.

title: Hybrid Frequency Regulation in Multi-Area Power Systems with Renewable Energy Sources, Electric Vehicles, and Cyber-Attack Mitigation: a MATLAB/Simulink Implementation Study
authors: Prakriti Kumar Srivastava, Pankaj Rai
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: Modern power systems are confronted with more than ever before in terms of frequency stability challenges as the utilization of renewable energy sources (RES) is beginning to rise in large volumes alongside low inertia nature. This paper will provide an extensive hybrid frequency control plan to multi-area interconnected power systems that will incorporate proportional-integralderivative (PID) controllers at the fractional-order, aggregators of electric vehicles (EV) to provide virtual inertia, and cyber-resilient architectures to avert denial-ofservice (DoS) and false data injection (FDI) attacks. Simulink was used to model and simulate the proposed system with realistic nonlinearities such as governor dead bands, generation rate limits, and communication weaknesses. The outcome of simulations shows that the hybrid FOPID controller (with EV aggregators) reduces the frequency overshoot by 67 %, and the settling time is 3.5 times shorter than that of the standard PID controllers. In the case of hybrid cyber-attacks, the Kalman filter-based detection and <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$H_{\infty}$</tex> control switching preserve a nominal performance of 93 %. The experiment confirms the methodology with different system inertia (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$H=5-8$</tex> seconds), levels of RES penetration (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$30-50 \%$</tex>), and attack strengths, and offers a practical idea of the next-generation implementation of smart grids.

title: Opacity-Informed UAV Navigation with 3D Gaussian Splatting and Model Predictive Control
authors: Jiahao Tu, Zhongrui Hu, Chenghao Yao, Jingyi Lu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: This paper presents an integrated UAV navigation system that combines 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for environment mapping, A* algorithm for global path planning, and Model Predictive Control (MPC) for trajectory tracking with obstacle avoidance. The 3DGS-generated Gaussian sphere map provides rich geometric information including opacity and scale parameters, which are utilized to construct a collision cost function embedded into the MPC optimization framework. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed system achieves significant improvements in both path planning and trajectory tracking performance. The A* algorithm with Gaussian opacity-aware cost function reduces the integrated occupancy probability by 26.7% compared to traditional grid-based methods. The MPC controller with collision cost achieves a position RMSE of 0.030m, representing a 66.3% improvement over the baseline MPC without collision cost. The integrated system operates at an effective control frequency of 75Hz with an average single-step computation time of 13.3ms, meeting real-time requirements for UAV navigation in GNSS-denied environments.

title: Predicting Waterborne Related Diseases in Rural Jharkhand Using Machine Learning Until 2030
authors: Shweta Bharti, Ravi Kumar Burman, Abhishek Kumar, Muskan Pandey, Usha Desai, Biresh Kumar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: This paper demonstrates an artificial intelligence enabled predictive model to forecast the risk of waterborne diseases in rural districts of Jharkhand, India which are estimated through the year 2030. The research combines the heterogeneous data such as hospital-reported cases of Integrated Disease Surveillance Programme (2017-2025), rainfall and temperature of the India Meteorological Department and NASA POWER, water supply of the Jal Jeevan Mission, and demographical forecast of the Census of India. The preprocessing of the data complicated the imputation of missing data, normalization and feature engineering of lag variables, rolling averages, rate of positivity, and seasonal encodings. Exploratory Data Analysis predicts high risk areas, monsoon related peaks, connected with health and climate. A Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) model achieving 81.8% accuracy, macro F1-score of 0.771, AUROC of 0.802 on the 2024-2025 test set. Forecasts for 2026-2030 indicated elevated average high-risk probabilities (<tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\sim 0.47$</tex>) compared with the historical mean (0.41). The proposed system will deliver probabilistic early warnings that will be used to conduct surveillance and facilitate climate-sensitive in the under-resourced area in the field of public health.

title: Cross-Dataset Performance Evaluation of Multiple Faster R-CNN Models for Generalized Pothole Detection Application
authors: Krish Patel, Anshul Patel, Harsh Kapadia
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The progressive deterioration of road infrastructure, specifically pothole formation, presents critical risks to vehicular safety and logistics efficiency in rapidly developing regions like India. Although deep learning-based object detection has advanced significantly, the robustness of these models when subjected to the domain shifts encountered in real-world deployments remains insufficiently characterized. This study presents a rigorous three-experiment framework to evaluate the cross-dataset generalization of Faster R-CNN variants Namely ResNet50, ResNet101, and ResNeXt101 equipped with Feature Pyramid Networks (FPN). Performance baselines were established on a primary composite dataset, followed by source-aware hyperparameter optimization to stabilize feature representation. Extensive cross-domain evaluations on multiple independent target datasets reveal a measurable generalization gap, with performance degrading by over 20 % in high-noise environments. While the ResNeXt101-FPN architecture demonstrates the highest resilience across unseen domains, the ResNet50 variant provides a superior balance of accuracy and inference speed for edge deployment. These findings quantify the impact of environmental domain shift and emphasize the necessity of crossdataset validation for the reliable deployment of automated road monitoring systems.

title: Performance Evaluation of Non-Invasive Attention Classification Using Physiological and Ocular Metrics
authors: Akshit Munjal, Divneet Singh Kapoor, Kiran Jot Singh
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The correct identification of cognitive attention states is critical for intelligent systems to perform optimally in applications like e-learning, driver monitoring, and humancomputer interaction. In this paper, the authors present a machine learning-based approach to classify three types of attention states: Sustained, Alternating, and Divided Attention. The classification is done by employing non-invasive physiological parameters and ocular responses. The data was collected using a Gazepoint eye tracker and a Galvanic Skin Response sensor. Relevant features like GSR values, fixations, and mean fixation duration were extracted. The dataset was normalized using the z-score method. The SMOTE technique was used to handle class imbalance. The performance of the proposed system was validated using four classifiers: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, XGBoost, and K-Nearest Neighbors. The experimental results showed that the KNN algorithm produced the best results with an accuracy of 96.13 %, followed by XGBoost with 96.00 %, and then the Random Forest algorithm with 95.86 %. The Logistic Regression algorithm produced the lowest accuracy of 49.04 %. The features were also ranked with the GSR values contributing the most to the classification at 70.5 %, followed by the fixation count at 16.0 %, and then the mean fixation duration at 13.5 %.

title: CS-MSENet: Centered Linear Attention and Edge Enhancement for UAV Detection
authors: Haibo Wang, Qingsong Meng, Lixin Qiao
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: To address the challenges of low resolution and cluttered backgrounds for small objects in UAV aerial imagery, this paper proposes a small object detection method based on an improved YOLO26m model. By introducing the Centered Squared Linear Self-Attention (CS-LSA) mechanism, the model achieves efficient global feature modeling while maintaining linear computational complexity. Furthermore, a Multi-scale Spatial Edge-enhancement Network (MSENet) is integrated to effectively sharpen the model's sensing precision for the boundary features of tiny targets. Experimental results demonstrate that the improved YOLO26m model strikes an optimal balance between detection accuracy and real-time performance, providing reliable technical support for intelligent inspection on resource-constrained embedded UAV platforms.

title: Shapley-Driven Federated Edge Intelligence for Fair and Secure Model Aggregation in Industry 4.0 Cyber-Physical Systems
authors: Natrayan L
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: Industry 4.0 is the collaboration of multiple sectors in the industry where the IoT (Internet of Things) based devices are deployed to collect information in a particular sector. Federated Learning system efficiently utilizes the network load by transferring the gradient of the information sensed rather than the raw content of the sectors. An edge node helps in processing the information of the local model that is deployed by the global model. The gradients from the local models are aggregated, and the gradients are transmitted to the global model. In the aggregation, the unequal data distribution and non-iiid data handling become more challenging. Hence, this work proposes the Shapley-based game theoretic approach to the aggregation of the gradients in the global model of the FL system. The coalition utility function is used for the sector-wise probability distribution, and the Shapley aggregation is done using it. The proposed work shown 95.12% accuracy due to the collaboration of the sector-wise distribution with Jain's Fairness Index of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{0. 9 3}$</tex>.

title: Enhancing Manifold Convexity in Deep MRI Image Clustering Using Adversarial Learning
authors: Sawsan Hilal, Nairouz Mrabah, Zyad Atef Allam, Riadh Ksantini
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: Till today, no cure has been found for some diseases such as cancer and early detection remains the only reliable way to increase the survival chances for patients. Recently, machine learning has been used to aid in the diagnosis of large numbers of patients, especially using medical image data such as Magnetic Resonance Imaging images. However, due to the difficulty of obtaining large and labelled datasets, clustering techniques have been favored in this field of research with particular interest in deep clustering which has proven capable of understanding and accurately grouping highly complex data. One of the most recent developments in deep clustering came in the form of the dynamic auto-encoder (DynAE), which solves a historic problem that faced autoencoders (AEs) prior. However, it suffers from feature twist, a phenomenon which causes the cluster boundaries to flatten and become coarse and more complex which in turn hinders the clustering performance. This study proposes a new model (AdversFAE) using adversarial learning to combat feature drift in the auto-encoders by utilizing adversarial constrained interpolation in the clustering phase to escape feature twist. The proposed model was tested on six datasets from the MedMNIST v2 collection of medical datasets. The results showed that the proposed model outperformed the convolutional dynamic autoencoder (ConvDynAE) in terms of accuracy and F1_score. Interestingly, AdversFAE achieved an accuracy of 85.9%, 66.9%, and 53.5% on PneumoniaMNIST, BloodMNIST, and RetinaMNIST respectively compared to $62.1 \%, 46.3 \%$, and 31.5% achieved by ConvDynAE on the same datasets. The proposed changes proved effective in improving the convexity of cluster boundaries and hence the clustering performance.

title: Multi-Agent Paradigms for Privacy Preservation in Federated Learning: A Review
authors: Rahul H.Bhole, Sachin R. Sakhare
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: In the recent years Federated Learning has emerged as a groundbreaking approach to train machine learning models using edge devices by ensuring that data is localized and central data aggregation is reduced. Despite the fact that this inherently provides a layer of privacy, it cannot be exempt from potential breaches or leaks during the training and aggregation phases. In this review, we examine how intelligent agents can augment privacy measures by integrating multi-agent systems with federated learning, emphasizing the innovative intersection between these two fields. In this paper, we examine the core functionalities of agents, from monitoring data exchanges to verifying node authenticity to proactively responding to anomalies, and provide an assessment of what is available in the state-of-the-art methodologies for using these agents. We highlight the challenges and potential future directions in harnessing multi-agent systems to enhance privacy in federated learning. In our opinion, our work illustrates the promise and complexity of this interdisciplinary crossroads and provides a foundation for future progress in developing robust, privacy-respecting federated learning infrastructure capable of meeting the needs of our students.

title: Efficient and Secure Watermarking for Encrypted Images Using LHE and DWT
authors: Ch. Praveen Kumar, Arla Gopala Krishna, Sanjay Kumar, Pandu Sowkuntla
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: Multimedia storage and image sharing over cloud platforms have increased the need for secure and privacy-preserving image authentication. Traditional watermarking methods either operate in the plaintext domain or rely on Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE), which incurs high computational cost and limits real-time applicability. To address these issues, this paper proposes an efficient watermarking framework that integrates Lightweight Homomorphic Encryption (LHE) with the Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT), enabling watermark embedding and extraction directly in the encrypted domain without decryption. The host image is encrypted using LHE and decomposed using DWT, where the watermark is embedded in the low-frequency LL sub-band to ensure high imperceptibility and robustness against common attacks. Compared to FHE- based methods, LHE significantly reduces computational time and memory usage. Experimental results on grayscale images demonstrate high visual quality in terms of PSNR and SSIM, along with reliable watermark extraction. The proposed method is suitable for privacy-sensitive applications such as medical imaging, cloud storage, and digital rights management.

title: Lightweight Thermal Fault Diagnosis in Induction Motors: A Comparative Study of CNNs and Depthwise-Separable CNNs
authors: Jimmy Palomino-Huilcapi, Mahmoud Kassas, Mohammad A. Abido
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: This paper investigates lightweight thermal fault detection and diagnosis (FDD) for induction motors, utilising Infrared Thermography (IRT) and compact deep neural networks. We use consistent pipeline to compare a standard convolutional neural network (CNN) with a depthwise–separable convolutional neural network (DS–CNN) across depths $d \in\{2,3,4\}$, employing purely geometric augmentation to maintain radiometric integrity, and evaluate using two protocols: a fixed $50 / 50$ train–test split that simulates limited training data and K-fold cross-validation. Experiments are performed on a publicly available motor thermography dataset featuring eleven different operating and fault conditions. At a consistent depth of $d=4$, the CNN reaches an accuracy of $96 \%$, while the DS–CNN attains $95 \%$, using approximately $25 \%$ of the parameters—similar results are shown in the cross-evaluation. Analysis of the confusion matrix reveals clear class separation overall, with some residual confusion mainly in low-severity inter–turn short–circuit fault (ITSC) cases. The results show that using DS–CNN preserves essential thermography cues, while significantly reducing the size of the model.

title: Corrigendum: Rethinking Teaching Evaluation Reports: Designing AI-transformed Student Feedback for Instructor Engagement
authors: Ruoxi Shang, Keri Mallari, Wei Bin Au Yeong, Ken Yasuhara, Anthony Tang, Gary Hsieh
publish: Proceedings of the ACM on Human-Computer Interaction
abstract: None

title: Petra: Enhancing Efficiency in Privacy-Preserving Three-Party Relational Analytics for Private Data
authors: Ziwei Peng, Kaiping Xue, Jingcheng Zhao, Jinjiang Yang, Yingjie Xue
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dependable and Secure Computing
abstract: As organizations increasingly rely on collaborative relational analytics over private databases, secure multi-party computation (MPC) has emerged as a key solution to process private data. While numerous schemes optimize many MPC-based algorithms for complex relational queries, the basic MPC operations underlying relational computation, such as comparisons, still cost high communication overhead and latency. This imposes a significant bottleneck. To further enhance practicality and efficiency, we aim to reduce the overhead of using MPC, enabling more efficient computation of relational operators and query execution. We propose Petra, a three-party model solution for privacy-preserving relational analytics. Petra introduces new primitives of efficient equality and inequality operations designed for databases. These primitives leverage a pseudorandom transformation and a “shuffle-and-compare” working pattern. Then, by composing these primitives and designing relational operator protocols including SELECT, aggregation, Join, and Group-by, Petra enables efficient execution of SQL operators. Importantly, Petra provides different security guarantees compared to typical MPC protocols, as it allows a limited form of leakage to one of the computing parties. This trade-off is central to Petra's design. Experimental results demonstrate that Petra significantly improves query performance compared to existing approaches and scales to complex analytics over large databases.

title: The electric field distortion characteristics and failure analysis of vehicle-mounted cable terminals under overvoltage impulses
authors: Tiangeng Li, Song Xiao, Guangning Wu, Jan Sykulski, Xiao Liu, Dongli Xin, Ye Cao, Qifeng Wang, Yujun Guo, Xueqin Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Dielectrics and Electrical Insulation
abstract: Vehicle-mounted cable terminals for high-speed trains endure recurrent transient overvoltage stresses induced by frequent operations of vacuum circuit breakers (VCB). Owing to interfacial complexities existing in the multi-layered composite insulation of the cable terminal, frequent overvoltage impulses may precipitate severe electric field distortion and partial discharges at interlaminar air gaps, ultimately shortening the lifespan of the terminal. Herein, the distorted electric field of cable terminals is studied through finite element simulation, and an improved inverse power model (IPM) that takes into account the distorted electric field is proposed to elucidate the coupled effects of overvoltage and gap defects on internal field distributions as well as to evaluate the lifespan of cable terminals. Through multi-physical simulations and partial discharge tests, it has been demonstrated that terminals with air gaps exhibit extreme field distortion, exceeding 20 times the air breakdown threshold at the main insulation-stress tube interface under transient overvoltage. Partial discharge results reveal distinctive ‘rabbit-ear’ shaped distributions, showing exponential discharge with voltage elevation. Computations based on the modified IPM reveal that significant degradation in terminal service life commences at the VCB switching-on and switching-off phase angles of 27° and 35°, respectively, with the lifespan of cable terminals exhibiting exponential decay as these phase angles increasing.

title: Federated Learning–Enabled Collaborative Beamforming for NGSO Direct-to-Vehicle Systems
authors: Almoatssimbillah Saifaldawla, Eva Lagunas, Madyan Alsenwi, Symeon Chatzinotas
publish: IEEE Transactions on Machine Learning in Communications and Networking
abstract: Non-geostationary satellite orbit (NGSO) systems are expected to play a central role in enabling direct satellite-to-vehicle (DS2V) connectivity. However, dense spectrum reuse and the coexistence of multiple NGSO constellations introduce severe co-frequency interference (CFI), which is further exacerbated by fast satellite motion and short channel coherence times. In this paper, we propose a federated learning (FL)–enabled collaborative receive beamforming framework for DS2Vs (FedSatBF). In FedSatBF, a serving satellite aggregates locally trained beamforming models from a subset of vehicular user terminals (VUTs) via federated averaging and broadcasts a global model that can be directly applied by both participating and non-participating VUTs under similar CFI conditions. An attention-based beamforming architecture is adopted as the base model for training local models at each VUT, with estimation-free sample covariance matrices as input, thereby eliminating the need for explicit estimation of the interference channel or the direction of arrival. The local model is trained via a reward-based learning objective in which the loss is the negative measured post-beamforming signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR), so that no ground-truth weight, no synthetic geometry-derived label, and no interferer’ channel state information (CSI) is provided or needed during training. The proposed framework is explicitly designed to operate within the limited channel coherence time imposed by NGSO mobility. We investigate the impact of key FL hyperparameters, including per-coherent block communication rounds, per-round training data, per-VUT local epochs, VUT participation, and geographical VUT clustering, on convergence speed, interference nulling capability, and computational efficiency. Simulation results demonstrate that FedSatBF achieves near-optimal signal-to-interference-plus-noise ratio (SINR) performance, within 0.1 [dB] of an optimal zero-forcing beamformer (ZFBF) used here as a perfect-CSI oracle upper bound, and significantly outperforms conventional maximum-ratio combining (MRC), all while using only a moderate FL hyperparameter setting. Moreover, the global model exhibits strong generalization to non-participating VUTs and remains effective under reduced settings, highlighting the practicality of FL-based collaborative beamforming for the next generation of DS2V systems.

title: Radiation Resistant Reinforcement and Integration Technology for 650V Half Bridge Gate Drive circuit
authors: Ning Tang, Liqiang Ding, Wei Li
abstract: Within the space electronics industry, several strategies have been implemented to mitigate radiation effects in integrated circuit fabricating processes, including radiation resistant reinforcement through process technology alterations and through circuit design, layout, and architecture. In this work, multi-method has been used to reinforce the high voltage half bridge driving circuit radiation resistance ability. These methods include fabricating in 1.0 m 650 V SOI (Silicon-On-Insulator) BCD (Bipolar-CMOS-DMOS) process, using ring gate devices, add negative feedback regulation loop and layout optimization. This work analyzes the total irradiation dose (TID) induced performance shifts in three critical circuits of the half bridge gate driving chip and uses techniques to repair and improve the gate driving circuit performance. The three circuits include: 15 V/650 V level shift circuit, 650 V ring LDMOS (Laterally-Diffused-Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor) and high/low side driving circuit. Experiment results shown that the proposed design circuit with a chip area of 3.88 mm × 2.80 mm can achieve a TID close to 100 krad(Si).

title: Single-Stage Switched Capacitor and Inductor-based Modified Totem-Pole PFC Converter with High Step-Down Voltage Capability for LEV Chargers
authors: Sushant Gupta, Vignesh Kumar Vethanayagam, Karthikeyan Anbalagan
abstract: In this article, a single-stage bridgeless switched-capacitor and inductor (SCI)-based modified totem-pole (MTP) PFC converter is proposed for light electric vehicle (LEV) charging applications. The proposed converter enables high step-down voltage conversion over an extended output range by integrating switched-capacitor and switched-inductor techniques within a totem-pole structure. Conventional boost-type PFC converters inherently provide step-up operation, requiring an additional DC-DC stage to obtain low output voltages, which increases component count, losses, and cost. To address this limitation, the proposed topology achieves high step-down conversion in a single stage, while reducing device stress and improving efficiency. A simplified control strategy using a single PWM signal further reduces implementation complexity. The feasibility of the proposed converter is validated through MATLAB/Simulink simulations and a 1-kW hardware prototype. Experimental results demonstrate high efficiency, low THD, and stable operation under steady-state and dynamic conditions with a 230 V, 50-Hz input supply.

title: A Soft Worm Robot for Sub-Centimeter Scale Pipeline Inspection_supp6-3699247.mp4
authors: Yitong Zhou
abstract: Inspection of sub-centimeter pipelines with continuous spatial curvatures remains challenging due to extreme geo metric confinement. This paper presents an ultralight, trunkless worm-inspired robot actuated by a single twisted and coiled actuator (TCA) specifically optimized for short-range inspection within localized extreme spatial bottlenecks. By integrating actuation and structural support into a single TCA and eliminating rigid trunks and auxiliary recovery mechanisms, the robot achieves a compact and lightweight design, with a mass of 0.5–1.1 g, a length of 32 mm, and a diameter of 4–10 mm. Directional peristaltic locomotion is enabled by a wedge-based micro silicone frustum foot that provides passive frictional anisotropy without complex control. A simplified mechanics-based anchoring model captures the dominant directional friction asymmetry induced by foot geometry and guides parameter optimization. Experiments demonstrate a maximum friction anisotropy ratio of 4.75, enabling stable crawling at 0.40 mm/s horizontally and 0.13 mm/s vertically in 4–9 mm pipelines, including complex three dimensional curved configurations. Blind inspection experiments with an onboard micro-camera further validate the robot's potential for inspection in highly confined pipeline environments.

title: Integrating Lateral Transportation and Collaborative Energy Scheduling: A Bi-Level Programming Approach for Port Waste-Energy Management
authors: Junyu Yu, Ji Quan, Xianjia Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: This study introduces a novel port waste transportation and energy scheduling coupled system, addressing existing research limitations that primarily focus on isolated waste transportation or disposal processes and lack integration of waste-to-energy technology with port energy management systems. The system integrates multiple treatment facilities equipped with waste-to-energy technology and multiple waste collection stations, establishing a dynamic link between solid waste transportation quantities and the energy output of treatment facilities, while considering collaborative transportation of solid waste and collaborative energy scheduling among facilities. To balance the conflicting objectives of port authority and treatment facilities, a bi-level programming model is proposed. After transforming the asymmetric Nash bargaining game and applying Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, the model is solved using commercial solvers. Comparative analysis of different cases under uncertain renewable energy generation and electrical load conditions shows that integrating lateral transportation and collaborative energy scheduling achieves the lowest economic costs and highest environmental benefits. Specifically, compared to the baseline Case 1, Case 4, which integrates both lateral transportation and collaborative energy scheduling, reduces the average cost of the port authority by 0.95%, the average total cost of treatment facilities by 54.39%, the average carbon emissions of the port authority by 3.63%, the average total carbon emissions of treatment facilities by 11.94%, and the average electricity purchased by treatment facilities from the distribution grid by 31.44%. The proposed system offers viable solutions for the sustainable development of port waste management and energy scheduling.

title: Vehicle Classification Based on Multi-Frequency Impedance Magnetic Profiles in Distance Domain
authors: Zbigniew Marszalek, Filip Hallo, Dominik Rzepka, Krzysztof Duda
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Vehicle magnetic profile (signature) acquired from inductive loop embedded in a road is a well-established signal for vehicle classification. This paper demonstrates how classification errors can be almost entirely suppressed by the Multi-Frequency Impedance Measurement (MFIM) system along with the proposed application of data fusion and artificial neural network. Two slim and two wide inductive loops operating at different measurement frequencies are employed to acquire the magnetic signatures, extracting not only the reactance but also the resistance component. The influence of vehicle speed is eliminated by scaling the magnetic profile from the time domain into the distance domain. This allows efficient pattern learning application directly on the signature samples, without the necessity of feature extraction. Furthermore, a comprehensive optimization of the classification process is presented, including the impact of signal resolution in distance domain. It is confirmed, by means of extensive tests, that the number of samples, that are the classification features, in distance domain can be reduced significantly up to 40 times, in respect to original 1 cm resolution, and still a high classification accuracy is preserved. Data fusion from different channels, i.e. different inductive loops and measurement frequencies, is investigated using an artificial neural network classifier. Finally, an energy-efficient spiking neural network is employed, and tested.

title: CFP-WMP: A Coarse-to-Fine Environmental Perception Framework for Walking Mode Prediction
authors: Chaoyu Song, Chao Chen, Xiaoxu Zhang, Jian Xu
abstract: Walking mode prediction is a critical intelligent capability for powered lower-limb prostheses to achieve smooth transitions across different terrains. Inspired by human visual anticipation, this paper proposes CFP-WMP, a hierarchical coarse-to-fine environmental perception framework. The system operates through four integrated modules. Initially, a coarse perception module identifies potential transition-critical terrains, triggering a fine perception module for high-precision local mapping only when a target enters the projected heading area; this selective activation ensures computational efficiency via a low-power standby state. Concurrently, a foothold prediction module estimates subsequent contact points based on historical trajectories. Finally, a locomotion mode prediction module fuses terrain geometry with these predicted footholds to anticipate gait transitions (e.g., from level ground to stair ascent), providing essential feed-forward guidance for gait control. Deployed on an NVIDIA Jetson AGX Orin, the framework achieved an average prediction accuracy of 98.92% across diverse lighting and weather conditions. With a processing latency of 92 ms per frame, the proposed framework demonstrates significant potential for real-time control, ensuring safer and more natural gait transitions for amputees in complex environments.

title: Toward Efficient Uplink Access in 6G LEO Satellite Networks: A Heartbeat-Based Design
authors: Zhenyu Cao, Swades De, Hu Jin, Jun-Bae Seo
abstract: Low Earth orbit (LEO) satellite integration is a key component of 6G non-terrestrial networks, but uplink (UL) access in LEO is fundamentally constrained by long round-trip time (RTT), rapid Doppler variation, and finite beam dwell time. For intermittent session traffic, the dominant cost is no longer a single random-access (RA) collision, but the repeated cold-start re-entry required after idle periods. Existing approaches therefore fail at opposite extremes: contention-based random access suffers from RTT-amplified retries, while terrestrial contention-free schemes assume warm synchronization or persistently reserved resources. This article identifies an intermediate UL access regime for session-oriented intermittent traffic and proposes a connection-lite (CL) mechanism that maintains lightweight access readiness rather than persistent resource reservation. By coupling synchronization refresh with access signaling through a periodic heartbeat, CL enables micro-grant-based UL transmission while reducing repeated RA and idle resource waste. Numerical results show that, for bursty session traffic, CL achieves substantially lower access delay and resource overhead compared to conventional schemes, making it a promising UL-access mode for satellite-enabled 6G networks.

title: Salt Typhoon and the Limits of “Quantum Security”: A Technique-Level Audit_supp2-3688536.csv

title: Motion analysis of a multi-stiffness concentric tube magnetic continuum robot and its remote constraint application for minimally invasive surgical operations_supp1-3699226.mp4
authors: Wenguang Yang
abstract: Conventional magnetic continuum robots (MCRs) show great potential for minimally invasive surgery due to their simple structure and ease of manipulation. However, their monolithic continuous structure and single-stiffness design limit kinematic performance and manipulation accuracy. To address these limitations, we developed a concentric tube magnetic continuum robot (CT-MCR) system with replaceable concentric tubes and adjustable magnetic particle positions. Motion analysis and modeling were performed using the Cosserat rod model to optimize structural characteristics and enhance manipulation accuracy. Within a 270° driving range, the operating end-effector achieves stable trajectory following with a fit rate exceeding 90%. The distal constraint effect minimizes end-effector hopping, improving stability by over 50% compared to conventional MCRs. Navigation and foreign body retrieval experiments in a complex branching vascular model within a 3D Helmholtz coil workspace demonstrate the CT-MCR’s potential for targeted drug delivery and minimally invasive cardiovascular surgery.

title: Toward a Soft Robotic Gripper Integrating High Force Grasping and Dexterous Manipulation: The SOFRo Gripper_supp2-3693142.mp4
authors: Dongjun Shin
abstract: Developing a robotic hand that integrates high fingertip force, rapid response, and multi-degree-of-freedom (DoF) motion to achieve human hand-like capabilities remains a challenge in the field of robotic hands. This study presents the SOFRo Gripper, a soft, high-force robotic gripper designed to combine these capabilities within a compact pneumatic architecture. The finger module of the SOFRo gripper incorporates synergistically arranged chambers together with a multinode tendon routing strategy that distributes actuation forces, enabling both flexion and abduction/adduction motions while enhancing fingertip force. Furthermore, a Force-Enhanced Pleated (FEP) mechanism was applied to the chambers to increase fingertip force, resulting in a 32.41% improvement over conventional chamber designs. The proposed SOFRo gripper achieves a high fingertip force of 68.76 N and dexterous motion capabilities, enabling a maximum lifting force of 400.0 N and in-hand manipulation. To validate its versatility, extensive experiments were conducted across a wide range of tasks, including object grasping, high-force manipulation (e.g., lifting heavy objects, closing a valve), delicate handling (e.g., grasping tofu, inserting a light bulb), and high-speed manipulation (e.g., spinning a top, catching a ball). These results demonstrate the SOFRo Gripper’s ability to combine high force with versatile soft robotic manipulation.

title: SFMamba: Social-Intention-Aware Human Trajectory Prediction with Social Force State Space Model
authors: Ziyang Ren, Ping Wei, Jin Yang, Haowen Tang, Huan Li
abstract: Human trajectory prediction is crucial for understanding human social behavior and motion intentions. Existing studies have often integrated social interaction and intent-driving features to predict changes in motion states, but overlook their varying impacts on specific motion states at different time nodes. In this paper, we propose a novel framework called Social Force Mamba (SFMamba) to tackle these issues for trajectory prediction. This framework constructs a social force state space model by integrating the motion differential equations and the social force model. It represents motion trajectories as transitions from hidden motion states to surface motion states and introduces the Social Mamaba Encoder to extract hidden motion states driven by social interaction and motion intentions. It projects surface motion states into coarse-grained future motion states and combines the Goal Mamba Decoder to refine motion transitions guided by goal intentions. By integrating the selection mechanism to adaptively regulate the injection of physical attributes at different time nodes, our approach models the social-intentionaware state transition process for motion trajectory prediction. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) results on ETHUCY and SportVU NBA datasets while effectively simulating real human movement with physical interpretability.

title: Different-frequency Differential Protection Method of Adjustable Speed Hydro-Generating Unit Based on Fixed Characteristic Frequency Band
authors: Jian Qiao, Haoyuan Yu, Yikai Wang, Yifan Zhao, Xin Yin, Zhihui Dai, Xianggen Yin
abstract: During the operation of Adjustable speed hydro-generating unit (ASHU), due to high speed and large centrifugal force, the rotor winding is easily squeezed and causes short-circuit fault. The development of a reliable protection scheme is imperative. As a new and potential method, the universality of different-frequency differential protection is still limited under wide speed operation conditions. Therefore, a rotor winding short-circuit protection method based on fixed characteristic frequency band is proposed in this paper. Firstly, the internal relationship between the harmonic characteristics of rotor current and slip rate in the case of winding short-circuit fault of ASHU is analyzed. On this basis, the construction method of different-frequency differential current protection criterion based on fixed characteristic frequency band is proposed. Furthermore, combined with the waveform of the dynamic simulation experiment, the Generalized S Transform (GST) is used to carry out time-frequency analysis. The feasibility of the construction approach of the protection criterion is verified. Finally, the experimental results show that the proposed protection method is suitable for the wide speed operation condition of ASHU. It can achieve fast action under rotor winding short-circuit fault (RWSF), and has high selectivity under stator winding short-circuit fault (SWSF), which can effectively avoid protection misoperation.

title: Image Fusion with Quantum Processing for Scene Classification using the BigEarthNet Dataset
authors: Leslie Miller, Glen S Uehara, Niraj Anil Babar, Andreas Spanias
abstract: This paper presents algorithms, simulations, and results on the use of optical and Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR) quantum image fusion and machine learning algorithms for radar and remote sensing applications. We propose novel quantum image fusion techniques that extract meaningful features for identifying and classifying scenes obtained from the BigEarthNet dataset. Specifically, this work focuses on multi-label scene classification using quantum image fusion of C-band SAR and optical imagery from the BigEarthNet dataset. The proposed fusion techniques enhance and sharpen structural and textural details that are not readily observable in raw SAR imagery, thereby improving downstream classification performance. To enable quantum image fusion, we design new quantum circuits that employ unitary and Pauli gates for phase encoding of pixel intensity values. These circuits allow optical and SAR image information to be encoded and fused within a quantum processing framework. Three fusion strategies are investigated, resulting in three distinct quantum image fusion models. Comparative evaluation of these models demonstrates that pixel-averaging–based quantum fusion yields improved classification accuracy relative to alternative fusion approaches. The fused images are subsequently processed using deep classical neural networks to perform scene classification. Our neural network study begins by customizing the Visual Geometry Group 16 (VGG16) architecture for multi-label remote sensing classification tasks. In addition, we examine the use of a Residual Network 50 (ResNet-50) architecture and provide comparative performance analysis between the two models. The effects of quantum noise on the fusion and classification pipeline are also studied to assess robustness under realistic quantum processing conditions. To evaluate classification performance, we employ standard metrics such as F1 and F2 scores, and additionally introduce false discovery rate and false negative rate metrics to provide a more comprehensive assessment of classification accuracy, particularly in multi-label settings. Overall, the results demonstrate the effectiveness of optical and SAR quantum image fusion in enhancing scene classification performance and highlight the potential of integrating quantum image processing with deep learning for advanced remote sensing applications.

title: Why Large Language Models Alone Fall Short for Responsible Learner Modelling in K–12 Tutoring: A Case Study
authors: Danial Hooshyar, Yeongwook Yang, Gustav Šír, Tommi Kärkkäinen, Raija Hämäläinen, Mutlu Cukurova, Roger Azevedo
abstract: The rapid rise of large language model (LLM)-based tutors in K–12 education has led to the misconception that generative models can replace traditional learner modelling and act as general-purpose engines for adaptive instruction. This is especially problematic in K–12 settings, which the EU AI Act classifies as a high-risk domain requiring responsible design. Motivated by concerns surrounding the role of learner modelling in responsible AI-powered tutoring, this study synthesises existing research evidence on key limitations of LLM-based tutoring systems and then presents an empirical case study investigating one critical aspect of these concerns: the accuracy, reliability, and temporal coherence of assessing learners’ evolving knowledge over time. To this end, we compare a deep knowledge tracing (DKT) model with a widely used LLM (with and without fine-tuning) that has demonstrated competitive performance in tutoring-related tasks, using a large-scale open-access dataset. Our findings show that DKT achieves the highest discrimination performance (AUC = 0.83) on next-step correctness prediction and consistently outperforms the LLM across evaluation settings. Although fine-tuning improves the LLM’s AUC by about 8% over the zero-shot baseline, it still remains 6% below DKT and produces higher early-sequence errors, precisely where incorrect predictions would be most harmful for adaptive learner support. Temporal-coherence analyses further reveal that while DKT maintains stable, directionally correct mastery updates, LLM variants display substantial temporal weaknesses, including smooth but wrong-direction updates, and, even after fine-tuning, remain inconsistent and unable to match DKT’s temporal stability. We also illustrate that these issues persist even though fine-tuned LLMs required nearly 198 hours of continuous high-compute training, far exceeding the computational demands of the lightweight DKT model. Our qualitative analysis of multi-skill mastery estimation further shows that, even after fine-tuning, the LLM produced unstable and inconsistent mastery trajectories, whereas DKT maintained smooth and coherent multi-skill updates. Collectively, these findings suggest that LLMs alone are unlikely to achieve the same positive effect sizes observed in decades long intelligent tutoring systems literature. Rather than replacing learner modelling, LLMs may be more appropriately deployed as pedagogical interfaces or content generators paired with dedicated learner modelling components to ensure responsible, accurate, reliable, and pedagogically sound support.

title: Effects of Magnetic Powder Filling Ratio on the Characteristics of a Magnetic Composite Core Motor
authors: Takashi Nakamura, Ren Washioka, Kenichiro Ochiai, Tsutomu Mizuno, Mitsuhide Sato
abstract: Magnetic-composite materials, comprising soft magnetic powder and a resin binder, are characterized by excellent machinability and low iron loss. Motors with stator cores made of magnetic-composite materials (magnetic composite core (MCC) motors) can reduce iron loss and permanent magnet eddy current loss, which are critical under high-speed operation. This study investigates the effects of magnetic powder filling ratio on the characteristics of MCC motors. First, the torque characteristics of prototype motors were experimentally evaluated. The analytical results indicate that high-filling MCC motors can improve efficiency in low-speed, high-torque regions, whereas low-filling MCC motors can improve efficiency in high-speed, low-torque regions due to reductions in iron loss and spatial harmonics. Furthermore, the improvement in the temperature characteristics resulting from high-filling MCCs was clarified through thermal analysis.

title: Enhancement of Tumor-Treating Field Efficacy by Time-Varying Frequency Sweeping
authors: Yushi Kawamura, Kosaku Kurata
abstract: Objective: This study aimed to enhance the therapeutic efficacy of tumor-treating fields (TTFs) by replacing a single fixed frequency with a time-varying, frequency-swept electric field. Methods: Human lung carcinoma A549 cells were exposed for 72 h to alternating electric fields (3 V<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">p–p</sub>/cm) generated between parallel electrodes. Fixed frequencies of 150 and 300 kHz were compared with continuously swept fields ranging from 100 to 400 kHz at modulation rates of 0.6–100 kHz/s. Cell proliferation was quantified using a tetrazolium-based viability assay, and intracellular electric-field nonuniformity around a dividing-cell geometry was analyzed by finite-element modeling. Results: Fixed-frequency exposure caused only minor, non-significant reductions in cell number, whereas frequency sweeping at 0.6–2.5 kHz/s reduced proliferation by more than 50%. Numerical analyses showed that the effective frequency range associated with pronounced electric-field redistribution near the cleavage furrow depends on cell diameter, ranging from 200 to 450 kHz for the observed size distribution. Frequency sweeping effectively encompassed multiple effective bands, suppressing heterogeneous cell populations without bias toward particular sizes. The most effective modulation rates coincided with the timescales of cell retraction and mitosis, suggesting that temporal synchronization between frequency variation and cellular events maximizes efficacy. Conclusions: Frequency-swept TTFs provide a robust and practical strategy to overcome tumor heterogeneity, significantly improving treatment performance compared with fixed-frequency application. Significance: Adjusting the time-dependent sweep of the applied electric-field frequency to match tumor-specific cellular characteristics may improve reproducibility and therapeutic outcomes in clinical TTF protocols.

title: Optimizing Server Placement for Vertical Federated Learning in Dynamic Edge/Fog Networks_supp1-3700898.pdf
authors: Su Wang
abstract: We investigate the control and optimization of vertical federated learning (VFL), a class of distributed machine learning (ML) methods in which edge/fog devices contain separate data features, in dynamic edge/fog networks. Owing to heterogeneous data features and hardware across edge/fog networks, devices’ contributions to VFL vary substantially, and, moreover, dynamic edge/fog networks can lead to the permanent exit or entry of select data features. In this setting, our proposed methodology, server controlled VFL in dynamic networks (SC-DN), first establishes the existence of a global first-order stationary point for every global round, and then leverages this result to jointly optimize ML model training and resource consumption based on four key control variables: (i) server placement, (ii) device-to-server transmit power, (iii) local device processor frequency, and (iv) local training iterations per global round. The resulting optimization formulation contains coupled variables as well as numerous forms of logarithmic constraints which we show is a mixed-integer signomial program, an NP-hard problem, and for which we develop a general solver. Finally, via experiments on both image and multi-modal datasets, we show that our methodology demonstrates superior classification/regression performance and resource consumption savings than even greedy methodologies.

title: Federated Clustering: An Overview of Algorithm Evolution and Research Prospects
authors: Shifei Ding, Chao Li, Lili Guo, Qiang Yang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: As a new paradigm that integrates clustering with federated learning, federated clustering (FC) has recently attracted increasing attention, as it addresses the practical issue of privacy protection in distributed data. In this paper, we provide a comprehensive survey of recent advances in FC. This survey is organized into four parts. First, since FC is often developed by extending existing clustering methods, we review several classical clustering paradigms. Meanwhile, the inherent challenges of FC are summarized, and common improvement strategies are categorized. Second, we summarize experimental setups and evaluation protocols used in FC studies. Third, from the perspectives of data partitioning schemes and whether deep representation learning is incorporated, FC methods are divided into four categories, and representative algorithms in each category are reviewed. Finally, we discuss the limitations of current FC approaches and highlight potential directions for future research.

title: Data Visualization for Teenage Teaching: A Review of Current Applications, Pedagogical Strategies, and Impact on Learning_supp1-3700620.xlsx
authors: Xingyu Lan
abstract: Data visualization has long been recognized and applied as a powerful tool for education. However, systematic reviews within the visualization community focusing on this specific area remain limited, and existing studies have focused more on higher education or early childhood education. In this work, we focus on a particular stage of education: teenage education, where students are undergoing significant changes in cognitive abilities and facing increased curriculum demands, making visualization a crucial support tool. We are particularly interested in two research questions: (i) What is the existing method space of implementing visualization in teenage teaching, and (ii) How are the effects of the implemented methods? Driven by these questions, we reviewed 112 papers that provide valuable empirical evidence on how data visualization has been applied in teenage education and analyze three main aspects: (i) visualization applications (e.g., visualization types, forms, tools, educational goals); (ii) its pedagogical integration with curricula and teaching activities; and (iii) how it affects learning performance and user experience. Based on our analysis, we discuss a set of key trends, such as a predominant focus on STEM disciplines and the increasing integration of AR/VR and AI technologies with visualization. We also highlight various implementation challenges, such as the gaps between professional visualization tools and the actual needs of educational settings, as well as the complexity of student characteristics. Through this review, we hope to provide visualization researchers, developers, and educators with in-depth insights to better align the use of visualization with the developmental needs of teenage learners.

title: Adversarial Attack Algorithm for Image Captioning Based on Semantic Alignment
authors: Dehai Zhang, Jiajun Zhu, Jiaqi Mao
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: With the deepening application of deep learning in computer vision, the robustness of deep neural networks has attracted increasing attention. However, current research mainly focuses on unimodal tasks such as classification and segmentation. Adversarial attack research on image captioning, a multimodal task, remains underdeveloped. A typical image classification model only requires a convolutional neural network, while an image captioning model needs to analyze the temporal features of output words to generate semantically accurate sentences. This makes gradient computation extremely challenging when generating adversarial examples for semantic description models. This paper aims to study adversarial attacks on image captioning by constructing and characterizing a semantic-visual cross-modal semantic alignment network to improve the transferability of adversarial examples. By providing richer semantic information for images, more efficient adversarial examples can be identified to enhance the attack success rate. Additionally, an improved loss function is used to optimize the network and increase attack efficiency.

title: A Robust LMI-SMC Controller for Quarter-Car Active Suspension Systems
authors: Ouafae Abarkan, Nabil El Akchioui, El Mehdi Mellouli
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
