title: Review of Stress Detection Methods Based on TinyML
authors: Lina Abousalama, Sanae Mazouz
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Stress has become a serious public health issue with significant effects on physical and mental health, highlighting the need for reliable methods for early and continuous detection. Wearable sensors enable non-invasive monitoring of physiological signals related to stress. However, cloud-based solutions face challenges such as latency, energy consumption and privacy restrictions, which limit their suitability for resource-constrained wearable devices. Tiny Machine Learning (TinyML) represents an alternative approach by enabling on-device stress detection in low-power embedded systems. This paper provides an overview of TinyML-based stress detection approaches published between 2020 and 2025. It describes the most common physiological signals (photoplethysmography (PPG), electrocardiography (ECG), heart rate variability (HRV), electrodermal activity (EDA), Heart Rate (HR), skin temperature, respiration (RESP) and triaxial accelerometer), as well as commonly used benchmark datasets (Wearable Stress and Affect Detection (WESAD), Nurse Stress Dataset, Montreal Stress Test (MIST), ISRUC and SWELL), and hardware platforms (ESP32, RP2040-based Raspberry Pi Pico, STM32 series microcontrollers, Arduino Nano 33 BLE Sense and heterogeneous platforms such as InfiniWolf). A comparative analysis evaluates performance, memory usage, latency and deployment feasibility of different TinyML architectures. The results show that lightweight models, particularly deep neural networks (DNNs) and one-dimensional convolutional neural networks (1D CNNs) combined with optimization techniques such as quantization, allow for an optimal trade-off between accuracy and resource usage. Future research directions and key challenges for developing scalable and reliable TinyML-based stress detection systems for wearable healthcare applications are also discussed.

title: IMU-Based Feature Engineering for Early Detection of UAV Motor Faults: A Study on RflyMAD
authors: Abdelilah Zadid, Soufiane Hamida, Amal Tmiri, Oumaima Majdoubi, Bouchaib Cherradi, Maryam Domar
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Motor-fault early warning for multirotor UAVs often relies on propulsion telemetry (RPM/current/ESC) or dedicated vibration sensors, increasing integration complexity and SWaP and limiting applicability on cost-constrained platforms. We study a stricter setting: predicting motor faults 3 s ahead using IMU-only sensing (3-axis accelerometer and gyroscope), without propulsion measurements or control signals. Experiments on the RflyMAD Software-in-the-Loop benchmark follow a strict anti-leakage protocol with flightdisjoint train/test splitting, causal past-only features, and operating thresholds learned from training out-of-fold predictions and frozen before test evaluation. We compare a RAW-IMU baseline using instantaneous samples with a causal multi-scale feature-engineering pipeline $(0.5 / 1 / 3 \mathrm{~s}$ windows with lag $g=0.5 \mathrm{~s}$) extracting temporal statistics, derivative descriptors, and spectral features. Despite ROC-AUC $\approx \mathbf{0. 9 0}$, RAW-IMU achieves only PR-AUC $\approx 0.36-0.38$ and Recall $\approx$ 0.27-0.29 at $\mathrm{FPR} \approx 2 \%$ on unseen flights. In contrast, engineered features reach PR-AUC up to 0.85 and Recall $\approx 0.77$ at comparable FPR under frozen thresholds. Feature-importance analysis shows multi-second and frequency-related descriptors dominate, suggesting precursors are encoded in evolving inertial dynamics rather than instantaneous amplitudes. These results support IMU-only early warning as a viable low-SWaP option when robust causal temporal descriptors are used.

title: Power Quality Analysis in a PV Integrated DVR System Using Optimizing Based Approach
authors: Devi Prasad Acharya, Soumya Ranjan Das, Bhagabat Panda, Ganesh Kudva, Jayalakshimi N. S
publish: 2026 1st International Conference on Artificial Intelligence &amp; Machine Learning in Communication and Power Systems (AIMLCPS)
abstract: The production of electrical power depends heavily on renewable energy sources (RES), especially in systems attached to utility. Power quality problems result from RES's communication with the grid. Power quality issues cause voltage sag, swell, and harmonics in the grid, which negatively affect consumers. A reimbursement device, like the Dynamic Voltage Restorer (DVR), is the most economical way to address the aforementioned issues. A PV-incorporated DVR is suggested in this study to address the problems with power quality. The principal goal of this research is to improve the quality of power by designing the PI controller for DVR using the special grasshopper optimization technique, which further increases the controller's robustness and efficiency. The system's dynamic functionality has been tested with the proposed method with addition of a nonlinear load and the voltage and current sag conditions are observed and analyzed. SIMULINK software is used to verify the proposed system's functionality in real time. The findings indicate that the design effectively limits harmonic, degrees of voltage sag and swell at the load side within the IEEE accepted limit.

title: The Role of Institutional Investment in Enhancing Academic Performance through Smart Education Systems
authors: Meryem Ziz, Sophia Faris, Khalifa Mansouri
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Understanding how universities allocate resources to support student success has become increasingly urgent as higher education systems undergo rapid digital transformation. This study analyzes the impact of institutional investments on student academic performance, focusing on six key areas: financial aid, academic support services, infrastructure, educational technologies, extracurricular activities, and health services. The main objective of this research is to understand the complexity of institutional investments and their contribution to the creation of smart, technologically enriched learning environments. A literature review is conducted to emphasize the strategic role of these investments in improving educational experience and student success. In addition, a quantitative study was conducted among students enrolled in Moroccan higher education institutions to empirically assess the effect of these investments on academic performance. Results show that strategic investments, particularly in educational technologies, financial aid, and infrastructure, are the main factors improving academic outcomes and promoting the development of sustainable Smart Education systems. The study contributes to the growing body of research on institutional resource allocation in developing countries with higher education contexts and offers practical guidance to pursue sustainable digital transformation.

title: A Parametric MAXQ Reinforcement Learning Accelerator on FPGA with Outlook Toward Quantum Electronic Architectures
authors: Aziz Oukaira, Ahmed Lakhssassi
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: This study focuses on the development of a parametric MAXQ Q-Learning hardware accelerator implemented on FPGA, within the broader evolution of Reinforcement Learning (RL) toward hybrid classical-quantum architectures. Recent advances in quantum electronics offer promising opportunities for accelerated value-function optimization and improved energy efficiency. In this work, we present a complete VHDL-based hardware implementation deployed on a CycloneV (5CSEMA5F31C6) FPGA platform. The proposed architecture supports Q-value precision ranging from 8 to 128 bits, state sizes $N=8,16,32$, and action sets $Z=4,8,16$. Experimental evaluation was conducted using Quartus Prime under a 2 ns timing constraint. Performance metrics include logic resource utilization (LUTs, LUTRAMs, flip-flops), maximum achievable clock frequency, and post-Place & Route power consumption.

title: Classification of Liver Disease Using Random Forest, Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors
authors: Syarifah Diana, Ghinaa Zain Nabiilah, Nixon, Fathan Aghnia Akmal
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Innovative Research in Applied Science, Engineering and Technology (IRASET)
abstract: Liver disease is a major global health problem affecting millions of people worldwide. Early detection is crucial for improving prevention, treatment options, and recovery outcomes. This study used a dataset of 1,700 records containing demographic information and laboratory test results, each labeled to indicate the presence or absence of liver disease. The goal of this study was to develop a machine learning model capable of identifying patterns in the data to aid in the early prediction of liver disease. Three models were used in this study. The Random Forest, Decision Tree, and K-Nearest Neighbors (KNN) were evaluated on both training and testing datasets. Among the three, the Random Forest model demonstrated superior performance, achieving the highest accuracy and balanced generalization, making it the most effective method for predicting liver disease in this study. This research highlights the potential of machine learning techniques to support the timely diagnosis and management of liver disease.

title: An Enhanced YOLOv11-Based Algorithm for Identifying Unhelmeted Power Plant Workers
authors: Qinbo Ren, Yapeng Su, Kuilong Zhang
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Sensors and Information Technology (ICSI)
abstract: Power plants represent high-risk industrial environments where head injuries account for $\mathbf{4 2 \%}$ of fatal accidents due to non-compliance with safety helmet regulations. This paper proposes a novel enhanced You Only Look Once version 11 (YOLOv11)-based algorithm (PowerHelmet-YOLO) tailored for real-time detection of unhelmeted workers in power plant scenarios. The algorithm integrates deformable convolutional network v2 (DCNv2) to handle geometric transformations and occlusions, a recalibrated feature pyramid (RFP) for multi-scale feature fusion, and a dual-threshold tracking mechanism to address target occlusion. Experimental validation on the PowerPlant-Safety Dataset (PPSD) demonstrates that PowerHelmet-YOLO achieves a mean average precision $\left(\mathrm{mAP}_{50}\right)$ of $94.2 \%$, precision of $93.7 \%$, and recall of $91.8 \%$, outperforming state-of-the-art models including YOLOv10n and YOLO with Deformable Convolutional Network v2 and Recalibrated Feature Pyramid (YOLO-DCNRFP). The model runs at 326 Frames Per Second (FPS) on edge devices, meeting the real-time monitoring requirements of power plant operations. This research provides a reliable technical solution for automated safety compliance monitoring in complex industrial environments.

title: Proactive Federated Anomaly Detection in Consumer Electronic Ecosystems via Adaptive Learning and Lightweight Auditability
authors: Chien-Ming Chen, Kailian Chai, Zahid Akhtar, Seema Agrawal, Rahul Kumar, Yachao Li
abstract: Consumer electronic ecosystems, such as smart vehicles, wearable devices, connected homes, and Internet of Things (IoT)-enabled transportation systems, face increasing cyber threats, privacy risks, and reliability challenges. Although federated learning (FL) enables privacy-preserving collaborative training, its deployment in such environments is still hindered by statistical heterogeneity, hyperparameter sensitivity, and limited training auditability. This paper proposes SABFL, a federated intrusion detection framework for consumer electronic ecosystems. It combines FedProx-based local training, online simulated annealing for adaptive hyperparameter tuning, and lightweight blockchain-assisted audit logging. The framework improves convergence stability under heterogeneous data and provides traceable training records with low additional overhead. Experimental results on the CICIDS2017 dataset show that SABFL achieves performance comparable to existing methods under IID conditions, while yielding significantly stronger robustness under non-IID scenarios. In highly heterogeneous settings, SABFL improves the Macro-F1 score by up to 20.58% compared with standard federated baselines, and outperforms the advanced FedNova approach by 16.43%. These results demonstrate the practicality of SABFL for proactive intrusion detection in distributed consumer electronic environments.

title: Person-in-WiFi 3D: Unified Model for 3D WiFi Perception
authors: Bo Qian, Xing Wei, Kangwei Yan, Han Ding, Jinsong Han, Fei Wang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Pattern Analysis and Machine Intelligence
abstract: WiFi signals, in contrast to cameras, offer privacy protection and occlusion resilience for some practical scenarios such as smart homes, elderly care, and virtual reality. Recent years have seen remarkable progress in the estimation of single-person 2D pose, single-person 3D pose, multi-person 2D pose, and single-person mesh. This paper takes a step forward by introducing Person-in-WiFi 3D, a pioneering WiFi system that accomplishes multi-person 3D human perception. Person-in-WiFi 3D has two main updates. Firstly, compared to previous systems that used only one WiFi transmitter and one WiFi receiver, it has a greater number of WiFi devices, which enhances its capability for capturing 3D spatial reflections from multiple individuals. This allows it to respond more flexibly to complicated 3D perception scenarios, achieving a breakthrough from 2D to 3D. Secondly, it utilizes a DETR-like architecture based on Hungarian matching to achieve the end-to-end estimation. It employs a hierarchical refinement strategy from coarse to fine, effectively utilizing features at the global level, human-instance level, and keypoint level, thereby achieving finer-grained estimation and reconstruction. On the basis of these designs, Person-in-WiFi 3D is storage-efficient, accurate, and fast compared to its predecessor. Wedeployed a proof-of-concept system in 4m × 3.5m areas and collected a multi-person human perception dataset Wiception3D of over 97K frames with seven volunteers, encompassing diverse scenes and multi-person scenarios. Wiception3D includes nearly 98k training samples and nearly 8k test samples, annotated for 3D pose estimation and 3D mesh reconstruction tasks. Person-in-WiFi 3D achieves a keypoint localization error of 93mm in 3D pose estimation and 41mm in mesh reconstruction, comparable to cameras and millimeter-wave radars. The project page is at https://aiotgroup.github.io/Person-in-WiFi-3D

title: Bounding Box Discrepancy-Driven Building Height Reconstruction from Optical and SAR Imagery
authors: Fanxun Luo, Pu Gu, Sihao Dong, Tao Lai, Qingsong Wang, Haifeng Huang
publish: 2026 7th International Conference on Geology, Mapping and Remote Sensing (ICGMRS)
abstract: Automatic building height extraction is crucial in remote sensing. The fusion of optical and synthetic aperture radar (SAR) imagery has been shown to improve the accuracy of height reconstruction. However, most existing cross-modal approaches rely on deep neural networks for implicit multimodal feature fusion, without explicitly modeling the geometric discrepancies of buildings between optical and SAR imagery, thereby limiting physical interpretability. This letter proposes a Bounding box Discrepancy Height Reconstruction Network (BDHR-Net), which reconstructs building heights by mapping bounding box offsets between optical and SAR imagery to physical heights. A Radar Geometry-guided Logarithmic Regression (RGLR) mechanism and a Height Consistency Constraint (HCC) are designed to enforce consistency with SAR imaging geometry and the physical constraints of building height. Experiments on the MSAW dataset show that BDHR-Net reduces root mean square error (RMSE) by 4.92 % and improves <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\delta_{1}$</tex> by 3.29 % over existing state-of-the-art methods. These results demonstrate that explicit geometric modeling offers clear advantages in both reconstruction accuracy and physical interpretability.

title: Research on the Design of Children's Color Cognitive Intelligent Toys Based on Embodied Cognition Theory
authors: Jiwei Liu, Yuhao Jiao, Jiangsheng Wang, Ensheng Zhao, Yunpeng Jiang, Jie Gao
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: Based on the embodied cognition theory, explore and construct a set of interactive color cognition intelligent toy methods and designs suitable for preschool children. Combining embodied cognition theory, multi-sensory interaction and color psychology, this paper explores the interactive relationship among body movements, sensory experiences and color cognition, and establishes a color cognition toy design hierarchy system constructed from the transformation layer, perception layer, interaction layer, emotion layer and feedback layer. Through market research, analysis of children's characteristics, questionnaires and KJ analysis method, the design requirements of color cognition toys were obtained. The Kano model and FBS model were introduced to categorize, refine and transform the design requirements, and clarify the logical relationship between the functions, behaviors and structures of the toys. The multisensory interaction cognitive model based on embodied cognition theory is more in line with children's learning methods. It can not only effectively improve children's efficiency in color cognition but also contribute to their intellectual growth.

title: Modified Lin-Kernighan-Helsgaun Based on Binary α
authors: Xianzhi Huang, Jiarui Zhang, Qunfeng Liu
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: The Lin-Kernighan-Helsgaun algorithm is one of the most effective heuristics for the traveling salesman problem, and its performance largely depends on the candidate edge sets built from 1-tree based <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\bold symbol{\alpha}$</tex> values. However, computing these <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> values requires repeated 1 -tree construction and updates, which becomes a major preprocessing bottleneck on medium- and large-scale instances and complicates integration with other metaheuristics. This paper proposes a lightweight candidate-set construction method that replaces the classical 1tree <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> computation with Minimum Spanning Tree (MST)guided binary <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\alpha$</tex> strategy. A minimum spanning tree is first built as a global structural backbone, and multiple MST-guided initial tours are generated to identify frequently used edges. Each edge is then assigned a binary label indicating whether it appears in at least one initial tour, and candidate edges are selected by sorting incident edges using the binary label and edge cost. Experiments on TSPLIB instances show that the proposed method achieves solution quality comparable to classical The Lin-Kernighan-Helsgaun while reducing preprocessing time on many medium and large-scale instances, although the total runtime can occasionally be dominated by the downstream local search, demonstrating an effective trade-off between efficiency and performance for large-scale settings and hybrid frameworks.

title: Geometric Phases in NV Centers: Abelian and Non-Abelian Protocols Using Hyperfine-Engineered Dark Subspaces
authors: S. Biswas, V. A. Chirayath, J. Asaadi
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: Geometric phases encode the holonomy acquired under cyclic control of a quantum Hamiltonian: for nondegenerate eigenstates the effect is Abelian (a scalar phase), while in degenerate manifolds the Wilczek-Zee generalization yields a matrix-valued holonomy that can mix states and depends on path ordering. We present an overview of measuring Abelian and non-Abelian geometric phases in nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centers in diamond and propose a non-rotating measurement protocol enabled by the intrinsic <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{14} ~\mathrm{N}$</tex> hyperfine manifold. As a first step, we propose to measure the Abelian Berry phase on a single hyperfine-resolved two-level transition using microwaveengineered closed loops with dynamical-phase cancellation by loop reversal. We then propose a non-Abelian protocol that engineers a two-dimensional degenerate dark subspace in an NV- <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${ }^{14} ~\mathrm{N}$</tex> manifold via RF dressing and three phase-coherent microwave couplings (a tripod Hamiltonian). Two closed control loops implemented by winding independent microwave phases generate non-commuting SU(2) holonomies, which can be verified using final-only population readout and minimal tomography. We also present timing trade-off curves, full unitary Schrödinger evolution of the four-level tripod, and projected dark-subspace diagnostics that quantify both leakage and non-commuting holonomy.

title: Autoencoder Semi-Supervised Feature Selection Algorithm Based on Double Graph Discriminant Information
authors: Miao Luo, Ping Gong
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: High dimensional data are mostly characterized by high dimensions and few samples. There are a lot of irrelevant features and redundant features, which seriously affect the efficiency and generalization ability of the model. In this paper, an autoencoder semi-supervised feature selection algorithm based on double graph discriminant information is proposed. The algorithm first uses K-NS strategy to generate pseudo-labels of unlabeled data. Then the labeled data and unlabeled data are combined into a new data set to calculate the positive and negative structure information of the dataset, and the double graph discriminant information of the dataset is obtained. Finally, using the weighted Euclidean distance, the double graph discriminant information is constructed as a regularization term and added to the autoencoder feature selection process. The algorithm is tested on multiple datasets, and it is verified that the proposed algorithm can select the most representative feature subset, effectively remove irrelevant and redundant features, and improve the performance of subsequent learning tasks.

title: MSA-CongNet: Multi-Scale Attention with Noise-Aware Regularization for VLSI Congestion Prediction
authors: Ziyan Qu, Yuxiang Liu, Wenjun Zhu, Zhengkang Zhu
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: Accurate congestion prediction at the placement stage is critical for reducing design iterations and accelerating timing closure in modern electronic design automation (EDA) flows. Although learning-based methods have shown promise on EDA benchmarks such as CircuitNet, existing approaches suffer from three persistent limitations: (1) single-scale feature extraction fails to capture the spatial heterogeneity of congestion patterns ranging from local pin clusters to global routing demand; (2) convolutional architectures lack explicit modeling of long-range dependencies between distant hotspots sharing routing resources; and (3) models trained on early-stage placement features are brittle under the inherent noise of pre-routing estimations. To address these challenges, we propose MSA-CongNet, a unified framework comprising three synergistic modules: (1) a Multi-Scale Feature Pyramid (MSFP) that encodes hierarchical spatial context through top-down fusion across four resolution levels; (2) a Spatial Cross-Attention (SCA) mechanism with learnable geometric bias that captures non-local hotspot correlations; and (3) a Noise-Aware Regularization (NAR) strategy that enforces prediction consistency under controlled input perturbation. Experiments on CircuitNet demonstrate that MSA-CongNet reduces MAE by 36.8% over the strongest baseline while achieving 24% faster inference.

title: Cross-Domain Multi-Agent Digital Twin for Adaptive Traffic Signal Control and Intelligent Campus Mobility
authors: Alfredo Alan Flores-Saldivar, Jorge de Jesus Lozoya Santos, Juan Carlos Tudon Martinez, Edson Fernando Acosta Argueta, Yuxian Li
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: Urban traffic congestion poses increasing challenges for intelligent mobility systems, particularly as robotic platforms require reliable infrastructure-aware information for decision-making. This paper presents a crossdomain digital twin framework that integrates empirically grounded traffic demand modeling, multi-agent simulation, adaptive signal control, and synchronized 3D visualization within a unified architecture. Signalized intersections are modeled as interacting vehicle and traffic light agents operating over a graph-based network. Infrastructure-level control is formulated as a Markov Decision Process (MDP), enabling adaptive and interpretable signal phase allocation based on observed queue states. Simulation outputs-including congestion dynamics, delay metrics, and signal evolution-are exported and synchronized with a Unity-based environment, forming a spatially and temporally consistent digital twin. Comparative evaluation against a fixed-time baseline demonstrates reductions in waiting time and queue length, together with increased throughput under realistic demand conditions. The framework further establishes a structured interface through which robotic agents, such as neighborhood electric vehicles (NEVs), can consume predictive infrastructure information to support navigation decisions. The results demonstrate that integrating adaptive multi-agent control with digital twin synchronization provides measurable traffic-level improvements while enabling scalable cross-domain coordination between infrastructure intelligence and robotic autonomy.

title: Exploring the Effectiveness of Artificial Intelligence in Transforming Future of Digital Marketing
authors: Shital Naikwade, Namita Chawla, Sunil Kumar Suvvari, Nilesh Anute, Rajendra Subhash Jarad, Mangesh Purushottam Dande
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: Artificial intelligence (AI) has an effect on digital marketing (DM) in today's globalized environment. If managed carefully, AI will change the DM landscape and businesses are starting to see the potential of AI associated with DM success. DM is one of many businesses that are radically changing from the rapid growth of AI and machine learning (ML) technology. This paper looks to explore how AI and ML is affecting digital marketing processes and what that means to businesses. Marketers use ML to find patterns of users on a mobile app or webpage. By allowing AI to learn these patterns undoubtedly evolves advertising offers by optimizing their campaign and predicting future behaviours of their users. This paper proposes a new method of employing ML for information systems, optimizing advertising services, engaging customers, generating sales, personalizing promotional offers based on user interests, and for just simply setting up a spam filter for email advertisements or Facebook services. This papers theory demonstrates that the methodology for enhancing advertising campaigns is in fact reasonable.
