title: Deep Learning Empowers Internet of Things Device Energy Consumption Optimization: Design of Intelligent Gateway Resource Scheduling Based on CNN-LSTM Model
authors: Junxu Wei, Huqiang Liu, Xiaoyun Huang, Rui Zheng, Yongjun Qi
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: With the surge in IoT (Internet of Things) devices, energy efficiency management challenges for smart gateways are becoming increasingly prominent. Traditional static resource scheduling strategies struggle to adapt to dynamic loads. This paper proposes a hybrid intelligent scheduling model that integrates a convolutional neural network (CNN) with a long short-term memory (LSTM). First, the CNN extracts spatial correlation features from multi-dimensional monitoring data. Then, the LSTM learns the temporal evolution of the load. Finally, based on the predicted results, CPU (Central Processing Unit) frequency scheduling rules are dynamically generated to achieve on-demand resource allocation. Experimental results show that this strategy reduces energy consumption by approximately 30% under typical loads, significantly outperforms the delay violation rate of comparable methods, and improves resource utilization stability. This study validates the effectiveness and advantages of deep learning-driven proactive scheduling in energy optimization and service assurance for IoT gateways.

title: Ionizing Radiation Effects on Lithium-Ion Battery Electrolyte Diffusion Properties
authors: Jeff Stubblefield, Mohammad Qasem, Yazan Yassin, Bruce Gnade, Mahesh Krishnamurthy
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: Lithium-ion batteries (LIBs) are increasingly used in high-radiation environments such as space, nuclear energy, medical, and waste management applications. Understanding the effects of total ionizing dose on LIB electrochemical performance is critical, as radiation-induced electrolyte decomposition is a primary failure mechanism. This study investigates the impact of X-ray irradiation (up to 6 Mrad, 320 keV) on 21700 NMC811 cells and isolated LiPF<inf xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">6</inf> based electrolyte (EC/DMC/DEC, 1:1:1). Impedance measurements show a 36 percent increase in SEI resistance, a 13 percent increase in subdiffusion time constant, significant HF formation, and near depletion of <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">${\text{PF}_{6}}^{-}$</tex> at 1 Mrad. A modified equivalent circuit model (ECM) based on broadband EIS and HPPC parameter extraction is proposed to support radiation-aware battery management system design.

title: Simulated DPN-Like Gait Abnormality Recognition Using Wearable IMU and Plantar Pressure Sensing Framework: A Pilot Study
authors: Jiayuan Zhao, Yan Wang, Ruixiang Hu, Hui Wang, Xiangyu Zhan, Aihui Wang
publish: 2026 6th International Symposium on Intelligent Robotics and Systems (ISoIRS)
abstract: Diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN) is associated with sensory-motor decline and altered gait, motivating objective, low-cost gait assessment tools. This paper presents a wearable multimodal sensing framework that combines an outsole-mounted plantar-pressure sensor array and an acquisition unit with on-board IMU to recognize DPNlike gait abnormalities in a pilot study. A dual-branch CNNBiLSTM network is developed to extract complementary spatial-temporal features from synchronized 14-channel sequences (8 pressure +6 -axis IMU) sampled at <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{5 0 ~ H z}$</tex>. On a subject-wise leave-one-subject-out (LOSO) evaluation, the proposed approach achieves 75.13 % accuracy with AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=$</tex> 0.8403 in distinguishing normal from DPN-like gait. Under a window-level random split, it reaches <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$\mathbf{9 5. 0 0 \%}$</tex> accuracy (AUC <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$=$</tex> 0.90) and outperforms single-modality baselines. These results suggest the potential of multimodal wearables for objective gaitabnormality recognition, while clinical validation on real DPN cohorts remains future work.

title: Secure Cloud Data Mining with Blockchain-Driven Privacy Protection and Adversarial Attack Mitigation
authors: Thanu Priya N, V. Sheeja Kumari
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: This paper presents a secure cloud data mining framework that integrates blockchain-based privacy protection with adversarial attack mitigation to ensure reliable and trustworthy data analytics in distributed environments. The proposed system leverages blockchain technology for decentralized data verification, secure access control, and tamper-resistant transaction logging, while incorporating deep learning-based adversarial detection to enhance model robustness against malicious inputs. Privacy-preserving preprocessing techniques, including encryption and normalization, are applied to safeguard sensitive data before mining. The framework combines secure data sharing, intelligent mining, and attack resilience into a unified architecture. Experimental evaluation demonstrates that the proposed approach achieves higher mining accuracy, improved attack detection rates, and enhanced privacy protection compared to conventional cloud mining and standalone security models. The results highlight the effectiveness of integrating blockchain and adversarial learning for secure, scalable, and efficient cloud data mining.

title: Decentralized Task-Allocation in Bayesian Search: An Empirical Study Under Degraded Communications
authors: James Lott, Vahraz Honary
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: Reliable multi-robot search is constrained by imperfect communications when deployed in field environments. This motivates empirical evaluation of lightweight decentralized alternatives that may degrade more gracefully as message reliability worsens. This paper presents a hardware-based empirical comparison of three decentralized task-allocation strategies for Bayesian search on a four-UGV testbed under progressive receiver-side Bernoulli message loss <tex xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">$(0 \%, 10 \%, 25 \%)$</tex>. The evaluated methods represent three coordination paradigms: belief-weighted Voronoi allocation, market-based assignment, and adaptive rectangular partitioning. Each was implemented in decentralized form and optimized for lightweight onboard computation and messaging. Performance is evaluated using post-clue efficiency (steps-to-find), workload balance, onboard computation fraction, and communication overhead density. Across all communication conditions, the market-based approach achieved the lowest post-clue steps-to-find and maintained nearly constant computation and messaging overhead as loss increased, while the Voronoi allocation and rectangular partitioning degraded more substantially. We also validate a hub-centric metric logging pipeline that enables accurate system-level evaluation even when message loss induces inconsistent local robot state.

title: Small-Signal Stability Analysis of Hybrid Grid-Forming and Grid-Following Converter Systems
authors: Ge Tang, Yanming Chen
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: With the rapid development of novel power systems—characterized notably by high penetration of power electronics and renewable energy sources—grid-following (GFL) converters demonstrate robust stability under strong grid conditions but are prone to instability in weak grid scenarios. In contrast, grid-forming (GFM) converters boast excellent adaptability to weak grids, while their operating performance under strong grid conditions is unsatisfactory. Accordingly, the hybrid grid-connected operation of GFM and GFL converters has become a mainstream configuration in the new-type power system, making research on such hybrid systems a hot topic in the field. Concurrently, an in-depth investigation into the synchronization stability of these hybrid systems is of paramount importance. This paper first establishes a small-signal model for a grid-connected hybrid system comprising both GFL and GFM converters. Subsequently, utilizing eigenvalue analysis, the impact of variations in the grid short-circuit ratio (SCR) on the system's small-signal stability is analyzed. Finally, the validity of the theoretical analysis is verified through time-domain simulation results.

title: Research on Capacity Optimization of Distribution Transformers in Metro Stations
authors: Ji Luo, Yin Li
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: The rational selection of power distribution transformer capacity is critical to the safe, reliable, and economical operation of subway station power distribution systems. Based on actual operational data from Xiamen Metro Line 3, this paper conducts a specialized study to address common issues in domestic subway stations, such as low load factors of distribution transformers and significant discrepancies between design parameters and actual operation. Through an in-depth analysis of the energy consumption characteristics of various loads, including ventilation and air conditioning, escalators and low-voltage systems, the study refines key parameters in the traditional demand factor method and verifies the power supply capacity under multiple operating conditions, including normal, fault, and fire scenarios. The results demonstrate that optimizing the capacity of station distribution transformers can significantly reduce equipment procurement costs and long-term operational energy consumption while ensuring power supply reliability. This provides a crucial basis for green and energy-efficient design in subsequent metro lines and stations across other cities in China.

title: Generative AI-Driven Analytics Model for Predictive Institutional Performance Mapping
authors: Swastisudha Punyatoya, Brijendra Singh Yadav, Naveen Nandal, Prachi Bismaya, Sandeep Sampat Tadakhe, John T Abraham
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: The suggestion is a collaborative contribution of the transformer-based neural networks such as GPT-4 or the variants of BERT algorithms with the conventional prediction algorithms (predictive analytics) to process the multidimensional data streams in the case of institutional organizations. The system can generate complete trajectories in performance by applying deep learning techniques like variational autoencoders, or VAEs, and generative adversarial networks or GANs through the application of historical performance metrics and stakeholder patterns of engagement and environmental variables. It relied on the python-based frameworks such as TensorFlow, PyTorch, Skitlearn, and the cloud-based environment to scale the implementation such as Azure ML and AWS SDK as SageMaker. The combination of Apache Kafka to process the data in real-time and Power BI to visualize the data in real-time makes it possible to have dynamic and real-time performance monitoring. It can be experimentally validated to yield much higher levels of accuracy in predicting 89% of various institutional situations compared to various traditional regression models. This strategy is transforming the manner of execution of strategic plans by offering practical knowledge concerning how to maximize on the resources and evade the hazards.

title: A Collision-Aware Mobile Robot Navigation in Grid Environment using Improved A*
authors: Zihao Chu, Jianheng Qiu, Jianing Xu, Zheyu Zhang, Haopeng Zhao, Hao Liu
publish: 2026 12th International Conference on Control, Automation and Robotics (ICCAR)
abstract: Effective path planning in cluttered environments remains a critical challenge for mobile robots. While traditional grid-based planners like A* excel at finding shortest paths, they often neglect overall path safety, leading to high-risk navigation. This paper proposes a Collision-Aware Improved A* (CA-A*) algorithm to address this limitation. Our approach enhances the classical A* framework by integrating a multi-layer environmental model that accounts for static obstacles and positional uncertainty. A novel cost function incorporates a collision risk assessment, penalizing paths that venture too close to hazards. Furthermore, an adaptive heuristic function guides the search towards safer regions. Comprehensive simulations in static benchmarks demonstrate that CA-A* generates significantly safer paths while maintaining competitive path lengths—substantially outperforming classical A*, Weighted A*, and Field D* in terms of safety metrics. Real-world experiments validate the algorithm’s practicality and robustness. The results confirm that the proposed method achieves a superior balance between path length and safety for mobile robot navigation, trading a minimal increase in distance for a substantial improvement in navigation safety.

title: Quantitative Evaluation of Amplitude and Phase Measurement Deviations Across Three Commercial VNAs for Common RF Components
authors: Liwen Liang, Kwok L. Chung, Jialiang Ni, Zhilie Tang
publish: 2026 IEEE 9th World Conference on Computing and Communication Technologies (WCCCT)
abstract: As the core high-precision test instrument in RF and microwave engineering, the Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) provides full-vector S-parameter amplitude and phase measurements that directly determine the reliability of electromagnetic performance evaluation for RF devices such as antennas and microstrip lines. Most existing studies focus on measurement comparison between two VNAs using standard metrology devices, with insufficient research on multi-brand VNA measurement consistency for commonly used engineering RF devices. This paper selects three commercial VNAs (CEYEAR 3671D, KEYSIGHT E5063A, ROHDE & SCHWARZ ZNL20) to test the S-parameters of two patch antennas and one microstrip line under uniform SOLT calibration, and quantitatively evaluates measurement deviation of each instrument via the RMSE method. Results show that quantifiable amplitude and phase deviations exist across different VNAs, with phase deviation significantly larger than amplitude deviation; each VNA has unique performance strengths in different frequency bands. This study provides direct reference for instrument selection and measurement uncertainty analysis in RF testing.

title: Solar Energy Prediction Using Voting Ensemble Learning Regressor
authors: Mukund Kuthe, Parambrata Sanyal, Gopal Kumar Gupta, Ajit Kumar Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Accurate solar energy forecasting is crucial for efficient management and resource allocation in future sustainable cities because of the probable increase in renewable energy sources. Machine Learning (ML) is one of the most reliable options in solar energy prediction, capturing complex pattern in historical datasets. The primary objective of this research is to accurately predict solar energy using ML. Therefore, this article proposes a voting ensemble regressor that outperforms the traditional ML models in this predictive methodology, upon evaluation metrics chosen. The complete ML pipelining in this study involves acquisition of dataset from Kaggle repository, performing data preprocessing and feature engineering. The methodology employs principal component analysis (PCA) to reduce the dimension and retain originality in the dataset. The proposed voting ensemble regressor model combines three ML models, namely, Optimized XG Boost, Support Vector Regressor, and Random Forest, to enhance the performance in this predictive methodology. Furthermore, the authors explores all traditional ML models and compares the result in terms of MAE, RMSE, R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> value. The outcomes demonstrate how much better the suggested model is than the conventional ones. Therefore, this study provides a path for future research in projecting renewable energy.

title: Smart InfraOps: Securing, Scaling and Monitoring GPU-Driven HPC in the Cloud-Native Era
authors: Ratan Raj Anandeshi, Devarsh Hemantbhai Patel
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: High-Performance Computing (HPC) workloads in the cloud-native environment based on GPUs have advanced exponentially, which increased the need to deploy smart, secure, and scalable operational frameworks. The reference HPC infrastructures are not able to satisfy the new demands of scalability and dynamism, real-time observability, and automated compliance because of their hard, manual-based structures. This research proposes the Smart InfraOps, which combines the adaptive orchestration, AI-based observability, and zero-trust security into HPC systems based on GPUs. The system is a combination of Kubernetes native automation, GPU-aware scheduling, and intelligent use is achieved via telemetry to make the system maximally utilized, reduce costs and enhance resiliency. Experimental simulations were run with empirical results that proved Smart InfraOps to be better at utilizing GPS in 85 percent, lowering the scaling latency in 57 percent, decreasing the operations costs in 40 percent, and increasing the security compliance by 24 percent relative to the traditional GPU clusters. These findings validate Smart InfraOps as an efficient, cloud-native operational model that can effectively and safely manage, optimize and monitor the existent HPC workloads that are high in GPUs in the cloud-native era.

title: Role of AI in Fostering Professors’ Well-Being and Job Satisfaction
authors: N Janani, E Nafeza, Mithilesh, Venus Claver Dennen
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: The academic industry is changing as AI solves academics' biggest working issues. This research investigates how artificial intelligence improves academics' well-being and work satisfaction via technological interventions and supportive mechanisms. The study examines how AI can speed up administrative processes, decrease workplace stress, and improve learning environments. Artificial intelligence automates scheduling, grading, and student administration, freeing professors to focus on academics and research. The most noteworthy results show how artificial intelligence can adapt professional development, offer data-driven teaching tactics, and provide flexible learning environments. The technology's complete support networks reduce burnout, improve resource management, and diversify the workplace. Artificial intelligence's predictive analytics may potentially uncover issues and provide proactive assistance. The research found that strategic artificial intelligence may improve academic work cultures, improving educator job satisfaction, career development, and well-being.

title: Optimization of Process Parameters in Milling Operation using VMC
authors: Ishan Jain, Aditiya Choudhary, Piyush Pant, Chitresh Nayak, Ajay Sharma, Narender Singh
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)
abstract: Nowadays, cutting-edge modernization have led to the adoption of Computer controlled machines in several industries to boost output and quality. The computer controlled machines outrival conventional equipment in terms of accuracy and finishing. By adjusting the framework, CNC milling machining can be used to its greatest potential. Thus, this work reviews the process parameter optimization for CNC milling with carbide tools. The review shows how feed, speed, and DOC affect MRR, SR, and tool wear. Experimenters have been using Taguchi for upgradation. Surface and profile graphs are used to investigate the effects of milling on tool wear, MRR, and SR. It was determined that the 316 L stainless steel process parameters may be improved.

title: IEEE Copyright and Consent Form
authors: N/A
publish: 2025 International Conference on Emerging Engineering Technologies and Applications (IC-EETA)

title: Website Heuristics and Their influence on Generation Z’s Purchasing Behaviour
authors: Mythili. R, Kiruthiga V
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: In the digital era, website design and usability significantly influence customer behavior, especially among the technologically adept Generation Z. This research examines the influence of website heuristics—namely navigation, aesthetics, interaction, trustworthiness, and personalization—on the purchase behavior of Generation Z in Chennai City. The study uses a mixed-method approach, integrating quantitative surveys and qualitative interviews to examine the preferences, expectations, and behaviors of this group. Research reveals that Generation Z prioritizes websites with intuitive navigation, aesthetically pleasing designs, engaging functionality, and extensive personalization. Trustworthiness, including safe payment methods and clear rules, is a pivotal component affecting purchasing choices. The research emphasizes the alignment of these heuristics with the psychological and cultural characteristics of Generation Z, including their inclination towards immediate satisfaction, social validation, and digital proficiency. Enterprises focusing on this demographic may use this information to optimize website design, elevate user experience, and increase online sales. This study offers practical advice for e-commerce platforms and digital marketers to enhance their strategies for Generation Z, hence fostering sustainable company success in Chennai's aggressive digital economy.

title: A lightweight deep learning and gradient boosting approach for Oral cancer classification
authors: S. Annie Kesviya, Manikandan J, R. Brindha
publish: 2025 International Conference on Intelligent &amp; Innovative Practices in Engineering &amp; Management (IIPEM)
abstract: Oral cancer is a major health problem in the world and more so in less developed nations where access to early diagnostic facility is small or absent. Oral cancer presents itself in its early life stages with no symptoms and thus it is not diagnosed and survival is largely compromised. Conventional methods of diagnosis are based on manual histopathological examination that besides being time-consuming, is prone to errors and prone to biases on personal grounds. Recent growth in artificial intelligence, especially in deep learning and machine learning, are the opportunities that can enhance the accuracy and efficiency of medical image analysis. Most of the solutions in the literature are based on single-model architectures, and they can often not simultaneously extract features well and classify well. To overcome these constraints, we will present a new hybrid neural network structure called Oral Net-CB and this network is specially created to detect oral cancer at an earlier stage and precisely. The last processing pipeline is a complete preprocessing system based on methods like histogram equalization, contrast-limited adaptive histogram equalization (CLAHE), and Gaussian filtering to boost the quality of the image and decrease noise. DenseNet121 is utilized to extract deep features because it is densely connected and it can extract dense spatial features efficiently. To classify, we apply Cat Boost, which is a high-performance gradient boosting algorithm that is most appropriate in case of class imbalance and in working with categorical data. Oral Net-CB with a publicly available data of oral cancer images is applied. The model had a classification accuracy of 94.3% that was relatively better and accurate than the current model.

title: Visual Detection of Corrosion Defects in In-service Oil Pipes by Pulsed Eddy Current
authors: Pan-Long Jia, Wen-Hong Liu, Rui Wang, Dong-Feng Li, Yong Li, Jun Han
publish: 2025 IEEE Far East NDT New Technology &amp; Application Forum (FENDT)
abstract: Under the circumstance that global oil and gas exploration and transportation have attracted much attention, the integrity of in-service oil pipes in the oil and gas well string is of vital importance to the safe and stable operation of the entire oil and gas production system. Due to being in a sulfur-containing corrosive environment for a long time, the in-service oil pipes are facing a severe problem of corrosions on their inner walls, which may lead to serious consequences such as the failure of the pipe string and the leakage of toxic gases. This research focuses on the pulsed eddy current detection of corrosions on the inner walls of in-service oil pipes. A pulsed eddy current dual-sensor differential probe is adopted to pick up the detection signals. The negative peak value of the normalized differential signal is utilized as the signal feature to establish the correlation law between the signal feature and the defect size and to achieve defect imaging. Finally, the Active Contour Models algorithm is used to effectively identify the edges of the defects.

title: Data Science for Climate Resilience and Smart Agriculture
authors: Sreekanth Yalavarthi, Monica Rohan Mali, Naim Shaikh, Suriya M, Kiran Kumar Reddy Penubaka, Karthikayen A
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: Climate change is one of the biggest challenges facing world agriculture, impacting crop yields, accessible resources, and food security. Traditional methods of agriculture are, however, less receptive to the adaptive demands required to deal with the changed environmental setting. The present research has, thus, attempted to address this issue by maximizing data science in support of climate resilience and intelligent agriculture through a multi-tiered approach for methodology. These range from the acquisition of satellite images, IoT-enabled sensor data in the field, and historical climate information, pre-processing, analytical modeling, system integration, and output validation. Machine learning techniques deployed include Random Forest and time-series forecasting to improve yield forecasting and estimation of climate risk. Results indicated that the enhancement in the accuracy of yield prediction attained 25 %, water consumption was reduced by 30 %, fertilizer usage dropped by 20 %, and the correct forecast for climate risk reached 85 %. A cloudintegrated platform featuring real-time analytics integrated with the decision support proved to be very flexible and easy to use in multiple agro-climatic zones. This research has shown the way data-driven models can influence the agricultural sector to an adaptive system, assuring a more efficient and sustainable system contributing good practices for climate-resilient farming and resource-informed management in the uncertain environmental scenario.

title: A Cascading Deep Learning Framework for Large-Scale Microwave Circuit Design
authors: Changchun Li, Weidong Chen, Chang Chen, Xiang Zhang, Sensong An
publish: 2026 IEEE 19th Dallas Circuits and Systems Conference (DCAS)
abstract: Electromagnetic (EM) simulation of large-scale, non-periodic microwave structures poses prohibitive computational challenges. Meanwhile, conventional domain decomposition methods (DDM) often struggle to resolve complex inter-subdomain connectivity. To address these limitations, we propose a novel AI-based computational framework leveraging the Multi-port Analysis Pixel EM Simulator (MAPES). This approach enables arbitrary partition connectivity and achieves rapid performance prediction with minimal training data requirements. Furthermore, to mitigate accuracy degradation arising from physical approximations, a neural network-based residual correction module is integrated to systematically refine the predictive responses. This hybrid physics-informed approach reduces computational complexity by orders of magnitude while maintaining high fidelity, providing a scalable and efficient solution for advanced EM design.

title: ISEAE 2026 TOC
authors: N/A
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)

title: Federated Reinforcement Learning Model for Smart Grid Load Distribution
authors: Hare Krishna Mishra, Gautam Kumar, Ashish Kumar Suman, S.B.G. Tilak babu, D. Galingana, Randolph M. Balleras
publish: 2026 6th International Conference on Recent Trends in Computer Science and Technology (ICRTCST)
abstract: The current study will present a Federated ActorCritic Reinforcement Learning (FA2C) framework of smart grids to offer intelligent and privacy-responsive load allocation. The proposed solution enables different distributed energy nodes to obtain the best load management policies without necessarily sharing uncoded consumption data thus ensuring security and scalability. The implementation with the assistance of the popular simulation tool of smart grid modeling, the and gridLAB-D, to facilitate realistic experimentation is done through the framework. The grid state provided by gridLAB-D is high-resolution, and the FA2C agents can associate with the dynamic grid situations and optimize the effectiveness of distribution. The results of the experiment demonstrate that the federated design works effectively in terms of load balancing, reduction of peak demand pressure, and user privacy. Overall, the FA2C-GridLAB-D framework offers a good guideline to decentralized, intelligent and secure energy management of smart grids.

title: Early Detection of Diabetic Foot Ulcers Using DF-RESEFFICIENTNET-B0: A Hybrid Deep Learning Approach
authors: Edara Divya Sai, Venigalla Swami Ayyappa, Datla Jaidev Venkata Ayyappa Varma, Repalle Kalyan Raghavendra, Addanki Sathyavani
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: Diabetic Foot Ulcers (DFUs) represent a serious complication of diabetes, often leading to infections, amputations, and increased mortality rates if not detected early. This study proposes DF-RESEFFICIENTNET-B0, a hybrid deep learning model that integrates ResNet50 and EfficientNetB0 for accurate DFU classification. The model leverages transfer learning, data preprocessing, and augmentation techniques to improve robustness and generalization. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed model effectively distinguishes between healthy and ulcerated foot images with high accuracy. Additionally, GradCAM is employed to provide visual interpretability, enhancing clinical trust in the model. The proposed approach offers a reliable and efficient solution for early DFU detection, supporting improved patient care and decision- making.

title: AI-Based Evaluation of Survivorship Quality Metrics from Healthcare Data
authors: Gandepalli Varshita, Doddi Ashwan, Aiswarya S
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: Evaluation of cancer survivorship is one of the important steps towards improving the care of cancer survivors, and it can significantly improve the quality of life of cancer patients. In this paper, a framework for predicting cancer survivorship quality and recurrence risk using artificial intelligence is proposed, and the METABRIC dataset is used to support this. Age at diagnosis, tumour size, number of positive lymph nodes, and Nottingham Prognostic Index are among the features used to train different machine learning algorithms, namely, Logistic Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting, and Support Vector Machine. A systematic approach to preprocessing, cleaning, and normalisation of the dataset is also implemented to ensure the precise execution of the algorithms. The best-performing algorithm is then used to develop a web application based on the Flask framework to predict, estimate probabilities, and categorise cancer survivorship. In addition, another tool called analytics has been developed to visualize prediction history and survivorship trends. The performance of the proposed system shows promising results and could be advantageous for healthcare professionals.

title: A Secondary Ripple Suppression Method Based on Multilevel Active Power Decoupling
authors: Peiyu Xiong, Xiaojun Wang, Daqing Gao
publish: 2026 8th International Conference on Information Science, Electrical and Automation Engineering (ISEAE)
abstract: In high-power precision power supply applications such as particle accelerator magnet power supplies, stringent requirements are imposed on output voltage ripple coefficients better than 1×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>. This paper focuses on suppressing the low-frequency output voltage ripple (below 300Hz). Conventional linear power supplies suffer from low efficiency, passive filtering demands excessively large capacitance, and single-stage active power decoupling (APD) is limited by the ripple current rating of the decoupling capacitor, making all of them inadequate for achieving the target level of ripple suppression. To address this, a multi-stage active power decoupling topology and its control method are proposed, designed to reduce the output voltage ripple coefficient from 1.5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup> to 5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>. The proposed method employs two independent APD circuits connected in cascade: the front stage provides preliminary attenuation of the majority of the low-frequency ripple power, while the rear stage achieves deep suppression of the residual ripple. A linear active disturbance rejection control (LADRC) strategy is incorporated to enhance system robustness. Experimental results on a 400W prototype demonstrate that the proposed method reduces the output voltage ripple coefficient from 1.5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−1</sup> (without APD) to 3.5×10<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">−4</sup>, achieving a ripple suppression of −52dB (attenuation ratio of 7/3000). Under the same attenuation ratio, compared with a passive LC filter requiring 300mF of capacitance, the proposed method utilizes only 2mF of capacitance. Moreover, output stability comparable to that of a linear power supply is attained without the use of a linear pass transistor, thereby validating the effectiveness of the multi-stage APD configuration in surpassing the performance limitations of single-stage topologies.

title: Optimizing food bank logistics: A mixed integer linear programming approach for efficient humanitarian aid distribution
authors: Jairo Enrique Parra Herrera, Diego Rossit, Daniel Rossit
publish: 2025 6th International Conference on Data Analytics for Business and Industry (ICDABI)
abstract: Food security stays a global challenge, as highlighted by the UN’s Sustainable Development Goal 2: Zero Hunger. Food banks reduce waste by addressing food surplus and shortages, but their operations require efficient planning. This study aims to develop a Mixed Integer Linear Programming (MILP) model to optimize food bank logistics, minimizing costs across the supply chain. The model considers transportation costs, supply and processing capacities, demand, and product types. Food is collected from suppliers, joined into packages at plants, and distributed via centers to recipients. Implemented in Python with Google OR-Tools and evaluated on data from Bogotá, Colombia, preliminary results show possible, cost-effective solutions. Decomposition of costs reveals that supplier-to-plant transportation dominates the overall costs $(97-98 \%$ of the total), while downstream stages (plants–distribution centers and plants–clients) together account for less than 3%. Fixed technology costs remain negligible ($0.2 \%$). This underlines that system-wide efficiency hinges almost entirely on upstream transportation, and interventions should target this stage. This research highlights MILP’s potential to improve logistics, reduce costs, and improve efficiency. Future work will incorporate dynamic demand, real-time data, and larger datasets.

title: An AI-Based Framework for Decoherence Mitigation in Quantum Systems
authors: Rithik Dubey, Sakshi Takkar
publish: 2026 International Conference on Signal, Systems, and Computing for Next-Gen Automation (ICSSCNA)
abstract: The susceptibility to decoherence is significantly high in quantum computing systems, and the latter effect significantly compromises the computational reliability, particularly in Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) computers. Even though the existing mitigation strategies are currently focused on hardware-level error correction and pulselevel noise mitigation, system-level execution variables (by far, the most notable of them being waiting times caused by queues) can also contribute to the worsening of fidelity degradation, but this is hardly addressed. In its turn, this manuscript suggests an AI-based decoherence mitigation framework, in which the time spent in the queue is incorporated into fidelity prediction and scheduling. The framework elucidates the interrelations between quantum-state fidelity and waiting time through a physics-informed decoherence formulation, which is followed by the use of machine-learning to predict degradation of fidelity prior to circuits execution. Based on the estimated measures of fidelity, decoherence-aware scheduling algorithms are presented to either give priority or re-scheduling to quantum jobs that become highly disturbed by the coherence loss. The viability of integrating the AI-based predictive mechanisms into the system-level quantum computing processes is shown through simulation-based assessment, which indicates that the suggested methodology can improve the effective fidelity of the implemented quantum jobs as compared to the traditional scheduling approaches. The findings indicate that AI-supported software-level alleviation mechanisms can be used as an effective and efficient enhancement of the dependability of nearterm quantum computing systems due to their scalability and use of hardware as the basis of the error reduction approach.

title: A Physically Interpretable Hybrid Framework for Spatial Modeling of Crowd-Modified Wet-Bulb Heat Risk During Mass Gatherings
authors: Fayçal Hamdi, Mohamed Benaida, Hachemi Bennaceur, Yasser Aldwyan, Hani Almoamari, Abdulaziz Alshahrani, Abdullah Alshanqiti
abstract: Extreme heat poses a critical risk in high-density mass gatherings, where atmospheric conditions, urban morphology, and crowd dynamics interact to produce hazardous microclimates. During the Hajj pilgrimage in Makkah, these effects are amplified by dense crowds, heat-retaining surfaces, and constrained ventilation. Conventional Urban Heat Island (UHI) indicators based on land surface temperature do not directly capture human thermophysiological stress and are therefore inadequate for operational heat-risk assessment. This paper introduces a physics-guided hybrid framework for modeling crowd-modified wet-bulb heat risk. The approach combines (i) wet-bulb temperature as a physiologically grounded baseline; (ii) a data-driven spatial amplification term derived from satellite-based morphological features; (iii) scenario-based estimates of crowd-induced heat contributions; and (iv) wind-related modulation that accounts for stagnation and dust effects. By explicitly separating time-varying atmospheric forcing from spatial susceptibility, the framework provides an interpretable decomposition of heat-risk drivers. The machine-learning model for estimating the spatial amplification component achieves a mean absolute error of 1.13 °C (R2 = 0.586). Application of the framework to the 2021–2025 Hajj seasons suggests distinct site-specific heat-risk regimes, with Arafat and Muzdalifah dominated by urban amplification and Haram and Mina more strongly influenced by crowd density. Across all years, the combined influence of urban morphology, crowd density, and wind conditions results in an average increase of approximately 2.1 °C to 2.3 °C in effective wet-bulb temperature across sites. The 2024 Hajj season is identified as the most severe, with a substantial increase in extreme-risk exposure. The proposed framework provides a spatially explicit, physically interpretable, and decision-support-oriented methodology for heat-risk assessment within the Hajj context. The decomposition-based framework is, in principle, transferable to other large-scale mass-gathering environments, subject to retraining of the spatial amplification model and replacement of the event-specific crowd allocation component with context-appropriate crowd estimation approaches.

title:
DP
                    <sup>2</sup>
                    O: Dual-Pair Direct Preference Optimization for Preference-Guided Adversarial Robustness in Vision Models
authors: Ahmed Dawod Mohammed Ibrahum, Jang-Eui Hong
abstract: Adversarial training remains the most effective empirical defense against adversarial examples, yet it is hindered by the high cost of inner maximization, the clean–robust accuracy trade-off, and the underutilization of information contained in misclassified or near-boundary adversarial samples.We propose DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>O, a preference-guided framework that converts adversarial evaluations into structured preference pairs and operationalizes Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for vision robustness. Specifically, we are the first to import the implicit-reward, reference-anchored DPO formulation into the adversarial robustness setting for vision models, andwe do so through a dual-pool construction in which adversarial failures formunstable pairs (U-pairs) preferring the ground-truth label over the model’s incorrect prediction, while adversarial successes form resilient pairs (R-pairs) preferring the ground-truth label over the runner-up class. A hybrid objective then combinesDPOon these typed pairs with cross-entropy on resilient examples.Across CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, and STL-10 using ResNet-18 and a diverse attack suite (FGSM, PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8,20</sub>, PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">64,40</sub>, EOTPGD, Jitter, andAutoAttack), DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>Oconsistently improves adversarial robustness over strong baselines while maintaining competitive clean accuracy. On CIFAR-10, the hybrid model improves PGD<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">8,20</sub> robustness from 0.4853 (TRADES) to 0.5846, increases FGSM robustness to 0.6718, and achieves 0.4934 under AutoAttack while maintaining 0.9125 clean accuracy. Similar gains are observed on CIFAR-100 and STL-10, where DP<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup>O improves robustness across both iterative and adaptive attacks. These results demonstrate that reframing adversarial robustness as preference learning effectively reuses adversarial outcomes as supervision and improves the clean–robust trade-off without requiring additional critic models.

title: A Simplified Dual-Output Power Conversion Scheme Based on Efficient Utilization of Resonant Forward-Flyback Topology
authors: Ting Qian, Chen Jin
abstract: This paper proposes a simple dualoutput scheme based on efficient utilization of resonant forward-flyback topology with adjustable resonant time. Specifically, the new structure adds an extra output port for the resonant forward by properly utilizing the flyback mode of the magnetic reset winding, thereby achieving independent control for two isolated outputs in a low-cost manner. Meanwhile, considering the limited adjustment range of resonant forward and the need for a preceding cascade circuit, this scheme couples the two stages by using resonant capacitor to support the intermediate bus. This enables the resonance to dynamically match the energy transfer time of forward portion for reduced conduction losses, and thus, allows the flyback portion to reduce switching losses by simultaneously adjusting switching frequency and duty cycle. Based on detailed theoretical analysis, experimental results verify effectiveness of the proposed concept.

title: Research on an AI Enhanced Interactive Narrative Model for Digital Comics
authors: Yue Zhou, Qi Li, Tianyi Sun, Meidi Zhang
abstract: Digital comics have become an important form of human media interaction, yet most existing comic platforms still rely on static presentation and limited narrative adaptivity. To improve narrative modeling in digital comics, this study proposes an AI enhanced interactive narrative model that formulates comic understanding as a visual narrative continuation task. Given a sequence of context panels, the proposed method predicts the most coherent next panel by jointly modeling spatial semantics, temporal progression, and stylistic consistency across comic images. To this end, we develop a dual branch spatio temporal architecture combined with a structured optimization strategy to better capture ordered story evolution under diverse visual styles. Experiments conducted on the Visual Storytelling Dataset (VIST) and ComicsPAP demonstrate that the proposed model consistently outperforms conventional multimodal encoders and recent large multimodal models in Accuracy, Top 3 Accuracy, Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), and NDCG@5. In particular, the method shows strong robustness on heavily stylized comic data while maintaining favorable computational efficiency. These results indicate that the proposed model provides an effective and scalable solution for AI enhanced narrative understanding in digital comics.

title: Market-Aware Asymmetric Loss Framework for Day-Ahead Building Energy Demand Forecasting in the Electricity Market
authors: Hyeongyu Son, Musu Kim, Younggyu Hwang, Sekyung Han
abstract: In electricity markets with two-settlement structures, forecast errors carry asymmetric economic consequences: under-prediction forces costly real-time procurement at prices 15-50% above day-ahead levels during peak hours, while over-prediction incurs milder opportunity costs of 4-6% of day-ahead prices. Despite this fundamental asymmetry, most building energy forecasting models optimize symmetric metrics such as MAPE and RMSE producing statistically unbiased but economically suboptimal predictions. Moreover, the degree of cost asymmetry is not constant but varies dramatically with time of day, yet existing asymmetric approaches apply fixed penalty ratios uniformly across all hours. This paper proposes a Market-Aware Asymmetric Loss (MAL) framework that bridges this gap between forecasting training objectives and market settlement realities. The core innovation is a Time-Dependent Quantile (TD-τ) mechanism that adapts prediction conservativeness hourly based on empirically-derived cost asymmetry ratios: τ = 0.70 during on-peak hours when under-prediction is 3.5 times costlier, relaxing to τ = 0.55 during off-peak hours when costs are nearly symmetric. The framework further integrates building pattern classification for strategy selection, a seven-component SMP simulator validated against 8,760 hourly KPX observations across 12 statistical metrics (mean values within 2%, distributional properties within 8%), and comprehensive uncertainty quantification. As a proof-of-concept study, experiments are conducted on two Korean buildings with contrasting consumption profiles (a high-variability commercial office and a regular-pattern residential complex) using 3-fold time-series cross-validation. The framework’s principal contribution is a substantial reduction in under-prediction settlement cost—the economically dominant error mode: up to 50.4% on the commercial building and 41.9% on the residential building in controlled ablation against a symmetric (RMSE-only) baseline. Relative to the symmetric baseline, the full framework further yields up to 16.3% total MAPE improvement and 28.5% settlement cost reduction for the high-variability commercial building (single-split ablation), and a 3.6% aggregate MAPE reduction in cross-validation. Statistical significance of the full-framework versus symmetric-baseline comparison is confirmed by the Diebold-Mariano test (DM=−4.37, p<0.001). Against a well-tuned fixed-quantile baseline, TD-τ achieves comparable aggregate accuracy (MAPE differences under one percent in both buildings); its added value is theoretical consistency with the Granger-optimal quantile result, per-hour interpretability, and automatic adaptability to structural changes in the hourly cost-asymmetry ratio. The benefit is strongly moderated by building type: substantial on high-variability commercial buildings and marginal on stable residential patterns, providing deployment guidance for practitioners.

title: PanSR: An Object-Centric Mask Transformer for Maritime Panoptic Segmentation
authors: Lojze Žust, Matej Kristan
publish: IEEE Transactions on Intelligent Transportation Systems
abstract: Environmental panoptic perception and obstacle detection are key components in enabling autonomy for autonomous surface vehicles (ASVs). Recent studies have demonstrated that general panoptic methods, currently dominated by mask transformer approaches, are unsuitable for maritime environments, as they perform poorly in crowded scenes and in scenes with a wide range of object scales, highlighting the need for domain-specific panoptic methods. In this work, we identify several fundamental shortcomings of these recent methods and rethink architecture design choices from the perspective of perception for autonomous navigation. We introduce PanSR, a new panoptic segmentation method with the following main contributions: 1) an object-centric proposal extraction module for better handling of object size variety, 2) an improved ground-truth matching scheme consistent with proposal extraction, and 3) object-centric mask prediction for better instance separation of similar objects. With these enhancements, PanSR effectively mitigates instance merging, enhances small-object detection and increases performance in crowded scenes, delivering a notable +3.4% PQ improvement over state-of-the-art on the challenging LaRS benchmark and +8.4% PQ on WaterScenes, while retaining competitive performance on the autonomous driving perception benchmark Cityscapes. The code and models are publicly available on GitHub.

title: DR-SFL: Dynamic and Resource-Aware Split Federated Learning for Semantic-Enabled 6G Industrial IoT Systems
authors: Saifur Rahman, Jeetasha Arora, Shantanu Pal, Chandan Karmakar
abstract: As 6G networks transition toward “Native AI”, Semantic Communication (SemCom) has emerged as a vital paradigm to reduce bandwidth by transmitting high-level features rather than raw data. Split Federated Learning (SFL) is a key enabler for this transition; however, static model partitioning often fails in heterogeneous 6G-IoT environments. We propose Dynamic Resource-aware Split Federated Learning (DR-SFL), a framework that optimises the semantic-level exchange between mobile clients and the cloud by dynamically adapting split points. Experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 datasets demonstrate that DR-SFL significantly enhances convergence efficiency compared to traditional FedAvg and static SFL. Notably, in non-IID CIFAR-10 environments, DR-SFL reaches an 80% accuracy target in just 27–28 rounds, outperforming FedAvg, which requires 35 rounds. Furthermore, on the MNIST dataset, DR-SFL achieves a 98% target in as few as 3–4 rounds, compared to 6–8 rounds for FedAvg. System-level analysis reveals that the framework maintains high energy efficiency, with average battery drain as low as 0.44% for simple tasks and 6.45% for complex non-IID tasks. These results highlight DR-SFL as a robust solution for scalable, energy-efficient semantic intelligence in the evolving 6G-IoT ecosystem.

title: A System-Theoretic Approach to Hawkes Process Identification with Guaranteed Positivity and Stability
authors: Xinhui Rong, Girish N. Nair
abstract: The Hawkes process models self-exciting event streams, requiring a strictly non-negative and stable stochastic intensity. Standard identification methods enforce these properties using non-negative causal bases, yielding conservative parameter constraints and severely ill-conditioned least-squares Gram matrices at higher model orders. To overcome this, we introduce a system-theoretic identification framework utilizing the sign-indefinite orthonormal Laguerre basis, which guarantees a well-conditioned asymptotic Gram matrix independent of model order. We formulate a constrained least-squares problem enforcing the necessary and sufficient conditions for positivity and stability. By constructing the empirical Gram matrix via a Lyapunov equation and representing the constraints through a sum-of-squares trace equivalence, the proposed estimator is efficiently computed via semidefinite programming.

title: Radio Frequency Fingerprinting: A Survey of AI Generalization Challenges and Solutions for Wireless Security
authors: Nordine Quadar, Abdellah Chehri, Benoit Debaque, Halim Yanikomeroglu, Gunes Karabulut Kurt
publish: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
abstract: Radio frequency (RF) fingerprinting has emerged as a promising physical-layer security technique for device authentication in wireless networks, offering protocol-independent identification by exploiting hardware-induced signal characteristics and imperfections. This paper explores its challenges and potential within the heterogeneous wireless networks, including UAV communications and IoT deployments which are characterized by dynamic operating conditions, heterogeneous device profiles, and elevated security demands. Although deep learning-based methods have substantially improved controlled recognition accuracy from 70–90% to as high as 95–99%, the generalization gap, identified as the severe performance degradation when deployment conditions differ from training environments, remains the fundamental barrier to reliable deployment in practical environments. Systems frequently demonstrate performance degradation, ranging from 30–70%, when confronted with unseen datasets and real-world operating conditions that differ from those encountered during training. This survey systematically traces the evolution of RF fingerprinting, from traditional manual feature engineering to cutting-edge learning architectures, with a particular emphasis on generalization challenges. Key research gaps are identified in multi-factor generalization modeling, real-world validation under diverse conditions, scalable architecture design for broad device deployments, adaptive learning mechanisms for dynamic contexts, and computational efficiency for resource-constrained platforms. The analysis provides foundational insight for advancing next-generation RF fingerprinting techniques capable of sustaining robust and consistent performance across highly variable and complex wireless environments. By clarifying current limitations and future directions, this work contributes to the practical realization of secure RF-based authentication for heterogeneous wireless networks.

title: Toward Inference Consistency: Polynomial-Driven Control Synthesis of Shared Steering Systems via Interval Type-2 Fuzzy Fatigue Monitoring
authors: Zhenyang Yan, Xiang-Peng Xie
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This article is concerned about the driving performance of human-machine shared steering systems under different fatigue level of drivers. To mitigate risks arisen from driver fatigue, a novel framework featuring an interpretable driver fatigue inference mechanism coupled with a flexible gain scheduling control scheme is proposed. First, an interval type-2 fuzzy logic system (IT2 FLS) with Gaussian membership functions that adhere to interpretability constraints is employed to monitor the driver’s fatigue level. This design provides a reliable basis for dynamic authority allocation that sensitively perceives changes in fatigue level while maintaining inference consistency. Second, a polynomial-driven switching gain-scheduling (PDSGS) scheme is constructed for path-tracking assistance in the human-machine shared steering system. Driven by the homogeneous polynomials technique, the PDSGS scheme can achieve more flexible steering assistance responses with the integration of dynamic gain matching mechanism. Indeed, by systematically classifying the membership distribution into distinct subsets, gain matrices are switched in real time to improve shared steering performance. Finally, the effectiveness of the proposed method is verified by the driver-in-the-loop simulation platform.

title: Adaptive Sliding Mode Path Following Control for Autonomous Ground Vehicles with Uncertain Mass and Cornering Stiffness
authors: Hongliang Li, Heng Wang, Gang Liu, Qing Li
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: This paper presents a novel integrated estimation-control framework to solve the path following problem for autonomous ground vehicles under coupled uncertainties in mass and cornering stiffness. The proposed method is characterized by the synergistic integration of three key innovations: 1) a fuzzy confidence-enhanced recursive least-squares algorithm (FC-RLS-MFF) that employs a rule-based mechanism to dynamically assess excitation sufficiency, ensuring robust online mass estimation; 2) a tightly-coupled identification scheme, where the estimated mass actively refines cornering stiffness in real time through tire vertical load updates, departing from the use of fixed nominal values; and 3) an adaptive fast nonsingular terminal sliding mode controller (AFNTSMC) that leverages this real-time parameter stream to enable model-based compensation, rather than mere disturbance rejection. Co-simulations, including ablation studies, validate the framework and demonstrate that this tight integration yields emergent performance gains, such as a significant reduction in path-tracking error.

title: A Novel Fractional-Order Second-Order Sliding Mode Control For Nonlinear Systems Under Matched and Mismatched Uncertainties
authors: Weiqi Zhang, Chuanyu Sun, Yanmin Wang, Hany M. Hasanien, Kai Song
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: To overcome traditional limitations in handling complex disturbances, a novel fractional-order second-order sliding mode control (FOSOSMC) is developed for a class of nonlinear systems in the presence of matched and mismatched uncertainties, ensuring the properties of stability and dynamic performance. The main novelties are highlighted as follows. Firstly, a structurally flexible continuous SOSMC manifold incorporating FO calculus is constructed by taking into account the design of a family of sliding variables, which ensures that the state trajectory can converge to the equilibrium point accurately in a finite time and address the chattering problem owing to its memory and hereditary properties, while uniformly compensating for mismatched uncertainties. Furthermore, the straightforward sliding manifold structure allows for diverse designs of control laws. For simple single-input single-output systems, their fast convergence and high precision under lumped uncertainties can be guaranteed by combining the FOSOSM manifold and a fast-terminal sliding mode-type reaching law. For complex multi-input multi-output systems, a state-dependent adaptive gain is fitted to the FOSOSM manifold to obtain stronger robustness without sacrificing control accuracy or complicating parameter tuning. The global finite-time stability of the closed-loop dynamics is demonstrated by means of Lyapunov functions. Finally, simulation and experimental results verify the excellent control performance of the proposed method for nonlinear systems with uncertainties.

title: Soft-Masked Transformer for Point Cloud Processing With Skip Attention-Based Upsampling
authors: Yong He, Hongshan Yu, Chaoxu Mu, Mingtao Feng, Tongjia Chen, Zechuan Li, Anwaar Ulhaq, Ajmal Mian
publish: IEEE Transactions on Automation Science and Engineering
abstract: Point cloud processing methods leverage local and global point features to cater to downstream tasks, yet they often overlook the task-level context inherent in point clouds during the encoding stage. We argue that integrating task-level information into the encoding stage significantly enhances performance. To that end, we propose SMTransformer which incorporates task-level information into a vector-based transformer by utilizing a soft mask generated from task-level queries and keys to learn the attention weights. Additionally, to facilitate effective communication between features from the encoding and decoding layers in high-level tasks such as segmentation, we introduce a skip-attention-based up-sampling block. This block dynamically fuses features from various resolution points across the encoding and decoding layers. To mitigate the increase in network parameters and training time resulting from the complexity of the aforementioned blocks, we propose a novel shared point position encoding strategy. This strategy allows various transformer blocks to share the same position information over the same resolution points, thereby reducing network parameters and training time without compromising accuracy. Experimental comparisons with existing methods on multiple datasets demonstrate the efficacy of SMTransformer and skip-attention-based up-sampling for semantic segmentation task. In particular, we achieve state-of-the-art semantic segmentation results of 73.9% mIoU on S3DIS Area 5 and 62.4% mIoU on SWAN dataset. Note to Practitioners—Point cloud processing underpins automation tasks such as robotic perception, navigation, and inspection, where accurate 3D understanding is essential. Existing methods often prioritize vision benchmarks while overlooking automation needs like efficiency on limited hardware and robustness in real-world environments. The proposed SMTransformer embeds task-level guidance into feature learning and employs skip-attention up-sampling to improve segmentation accuracy with practical efficiency. It is well-suited for robotic manipulation, autonomous driving, and inspection applications. Current limitations include reliance on GPUs and sensitivity to extreme density variations. Future work will target edge-device deployment and multi-task extensions.
